With variations of up to 10 per minute at 5 GHz, J1819+3845 exhibits the most dramatic radio variability observed in an extragalactic source. If such variations were intrinsic then the source, at z=0.54 , would have an angular size of order 10 nano-arcseconds and would therefore have to scintillate, such that ISS would in any case dominate the observed variability. Unlike PKS 0405 385, J1819+3845 does not exhibit outbursts of variability but rather has continued to show its characteristic rapid variability since discovery. The rapidity of the variability in J1819+3845 was immediately explained as being due to an unusually nearby scattering screen, within a few tens of pc from the Sun dtdb2000 .

sources.summary: The paper models J1819 + 3845 as a persistent rapid scintillator whose time-delay and annual-cycle data favor a one-dimensional, extremely anisotropic scattering pattern and predict repeating flux variations.

quotes.quote: For the persistent rapid scintillators J1819 + 3845 and PKS1257 326 we show that this model offers a good description of the two-station time-delay measurements and the annual cycle in the scintillation time-scale. Generalising the model to finite anisotropy yields a better match to the data but the improvement is not significant and the two additional parameters which are required to describe this model are not justified by the existing data. The extreme anisotropy we infer for the scintillation patterns must be attributed to the scattering medium rather than a highly elongated source.

In the case of J1819 + 3845 a strictly one-dimensional model predicts that the particular scintillations seen between late August and mid November each year should also be seen before and after this period as the Earth's velocity across the flux pattern changes sign at these times and the observer moves back and forth across the same part of the pattern. If repeating flux patterns can be identified in the data they will permit precise determination of the major-axis orientation and the minor-axis velocity component.

sources.summary: The paper uses J1819+385 as a comparison extreme IDV quasar, noting peaked modulation, time-delay evidence, annual variability changes, and a ~35 km/s scintillation velocity.

quotes.quote: In the two extreme IDV sources (PKS 0405-385 and J1819+385) the modulation index (rms/mean flux density) was found to peak at tens of percent at a frequency near 5 GHz (KCJ and DTB).

Conclusive evidence for the ISS interpretation of IDV comes from the observations of the time delay in the variability pattern observed in PKS 0405-385 between the ATCA and VLA (Jauncey et al., 2000) and between Westerbork and the VLA for J1819+385 (Dennett-Thorpe and de Bruyn, 2002). Further, annual changes of the time scale of variability due to orbital motion of the Earth have been observed for sources J1819+385 (Dennett-Thorpe 2000) and B0917+624 (Rickett et al. \ 2001; Jauncey and Macquart, 2001).

A velocity of about 35 km s ^ 1 relative to the LSR was obtained for the scintillations of quasar J1819+385 both from and intercontinental time delay measurement and from the annual modulation in that source (Dennett-Thorpe and de Bruyn, 2002a,b).

gmw_00005276.name: SDSS J150813.02+484710.6
gmw_00005276.overview: SDSS J150813.02+484710.6 is an extreme quasar with a line profile that resembles typical A3 and A4 sources [arXiv:1210.2059]. The galaxy exhibits a large displacement of its line core, which implies that the line-emitting gas is predominantly in outflow [arXiv:1210.2059].

sources.summary: The review singles out SDSS J150813.02+484710.6 as an extreme source whose line profile resembles A3/A4 quasars and whose core displacement implies predominantly outflowing gas.

quotes.quote: The \ profile of SDSS J150813.02+484710.6 resembles a typical \ profile for A3 and A4 sources. (Fig. fig:sdss ). Apart from this extreme SDSS source we note that \ is different from both \ and . In \ we see a displacement of the core of the line. The shift normalized by line width is large, with the implication that the line emitting gas is predominantly in outflow (see Fig. fig:mgii ).

gmw_00005277.name: 3C 220.3
gmw_00005277.overview: The source 3C 220.3 is a radio galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.685 [arXiv:1406.2872] that exhibits a double-lobed structure of 8 arcsec extent [arXiv:1809.06661]. It acts as a strong gravitational lens for a background submillimeter galaxy at z=2.221 [arXiv:1406.2872].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3C 220.3 as a z=0.685 powerful radio galaxy that strongly lenses a background SMG; lens modeling constrains the host system's dark matter content.

quotes.quote: \ photometry and extensive multiwavelength followup have revealed that the powerful radio galaxy 3C 220.3 at z=0.685 acts as a gravitational lens for a background submillimeter galaxy (SMG) at z=2.221 . At an observed wavelength of 1 mm, the SMG is lensed into three distinct images. In the observed near infrared, these images are connected by an arc of 1 8 radius forming an Einstein half-ring centered near the radio galaxy. In visible light, only the arc is apparent. 3C 220.3 is the only known instance of strong galaxy-scale lensing by a powerful radio galaxy not located in a galaxy cluster and therefore it offers the potential to probe the dark matter content of the radio galaxy host.

Lens modeling rejects a single lens, but two lenses centered on the radio galaxy host A and a companion B, separated by 1 5, provide a fit consistent with all data and reveal faint candidates for the predicted fourth and fifth images. The model does not require an extended common dark matter halo, consistent with the absence of extended bright X-ray emission on our Chandra image. The projected dark matter fractions within the Einstein radii of A (1 02) and B (0 61) are about 0.4 0.3 and 0.55 0.3. The mass to i -band light ratios of A and B, M/L_ i 8 4 M_ /L_ , appear comparable to those of radio-quiet lensing galaxies at the same redshift in the CASTLES, LSD, and SL2S samples.

sources.summary: The paper uses 3C220.3 as a bright, double-lobed radio galaxy at the LOFAR-LBA field center, modeling it as an unresolved 38 Jy point-source calibrator with spectral index -0.8.

quotes.quote: The 3C220 field consists of a reasonably bright source located at the phase centre (the 3C220.3 radio galaxy with a flux of 38 Jy at 74 MHz cohen2007 ) which can be utilised as a bandpass calibrator, making calibration of the 3C220 field fairly straightforward.

The 3C220.3 radio galaxy is a double-lobed source of 8 arcsec extent, but it is unresolved with the LOFAR-LBA array which has a maximum resolution of 15 arcsec. Therefore, we use a single point source representing 3C220.3 with 38 Jy flux at 74 MHz cohen2007 and a spectral index of -0.8 as a starting model for DI calibration.

gmw_00005278.name: 2MASX J16273931+3002239
gmw_00005278.overview: 2MASX J16273931+3002239 is a superluminous spiral galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.2599 [arXiv:2201.12365]. It serves as the brightest cluster galaxy of its host galaxy cluster, positioned just 9.6 kpc from the cluster's X-ray center [arXiv:2201.12365].

sources.summary: The paper establishes 2MASX J16273931+3002239, later called J16273, as a superluminous spiral BCG: it lies only 9.6 kpc from the X-ray cluster center and outshines nearby galaxies.

quotes.quote: We detected massive galaxy clusters around five superluminous disk galaxies and established that one superluminous spiral, 2MASX J16273931+3002239, is the central BCG of a galaxy cluster.
For one galaxy, J16273, the projected distance from the cluster center is only 2.4 , which corresponds to 9.6 kpc at the redshift of the galaxy ( z=0.2599 ).
By deriving the band luminosity of the galaxies, we found that the neighboring galaxies are factor of 4-630 less luminous than J16273. This implies that J16273 is the BCG of this galaxy cluster.

gmw_00005279.name: NGC 3628-UCD1
gmw_00005279.overview: NGC 3628-UCD1 is an ultracompact dwarf galaxy that is currently forming through tidal stripping within a stellar stream [arXiv:1511.03264]. It has a moderate metallicity, an intermediate age, and a near-solar alpha-element abundance that distinguish it from typical globular clusters [arXiv:1511.03264].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 3628-UCD1 as a stripped UCD caught in formation, with consistent spectra, moderate metallicity, and intermediate-age, near-solar-alpha populations unlike ordinary globular clusters.

quotes.quote: NGC 3628-UCD1 Jennings15 described NGC 3628-UCD1 ( a in Fig. fig:massrad_stellpop ) as an Cen-like object caught in formation via tidal stripping, as it is found within a stellar stream of material of a progenitor object that has been torn apart by NGC 3628. This object is one of those that were observed twice. Both the ESI and MODS spectra result in very consistent age, metallicity, and [ /Fe ] . The metallicity ( [ Z/ H ] =-0.77 dex) is moderate in comparison to other CSSs, and brings NGC 3628-UCD1 closer to the galaxy mass--metallicity relation. Compared to GCs, the intermediate age and (sub-) solar [ /Fe ] set the object apart, consistent with the formation via stripping instead of a GC-type object.

gmw_00005280.name: NGC 4546-UCD1
gmw_00005280.overview: NGC 4546-UCD1 is an ultracompact dwarf galaxy that exhibits an extended star formation history [arXiv:2002.02765, arXiv:1511.03264]. It is likely the remnant nucleus of a dwarf galaxy that was tidally disrupted and stripped during past interactions with NGC 4546 [arXiv:2002.02765, arXiv:1511.03264].

sources.summary: NGC 4546-UCD1 is described as a young UCD and likely remnant nucleus of a tidally disrupted dwarf, with extended star formation, supersolar metallicity, and past interaction with NGC 4546.

quotes.quote: In addition, norris11 and norris15 studied the stellar population of the ultra-compact dwarf (UCD) NGC 4546-UCD1 linked to the NGC 4546 system. These authors found that NGC 4546-UCD1 displays an extended star formation history with a light-weighted age of 4 Gyr, suggesting that it is the remnant nucleus of a dwarf galaxy that was tidally disrupted by NGC 4546.

If this intermediate colour GC group is really different from the classic subpopulations, a possible scenario about its origin could involve the young NGC 4546-UCD1 mentioned above. norris15 assume that this object is the final result of tidal interaction between NGC 4546 and a dwarf galaxy of mass 3 10^ 9 M _ . NGC 4546-UCD1, with a metallicity [ Z/H ] =0.18 dex and an extended star formation history from early epochs has previously interacted with the galaxy in the last Gyrs, beginning its current state between 1-3 Gyr ago. These previous encounters ( >3 Gyr) may have formed new GCs with a different metallicity compared with the typical blue and red ones, which we observed in our analysis.

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC 4546-UCD1 as a recently stripped dwarf-galaxy nucleus with prolonged star formation, relatively young and metal-rich populations, and near-solar alpha abundance.

quotes.quote: NGC 4546-UCD1 Using full spectral fitting of a high S/N spectrum of NGC 4546-UCD1 ( b in Fig. fig:massrad_stellpop ) using the same spectrum as examined here Norris15 found that this object was actively forming stars from early epochs until quite recently. This prolonged star formation is unexpected for a star cluster and, taken together with the observation that this object counterrotates its host galaxy, indicates that the object was once the nucleus of a dwarf galaxy that was stripped by NGC 4546 relatively recently. Despite using the same spectrum and similar stellar population models, the luminosity weighted age and metallicities we derive here for NGC 4546-UCD1 are slightly inconsistent with those of Norris15 ( Norris15 ; age of 5.8 0.1 vs 4.0^ +0.93 _ 0.75 Gyr, [ Z/ H ] =0.05 0.01 vs 0.18 0.06 dex), difficulty of achieving consistent stellar population parameters when even slightly different analysis codes or models are used. Nevertheless our measurements confirm that NGC 4546-UCD1 is relatively young and metal rich, at least compared to the vast majority of GCs, and furthermore has near solar [ /Fe ] . This last fact is expected given the observation that NGC 4546-UCD1 had an extended SFH Norris15 .

gmw_00005281.name: VCC 165cE
gmw_00005281.overview: VCC 165cE is a compact elliptical galaxy in formation [arXiv:1511.03264]. It is connected to the galaxy VCC 165 by a visible tidal stream and hosts a young stellar population with near-solar metallicity [arXiv:1511.03264].

sources.summary: The paper reinterprets VCC 165cE as a compact elliptical in formation, connected to VCC 165 by a stream and having young, near-solar-metallicity stellar populations.

quotes.quote: VCC 165cE Paudel13 described a compact source close to VCC 165 as a background AGN. However, the current SDSS spectrum SDSSDR10 reveals that VCC 165 has a recession velocity exceeding 12000 km s ^ 1 and close to that of the compact object in its vicinity. While the old velocity measurement of 255 km s ^ 1 (NED) placed VCC 165 in the Virgo cluster, like the NGC 4216 system analysed by Paudel13 , the new value puts it behind the Virgo cluster together with the compact object. The stream connecting the two objects is clearly visible in the NGVS image of Paudel13 and likely belongs to this system in the background. Therefore, the compact object should be considered as a cE in formation, and we called it VCC 165cE.

The stellar population parameters with a young age ( 1.5^ +0.4 _ 0.3 Gyr), metallicity around solar ( [ Z/ H ] =-0.13 0.16 ), and [ / Fe ] =0.20 0.12 fit very well to this scenario. cE1 and cE2 Huxor11b discovered two cEs embedded in tidal streams.

gmw_00005282.name: cE0
gmw_00005282.overview: The galaxy cE0 is classified as an isolated compact elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1511.03264]. It is characterized by a stellar content with approximately solar metallicity [arXiv:1511.03264].

sources.summary: The paper treats cE0 as an isolated compact elliptical with roughly solar metallicity, tension between MODS and SDSS age estimates, and a weighted adopted value.

quotes.quote: Isolated cEs Interestingly, one of the two isolated CSSs CGCG 036-042; Paudel14 has a low metallicity compared to the bulk of our CSSs, which places it within the scatter of the galaxy mass metallicity relation (albeit as an extreme case). The other isolated cE in our sample cE0; Huxor13 , has around solar metallicity. While the results for metallicity and [ /Fe ] are consistent within the uncertainties, there is some tension for the age, with the age derived from the MODS spectrum being younger than the Huxor13 value based on the SDSS spectrum, which has a slightly higher S/N of 18--27. We reanalysed the SDSS spectrum and find an age consistent with that of Huxor13 . The value we use throughout the paper is the weighted average, consistently with other objects that have multiple spectra.

gmw_00005283.name: J2000.9 1749
gmw_00005283.overview: J2000.9 1749 is a flat-spectrum radio quasar [arXiv:1807.02085]. Its broadband spectral energy distribution has been modeled using both leptonic and lepto-hadronic emission scenarios [arXiv:1807.02085].

sources.summary: The paper derives disk and black-hole parameters for J2000.9 1749 from Mg II information, models its time-averaged FSRQ SED, and notes its catalog spectra do not join smoothly.

quotes.quote: Moreover, as can be seen in Figure fig_gamma_sed , ray spectra from the three catalogs join smoothly (except for J2000.9 1749), thus supporting our assumption.
For J2000.9 1749, Mg ii line information are available in 1984ApJ...277...64O which we use to derive M_ BH and L_ disk .

We generate steady-state, i.e. time-averaged, broadband SEDs of all 8 FSRQs following the details outlined in Section sec3 and reproduce them using both leptonic and lepto-hadronic emission scenarios. The leptonic model SEDs are presented in Figure fig_leptonic_sed and the associated SED parameters are given in Table tab:leptonic_sed_param . The results of the lepto-hadronic SED modeling are shown in Figure fig_hadronic_sed and we provide the physical parameters derived from the modeling in Table tab:hadronic_sed_param . Using the results of the SED modeling, we compute the degree of X-ray and ray polarization predicted by the leptonic and hadronic models.

gmw_00005284.name: HSCJ023322 020530
gmw_00005284.overview: HSCJ023322 020530 is a strong gravitational lens system featuring a very bright, doubly lensed source that is potentially an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2207.10124]. The system also contains a second, fainter source that is lensed into extended arcs [arXiv:2207.10124].

sources.summary: The paper discusses HSCJ023322 020530 as a difficult lens system with a compact, doubly lensed AGN-like source, an additional arc source, and persistent model residuals.

quotes.quote: HSCJ023322 020530 : This lens system has one very bright source, potentially an active galactic nucleus, which is doubly lensed. Since those two lensed images are extremely compact, this configuration is very hard to model with , which is optimal for modeling extended sources but not point-like objects. Additionally, it seems that the PSF is not perfectly symmetric, leading to significant residuals of the point-like images. Since there is another fainter source lensed into extended arcs, we manually included here a second source at the same redshift.

However, even with this second source included, visible residuals remain, resulting in a relatively high ^2_ red of 1.87. We tried several different options such as including additional profiles or relaxing assumptions on the structural parameters but obtained no notable improvement that would justify the increase in model complexity.

gmw_00005285.name: HSCJ090429 010228
aliases_external: HSC J0904--0102 lens system, component G
gmw_00005285.overview: HSCJ090429 010228 is a quadruply lensed Lyman-break galaxy located at a redshift of z = 3.403 [arXiv:1909.00120]. The source is unusually compact [arXiv:1909.00120, arXiv:2207.10124] and is gravitationally lensed by a foreground early-type galaxy at z = 0.957 [arXiv:1909.00120].

sources.summary: The paper reports HSC J0904--0102 as a quadruply lensed LBG at z=3.403, modeled by a z=0.957 foreground galaxy; the source is magnified, unusually compact, and less luminous than typical LBGs.

quotes.quote: We report the serendipitous discovery of HSC J0904--0102, a quadruply-lensed Lyman break galaxy (LBG) in the Survey of Gravitationally-lensed Objects in Hyper Suprime-Cam Imaging (SuGOHI). Owing to its point-like appearance, the source was thought to be a lensed active galactic nucleus. We obtained follow-up spectroscopic data with the Gemini Multi-Object Spectrographs on the Gemini South Telescope, which confirmed this to be a lens system.

The deflecting foreground galaxy is a typical early-type galaxy at a high redshift of z_ = 0.957 with stellar velocity dispersion _v=259 56 km s ^ 1 . The lensed source is identified as an LBG at z_ s = 3.403 , based on the sharp drop bluewards of Ly and other absorption features. A simple lens mass model for the system, assuming a singular isothermal ellipsoid, yields an Einstein radius of _ Ein = 1. 23 and a total mass within the Einstein radius of M_ Ein = (5.55 0.24) 10^ 11 M_ corresponding to a velocity dispersion of _ SIE = 283 3 km s ^ 1 , which is in good agreement with the value derived spectroscopically. The most isolated lensed LBG image has a magnification of 6.5 . In comparison with other lensed LBGs and typical z 4 LBG populations, HSC J0904--0102 is unusually compact, an outlier at >2 confidence.

sources.summary: The paper identifies HSCJ090429 010228 as a lens system whose source is a compact Lyman-alpha break galaxy and reports modest residuals with a good reduced chi-square.

quotes.quote: HSCJ090429 010228 : The lens system HSCJ090429 010228 appears also like a point-source such as an active galactic nucleus. However, based on jaelani20b , it is a compact Lyman alpha break galaxy. As mentioned already above, works best for extended sources such that residuals are expected. Interestingly, we find only moderate residuals in the i band but nearly no residuals in the other filters and obtain a good ^2_ red of 1.22.

gmw_00005286.name: SQ B
gmw_00005286.overview: SQ B is a tidal dwarf galaxy candidate located within the tidal tail of NGC 7319 [arXiv:1202.2862]. The region is rich in carbon monoxide, ultraviolet, and mid-infrared emission, exhibiting a high star-formation rate and solar-metallicity gas [arXiv:astro-ph/0407473, arXiv:1202.2862].

sources.summary: The paper describes SQ B as a tidal-dwarf-galaxy candidate in Stephan's Quintet's tidal tail, with CO, ionized gas, and H I kinematically aligned, solar-metallicity gas, high extinction, and a high star-formation rate.

quotes.quote: We find two main areas of CO emission: (i) an elongated region towards the area known as SQ B where a Tidal Dwarf Galaxy could currently be forming (ii) a barely resolved area at the tip of the optical tidal arm. Both regions follow dust lanes visible on HST images and their CO peak coincides spatially exactly with the maximum of the \ line emission. In SQ B, there is furthermore very good kinematical agreement between the CO, \ and HI components.

Furthermore, the relatively high oxygen abundances (about solar) estimated from the optical spectra of the \ regions imply that the gas feeding the star formation originated from the inner regions of the parent galaxy. In SQ B, we derive from different tracers a star formation rate, corrected for dust extinction - which is important in the area - of 0.5 /yr, i.e. one of the highest values so far measured outside galaxies. The inferred molecular gas consumption time of 0.5 Gyr lies in the range of values found for spiral and starburst galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper describes SQ-B as a CO-rich, UV and mid-infrared-bright tidal dwarf galaxy candidate formed from material in NGC 7319's tidal tail.

quotes.quote: Another interesting feature of SQ is the presence of a CO-rich 7 10^8 M _ Braine2001, Lisenfeld2002, Lisenfeld2004 region, SQ-B, located within the young H i tidal tail see Sulentic2001 , to the south of NGC 7319 (outside the field of view of Figure fig:CO_beams_on_HST ). This region is also bright in the mid-IR Xu1999, Guillard2010 , H Arp1973a and UV Xu2005 . The H_2 to-H i gas mass ratio of 0.5 indicates that SQ-B is a tidal dwarf galaxy (TDG) candidate, i.e. a small galaxy which is in the process of formation from the material of the NGC 7319's tidal tail Boquien2009 . The gas metallicity in SQ-A and SQ-B is slightly higher than solar Xu2003, Lisenfeld2004 .

gmw_00005287.name: PG 0946+301
gmw_00005287.overview: PG 0946+301 is a broad-absorption line quasar [arXiv:1408.3633, arXiv:1408.4941]. The object is strongly X-ray weak [arXiv:1408.3633], and its absorption features can be modeled as a dustless radiation pressure confined slab [arXiv:1408.4941].

sources.summary: The paper includes PG 0946 in its NuSTAR BAL-quasar sample, finding the targets strongly X-ray weak and this object detected in at least one band.

quotes.quote: The _ OX constraints for the targets are shown in Figure 2, which indicate that their rest-frame 2  keV luminosities are 14 to >330 times weaker than expected from the Steffen2006 _ OX - L_ 2500  relation. These _ OX values have not been corrected for any intrinsic optical/UV reddening, which would render the values even more negative. Note that the spread of the Steffen2006 data points constrains the expected amount of quasar variability e.g., Gibson2012 , and after accounting for the measured flux variability of our targets (see Section 3 below), they are still significantly X-ray weak.

PG 0946, PG 1001, and PG 1254 are detected in at least one band in each FPM. The minimum positional offsets (Table  tbl-obs ) between the optical and 3--24 keV positions are within expectations for faint sources. The chance of getting any spurious detections by wavdetect at these known source positions is negligible.

sources.summary: The paper uses PG 0946+301 as a BAL-quasar comparison case: its ionic and hydrogen columns mostly match a dustless RPC absorber, except P V requires enhanced phosphorus in uniform-density models.

quotes.quote: For PG 0946+301, arav_etal01b derived 15.4< ( H ^0)<17 . The reported lower limits on for C ^ 3+ , N ^ 4+ , O ^ 5+ , P ^ 4+ , Si ^ 3+ and S ^ 5+ are 16.1, 16.2, 16.6, 15.0, 15.1 and 15.8, respectively ( <14.7 for C ^+ ). A dustless RPC slab with 3.8 10^ 22 \ (Fig. fig:NOdust_Nion_Sigma ) has \ values that are consistent with the measured lower limits for all ions, except P ^ 4+ , where the predicted maximal log column is <14.5 (Fig. fig:NOdust_Nion_Z_and_alpha ). In order to reproduce the observed P ^ 4+ column by a uniform-density photoionization model, arav_etal01b require an enhanced P abundance by a factor of 10 relative to other metals compared to the solar abundance.

gmw_00005288.name: SDSS J0838+2955
gmw_00005288.overview: SDSS J0838+2955 is a broad-absorption line quasar [arXiv:1408.4941]. Observations of this active galactic nucleus reveal ionic columns of carbon, magnesium, aluminum, and silicon [arXiv:1408.4941].

sources.summary: The paper compares SDSS J0838+2955 to RPC absorber predictions, reporting ionic columns and noting a mismatch where dustless models overpredict C IV for the observed Si IV column.

quotes.quote: For SDSS J0838+2955, moe_etal09 use the non-saturated \ and \ doublets to evaluate the absorber CF, and report of 14.9, 16.4, 13.9, 14.6 and 16.1 for C ^+ , C ^ 3+ , Mg ^+ , Al ^ ++ and Si ^ 3+ , respectively. For log (Si ^ 3+ ) 16 , all dustless RPC models predict log (C ^ 3+ ) 17.5 (Fig. fig:NOdust_all_Nion_Sigma ) which is 1 dex larger than the measured C ^ 3+ column. This discrepancy may be caused by deviations of the observed absorber from hydrostatic equilibrium assumed by the RPC models. A more detailed fit to observations is beyond the scope of this paper.

gmw_00005289.name: Centaurus I
gmw_00005289.overview: Centaurus I is an old, metal-poor ultra-faint dwarf galaxy associated with the Milky Way [arXiv:1912.03301, arXiv:2308.08602]. It is a dark-matter-dominated system containing horizontal branch and RR Lyrae stars, with no strong evidence of tidal stripping [arXiv:2308.08602, arXiv:2312.07279].

sources.summary: The paper confirms Cen I as a dark-matter-dominated ultra-faint dwarf galaxy, measuring 34 members, systemic velocity, velocity and metallicity dispersions, systemic proper motion, orbit, J-factor, and no strong tidal-stripping evidence.

quotes.quote: We present a spectroscopic analysis of Eridanus IV (Eri IV) and Centaurus I (Cen I), two ultra-faint dwarf galaxies of the Milky Way. Using IMACS/Magellan spectroscopy, we identify 28 member stars of Eri IV and 34 member stars of Cen I.

For Cen I, we find a velocity v_ sys = 44.9 0.8 km s^ 1 and velocity dispersion _ v = 4.2^ +0.6 _ 0.5 km s^ 1 . We measure the metallicities of 27 member stars of Cen I, and find a mean metallicity [ Fe/H ] = -2.57 0.08 and metallicity dispersion _ [ Fe/H ] = 0.38^ +0.07 _ 0.05 . We calculate the systemic proper motion, orbit, and the astrophysical J-factor for each system, the latter of which indicates that Eri IV is a good target for indirect dark matter detection.

We also find no strong evidence for tidal stripping of Cen I or Eri IV. Overall, our measurements confirm that Eri IV and Cen I are dark matter-dominated galaxies with properties largely consistent with other known ultra-faint dwarf galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper presents Centaurus I, alias DELVE J1238 4054/Cen I, as an old, extended, metal-poor ultra-faint dwarf-galaxy candidate with luminosity and size consistent with ultra-faint galaxies.

quotes.quote: The first of these systems, DELVE J1238 4054, has a physical size and luminosity consistent with the locus of ultra-faint galaxies ( properties , satellites ), and we tentatively denote it Centaurus I (Cen I).

Centaurus I We found that is an old ( >12.85 ), extended ( r_ 1/2 =79^ +14 _ 10 ), and faint ( M_V=-5.55^ +0.11 _ 0.11 ) stellar system with an average systemic metallicity ( = -1.8 dex) consistent with that of most ultra-faint galaxies 2019 edition McConnachie:2012 . Given its physical size, is relatively bright compared to the population of ultra-faint galaxies with similar size, but its absolute magnitude and physical size are consistent with the definition for ultra-faint galaxies put forth in Simon:2019 ; i.e., a dwarf galaxy with M_V 7.7 . The well-populated horizontal branch of makes it an excellent candidate for RR Lyrae star searches.

sources.summary: The paper notes a distant blue horizontal branch star and RRL in Centaurus I, discusses Gaia-limit non-recovery, and includes it among clean extended-population UFDs.

quotes.quote: Another example is Centaurus I, in which report a blue horizontal branch star at a distance > 9 R_h and a RRL at 6 R_h .

In the case of Centaurus I, the expected magnitude of the RRL is 20.80 mag, within the Gaia's limits but approaching its limiting magnitude. Centaurus I is known to have RRL: report three of them based on their own multi-epoch giz DECam observations of this UFD.
The galaxies in the clean sample with RRL at S_ ell 4 R_h are Tucana II, Tucana III, Grus II, Sagittarius II, Pegasus IV, Hercules, Ursa Major I, Centaurus I, Leo IV, and Eridanus II.

gmw_00005290.name: 3C 136.1
gmw_00005290.overview: 3C 136.1 is a radio galaxy that exhibits two bi-conical emission structures in its central region [arXiv:1811.04946]. This bipolar emission-line structure is oriented transverse to the radio jet, representing the largest radio/optical axis discrepancy in its sample [arXiv:1811.04946].

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 136.1 as a radio galaxy with central bi-conical emission, dust-affected line ratios, and the largest radio/optical axis discrepancy in the sample.

quotes.quote: Interesting are the cases of 3C 136.1 and 3C 196.1, which show two bi-conical structures of emission in the central region.

We find that such components are evident in three of the galaxies in our sample, 3C 33, 3C 136.1 and 3C 285. To further investigate the effects of dust on our emission line maps, we used the H and H flux values from B09+ to calculate the H /H ratio, which is sensitive to reddening. Assuming Case B recombination theory, this line ratio is 3 if reddening is present. We found line ratios significantly higher than this value for three of the galaxies in our sample (3C 136.1, 3C 285 and 3C 319) due to the dimmed H . For 3C 319 the spectrum is intrinsically weak and H and [ O III ] are not detected, probably due not to absorption. Since dust attenuation is more important at shorter wavelengths, the [ O III ] /H line ratios presented in that figure for 3C 33, 3C 136.1, and 3C 285 can be considered as lower limits.
The source which shows the largest radio/optical axis discrepancy is 3C 136.1 which shows a bipolar emission-line structure, traversal to the jet.

gmw_00005291.name: 2FGL J1958.4 3012
gmw_00005291.overview: The active galactic nucleus 2FGL J1958.4 3012 is associated with a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1211.6344, arXiv:2012.05176] that may be a brightest cluster galaxy [arXiv:1211.6344]. Its host is an elliptical galaxy [arXiv:2012.05176] at a redshift of z = 0.119 [arXiv:2012.05176] that exhibits weak nitrogen II emission [arXiv:2012.05176].

sources.summary: The paper confirms z=0.119 with a high-S/N spectrum combining a power law and elliptical-galaxy emission, including weak NII emission.

quotes.quote: Two independent publications give the same value for its redshift, z = 0.119. This value is reported in the DR3 6dF catalog Jon04,Jon09 and in the Reflex Cluster survey Guz09 . The public 6dF spectrum has a very low signal to noise (see Fig. fig_newplots ) while the Reflex spectrum is not available. We thus took a 30 min spectrum with EFOSC2 in order to confirm or disprove this result.

The resulting high signal to noise spectrum is a combination of a power law with the emission of an elliptical galaxy modelled with a 11 Gyrs template Bru03 at z =0.119. Weak [ NII ] emission is also detected (see Fig. fig_spec2 , fourth row, left). The rest-frame power law/galaxy ratio at 5500 is 1.2 0.2 and the galaxy has absolute magnitude M _ R =-22.1 0.2. This is thus a firm redshift value.

sources.summary: The paper links 2FGL J1958.4 3012 to the BL Lac object RXC J1958.2-3011, possibly a BCG, and finds its longer LAT detection remains spectrally consistent.

quotes.quote: The source 2FGL J1958.4 3012 is associated with the BL Lac object RXC J1958.2-3011, and may be a BCG (see sec:sample ). It is listed with a 24-month detection significance of 5.1, 100 MeV E 100 GeV flux of (5.94 1.78) 10^ 10 ph cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 and photon index of 1.9 0.2. With 21 months more data (and in an energy band 300 MeV E 300 GeV ) we calculate an increased detection significance of 6.8 , and a consistent (within errors) photon index, as expected.

gmw_00005292.name: PKS 1346 112
gmw_00005292.overview: PKS 1346 112 is a flat-spectrum quasi-stellar object located at a redshift of z=0.34 [arXiv:1211.6344]. The source is identified as a possible blazar counterpart to a transient GeV gamma-ray flare [arXiv:1211.6344].

sources.summary: The paper discusses PKS 1346 112 as a flat-spectrum QSO and possible blazar counterpart to an offset gamma-ray detection near the MACS J1347.5 1145 field.

quotes.quote: Consulting the literature reveals the detection of a GeV flare consistent (within the quoted statistical errors) with this location in November 2011 torresi11 , and the flat-spectrum z=0.34 Quasi Stellar Object (QSO) PKS 1346 112 jackson02 , is given as a possible counterpart. Following this detection, a possible Wide-field Infrared Survey Explorer (WISE) blazar counterpart was identified, coincident with the radio source paggi12 . Re-running the likelihood analysis with this new source included in the model yields a negligible TS at the BCG position, and a TS of 179.6 (corresponding to a detection significance of 13.4 ) at the peak position, with a corresponding photon index of 2.44 0.03 and flux of (3.04 0.15) 10^ 8 ph cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 . Despite the high TS value, the transient nature of the source may account for its absence from the 2FGL.

gmw_00005293.name: B2 1308+326
gmw_00005293.overview: B2 1308+326 is a high-redshift blazar [arXiv:2310.05096] that features a diffuse halo [arXiv:astro-ph/0111279] and a curved radio jet [arXiv:astro-ph/0111279, arXiv:2404.06882]. The active nucleus exhibits multiwavelength flux variations [arXiv:2310.05096, arXiv:2403.13713], including major gamma-ray flares in 2014 [arXiv:2404.06882] and 2022 [arXiv:2310.05096].

sources.summary: The paper studies B2 1308+326 as a high-redshift transitional blazar, finding strong 2022 multiwavelength flaring and arguing that increased Doppler boosting can explain its LSP-to-ISP transition.

quotes.quote: B2 1308 + 326 (OP 313) is a high-redshift (z=0.9980 0.0005; ) blazar.

We extensively investigated the multi-wavelength emission from the transition blazar B2 1308 + 326 with a focus on its most recent outburst in 2022 using data from Fermi , Swift , and ASAS-SN. The blazar showed flux variations in each of the studied energy bands. The maximum daily averaged ray flux of (14.24 2.36) 10 ^ 7 ph cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 was detected on MJD 59761.65 during the 2022 flare.

The results of our broadband SED modelling suggest that the transition of B2 1308 + 326 from LSP to ISP is due to an increase in the Doppler factor, which also explains the shift in the IC peak and the decrease in the CD.

sources.summary: The paper describes OP 313 as a blazar whose GeV and optical activity triggered MAGIC observations, with no MAGIC detection during the studied high states.

quotes.quote: OP 313 ( z=0.997 , 2010AJ....139.2360S ): In 2014, this blazar exhibited an upsurge in its activity in the GeV energy range, which led to its inclusion in the LAT Monitored Sources catalog 2014ATel.6068....1B . From 2019 onwards, an increase in the source's activity was observed once again, evident in both the optical 2019ATel12898....1B and the ray bands 2021ATel14404....1H . MWL LCs, focusing on the time of the MAGIC observations, are shown in Fig.  fig:plot_mwl_op313 . During these periods of high activity, the MAGIC telescopes gathered 12.3 hours of high-quality data.
Despite these high activity periods, the MAGIC telescopes made no detections at that time.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes blazar 1308+326 from radio to gamma rays, linking its 2014 gamma-ray flare to spectral changes, delayed 43 GHz emission, and a new jet component.

quotes.quote: In this paper, we conduct a multi-frequency analysis of the gamma-ray bright blazar 1308+326 from February 2013 to March 2020, using the Korean VLBI Network at 22 and 43 GHz and gamma-ray data from the Fermi Large Area Telescope (LAT). Our findings reveal spectral variations around the 2014 gamma-ray flare, aligning with the shock-in-jet model. A strong correlation is observed between gamma-ray and 43 GHz emissions, with a 27-day lag in the VLBI core light curve, indicating a 50-day delay from the beginning of a specific radio flare to the gamma-ray peak.

This radio flare correlates with a new jet component, suggesting the 2014 gamma-ray flare resulted from its interaction with a stationary component. Our analysis indicates the 2014 gamma-ray flare originated 40-63 parsecs from the central engine, with seed photons for the gamma-ray emission unlikely from the broad-line region.

sources.summary: The paper describes 1308+326 as having a diffuse halo, a westward VLBI/MERLIN jet that bends sharply southwest, and a trajectory compatible with either shock-induced curvature or helical motion.

quotes.quote: 1308+326 : on the arcsecond scale this source shows a diffuse halo around the core with a luminous region toward the East and a bright pointlike component at about 10 arcseconds toward the North (Murphy et al. 1993). The VLBI image (Fig. 1308e ) shows a jet extended about 10 mas in the W direction, with a PA of 180 or 90 depending on which arcsecond structure we consider as the end of the jet. We favour the first possibility, given that the MERLIN image (Fig. 1308m ) reveals a well-defined jet which departs to the West and after about 100 mas bends sharply toward the SW direction, for a total extension of about 200 mas. The jet appears to follow a curved path that encounters the arcsecond-scale East component. In this case the hypothesis of a curvature due to a shock could be considered, but a helical structure can also easily explain the jet trajectory from mas to the arcsecond-scale.

gmw_00005294.name: AzTEC/GS3
gmw_00005294.overview: AzTEC/GS3 is a high-redshift dusty galaxy [arXiv:0910.3642] that is optically invisible but clearly detected in the infrared and radio bands [arXiv:0910.3642, arXiv:1109.6286]. Photometric redshift estimates support a high-redshift interpretation, placing the galaxy at a redshift of at least 2.5 [arXiv:0910.3642, arXiv:1109.6286].

sources.summary: BLAST 593 is a high-redshift dusty galaxy candidate with a 500 m detection, LABOCA coincidence, robust VLA 405 counterpart, and optical invisibility but IRAC detection.

quotes.quote: BLAST 593: z_ est > 2.5 This source has no catalogued 350 m counterpart, but is a clear detection at 500 m , and is nearly coincident with the second brightest LABOCA source in the field. In addition it has a solid and unambiguous radio counterpart in VLA 405. The host galaxy of this radio emission is completely invisible in the optical imaging, perhaps just visible in K , but clearly seen at 3.6 m (Fig. 7). All evidence points towards a high-redshift dusty galaxy (perhaps the most distant in our sample), but the lack of detections over a wide range in wavelength means that the photometric redshift is poorly constrained (see Fig. 5), and thus we adopt a lower limit for z_ est .

sources.summary: AzTEC/GS3 is matched to GS3a, a red IRAC/MIPS radio source near LABOCA and BLAST detections, with photometric evidence supporting a high-redshift interpretation.

quotes.quote: AzTEC/GS3. The faint IRAC source GS3a ( S_ 1.4GHz =40.7 6.5 Jy; P_ 1.4 =0.045 ) is also a MIPS 24 \ source with a red IRAC color ( [ 3.6 m ] [ 4.5 m ] = +0.37; P_ color =0.174 ). The 870 \ LABOCA source LESS J033248.1 275414 ( S_ 870 =8.8 1.2 mJy) is only 2.4 \ away from GS3a, leading biggs11 to conclude this source as a secure counterpart as well. dunlop10 also identified GS3a as the counterpart to the 250 \ BLAST source 593 and estimated a redshift z>2.5 for this optically invisible source. Our radio-mm photometric redshift of z_ MR =3.09^ +0.55 _ 1.11 supports this high-z hypothesis. There is a faint red IRAC source, GS3b, which is is also a tentative detection that cannot be ruled out.

gmw_00005295.name: AzTEC/GS4
gmw_00005295.overview: AzTEC/GS4 is a high-redshift candidate galaxy [arXiv:1109.6286]. It is identified by a single red IRAC counterpart that is also a faint radio emitter [arXiv:1109.6286].

sources.summary: AzTEC/GS4 has a single red IRAC/radio counterpart within the LABOCA error circle and is identified as a robust AzTEC counterpart with high-redshift photometric estimates.

quotes.quote: AzTEC/GS4. There is only one red IRAC source (GS4a; P_ color =0.070 ) within the 6.5 \ search radius. It is a faint radio emitter ( S_ 1.4GHz =25.4 6.5 Jy, P_ 1.4 =0.021 ). There are two other radio sources within 17 \ from the AzTEC position, but GS4a is the only source falling within the error circle of the 870 \ LABOCA source LESS J033249.2 274246 ( S_ 870 =8.8 1.2 mJy). Therefore GS4a is a robust counterpart for the AzTEC source although it is only a tentative counterpart for the LABOCA source biggs11 . This is another high-z candidate source with z_ opt =3.37 0.25 and z_ MR =3.53^ +0.57 _ 1.27 .

gmw_00005296.name: AzTEC/GS6
aliases_external: Luo et al. CDF-S Chandra source 230
gmw_00005296.overview: AzTEC/GS6 is an infrared galaxy [arXiv:1109.6286] that is classified as a candidate Compton-thick active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1605.08189]. The galaxy is associated with a red infrared and radio counterpart [arXiv:1109.6286], and it is located near a blue foreground source [arXiv:1109.6286].

sources.summary: The paper classifies source 230 as a CT AGN candidate, noting no strong Fe K line was detected, Brightman et al. fitted only a flat photon index, and its X-ray/mid-IR location supports CT absorption.

quotes.quote: However, only three of these can be considered as CT AGN: sources 95, 230, and 309 ( Chandra ids), although source 95 is only marginally CT.
Although most CT AGN display a very strong Fe K emission line, we were unable to detetect it in the case of source 230.

For the remaining two CT candidates in our sample (sources 95 and 230) and because of the limited number of counts, they only fitted the photon index for source 230 (obtaining a flat value 0.8) and all parameters were fixed in the case of source 95. In both cases, the column density was fixed to 10 ^ 20 cm ^ 2 , i.e. they were classified as unabsorbed AGN. In any case, the location of these two sources in the L _ 2-10 keV /L _ 12 m plot supports their CT nature.

sources.summary: AzTEC/GS6 has a nearby blue foreground source, but the authors favor the red IRAC/radio galaxy GS6a, also the robust LABOCA counterpart, as primary.

quotes.quote: AzTEC/GS6. The IRAC/MIPS source GS6b is located only 0.4 \ from the AzTEC/GS6 centroid. However, its blue IRAC color ( [ 3.6 m ] [ 4.5 m ] = 0.23) and spectroscopic redshift of z=1.102 (Stern et al., in prep) suggest that GS6b is likely a foreground object. At a distance of 12.4 , the IRAC/MIPS source GS6a is located outside the 7.5 \ formal search radius for a counterpart, but it has very red IRAC color ( [ 3.6 m ] [ 4.5 m ] = +0.45) and is a faint radio source ( S_ 1.4GHz =31.0 6.3 Jy; P_ 1.4 = 0.268). biggs11 identify GS6a as the robust counterpart for the 870 \ LABOCA source LESS J033225.7 275228 ( S_ 870 =5.8 1.4 mJy) located 6.2 \ away, and this galaxy is likely the primary counterpart to the AzTEC source as well.

gmw_00005297.name: AzTEC/GS7
gmw_00005297.overview: AzTEC/GS7 is a high-redshift infrared galaxy [arXiv:1109.6286] that is also identified as an X-ray source [arXiv:1109.6286]. It is associated with a red radio and infrared counterpart at a spectroscopic redshift of z=2.676 [arXiv:1109.6286].

sources.summary: AzTEC/GS7 is tied to red radio/IRAC/MIPS source GS7a at z=2.676 and near LABOCA emission, though GS7b may also contribute to the millimeter flux.

quotes.quote: AzTEC/GS7. The red IRAC/MIPS source GS7a is the only radio source within the 8.7 \ search radius ( S_ 1.4GHz =51.2 6.4 Jy; P_ 1.4 = 0.126). Therefore, it is considered a tentative counterpart to AzTEC/GS7. The spectroscopic redshift of GS7a, which is also identified as a \ X-ray source, is z=2.676 , in excellent agreement with its radio-mm photometric redshift of z_ MR =2.56^ +0.52 _ 0.92 . The 870 \ LABOCA source LESS J033213.6 275602 ( S_ 870 =9.1 1.2 mJy) is located only 4.2 \ away, and biggs11 also identify GS7a as a robust counterpart. A second possible counterpart, GS7b, is a red IRAC/MIPS source ( P_ 24 =0.151 , P_ color =0.168 ) located on the other side of the AzTEC centroid and may contribute to the observed 1100 \ emission.

sources.summary: The paper treats XID 197 as a likely high-redshift source with z=6.071, a secondary z=4.385 solution, no GEMS optical counterpart, near-infrared detection, and high inferred obscuration.

quotes.quote: We note the presence of 3 sources at z>5 , for which we investigated in more detail the redshift information. Two of them (XID 139 and 197) have only one photometric redshift z=5.729 and 6.071, respectively, from which also provides a secondary solution of z=4.385 for XID 197 Luo10 , while XID 485 has two different photometric redshifts ( z=7.62 from Luo10 , with secondary solution z=3.309 , and z=4.42 from Santini09 ). These objects have no optical counterparts in the Great Observatories Origins Deep Survey (GOODS; XID 139 and 485) or Galaxy Evolution from Morphologies and SEDs (GEMS; XID 197, which is not in the GOODS field) observations, while they are significantly detected by the Infrared Array Camera (IRAC) in the near-IR. Thus, a high-redshift ( z>3 ) solution appears probable.

If they were placed at z=3.7 (the median redshift of the sample), the best-fitting N_H would be 50^ +23 _ 18 10^ 22 , 42^ +20 _ 24 10^ 22 and 118^ +41 _ 28 10^ 22 cm^ 2 for XID 139, 197 and 485, respectively (i.e., they would still be in the highly obscured class).

gmw_00005298.name: AzTEC/GS8
gmw_00005298.overview: AzTEC/GS8 is a high-redshift galaxy [arXiv:0910.3642, arXiv:1109.6286] with a robust radio and infrared counterpart [arXiv:1109.6286]. The system is associated with a likely Compton-thick active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2004.07604] and exhibits bright infrared emission [arXiv:0910.3642, arXiv:1109.6286].

sources.summary: The paper describes PID 215 as an XMM source blended from two Chandra sources; its Fe K line and hard continuum make it a likely Compton-thick AGN.

quotes.quote: For Fe line driven X-ray redshift measurements, for example, for PID 352 vignali15 and PID 215, the Fe K line is assumed to be a cold line at 6.4 keV, since most Fe lines in our sample are found at that energy, as shown below, and the source redshift was fitted using these fine resolution spectra.

This selection results in five sources (PID 102, PID 114, PID 131, PID 215, and PID 398). They are thus considered as reflection-dominated sources and their true \ would be in the Compton-thick regime.

PID 215: Two Chandra sources are present at the XMM source position luo17 . The fainter source has a spectroscopic redshift of z=2.252 casey11 while the brighter source (which is fainter in optical/NIR) has photometric redshift estimates by H14 and z_ ph =2.6 by straatman16 . The XMM spectrum of this source shows a strong Fe K line feature at 1.75 keV, which is compatible with the photometric redshift for the brighter Chandra source. The strong Fe line and the hard continuum make this source as a likely Compton-thick AGN.

sources.summary: BLAST 66 is a robust high-redshift source with secure radio identification, z about 2 photometry, LABOCA support, and faint optical but bright IRAC emission.

quotes.quote: BLAST 66: z_ est = 1.94 This 250 m source has clear detections at 350 m and 500 m , both reassuringly close to the 250 m position. The radio identification is robust and unambiguous. The source lies just outside the ISAAC near-infrared imaging, but the ACS and Spitzer IRAC photometry is adequate to yield a unique and well-constrained redshift solution at z 2 , as shown in Fig. 5. The high-redshift nature of this source receives support from its detection by LABOCA at 870 m . The multi-colour postage stamps in Fig. 7 reveal a faint complex source at optical wavelengths, which is bright and unconfused at 3.6 m .

sources.summary: AzTEC/GS8 has a robust red radio/IRAC/MIPS counterpart, GS8a, a z=2.252 galaxy associated with the LABOCA source, plus a plausible red X-ray alternative.

quotes.quote: AzTEC/GS8. There is a single robust radio counterpart GS8a, 4.4 \ from the AzTEC centroid position with P_ 1.4 =0.038 . The IRAC/MIPS counterpart GS8a also has a red IRAC color ( [ 3.6 m ] [ 4.5 m ] = +0.33) and relatively bright MIPS 24 \ emission ( S_ 24 =620 Jy; P_ 24 =0.203 ). Both chapin11 and biggs11 have identified this z=2.252 galaxy as the counterpart to the 870 \ LABOCA source LESS J033205.1 274652 ( S_ 870 =7.5 1.2 mJy), located only 7.7 \ away from AzTEC position. The second IRAC/MIPS source GS8b, located 2.9 \ from the AzTEC position, is also a plausible MIPS 24 \ counterpart ( P_ 24 =0.089 ) with red IRAC color and is also a \ X-ray source.

gmw_00005299.name: AzTEC/GS9
gmw_00005299.overview: AzTEC/GS9 is a high-redshift infrared galaxy candidate [arXiv:1109.6286]. It has a strong tentative radio counterpart associated with a red IRAC/MIPS and X-ray source, as well as a nearby alternative red IRAC/MIPS counterpart candidate [arXiv:1109.6286].

sources.summary: AzTEC/GS9 has a strong red radio/MIPS/X-ray candidate, GS9a, but GS9b is a nearby red MIPS alternative that would imply a high-redshift radio-undetected system.

quotes.quote: AzTEC/GS9. The single tentative radio counterpart ( S_ 1.4 =86.8 6.6 Jy; P_ 1.4 =0.070 ) is associated with a red IRAC/MIPS source GS9a, located 6.2 \ from the AzTEC centroid position. It is also a detected X-ray source and should be considered a strong candidate for the AzTEC counterpart. Located only 3.5 \ away from the AzTEC centroid, the IRAC/MIPS source GS9b is an intriguing alternate counterpart candidate given its red IRAC color ( [ 3.6 m ] [ 4.5 m ] = +0.29) and MIPS 24 \ emission. If GS9b is the primary source of 1100 \ continuum emission, then it is likely to be a high redshift system as its radio non-detection requires z_ MR >3.3 . Slightly further away is GS9c, also a tentative red IRAC source.

gmw_00005300.name: AzTEC/GS10
gmw_00005300.overview: AzTEC/GS10 is a high-redshift infrared galaxy [arXiv:1109.6286]. It is associated with a robust radio counterpart, GS10a, which has a revised spectroscopic redshift of z=2.035 [arXiv:1109.6286].

sources.summary: AzTEC/GS10 has a robust red radio/IRAC/MIPS counterpart, GS10a, whose revised z=2.035 spectroscopic redshift matches the photometric redshift and LABOCA identification.

quotes.quote: AzTEC/GS10. There is a single robust radio counterpart to AzTEC/GS10, located 5.3 \ from the AzTEC centroid ( S_ 1.4 =89.3 6.4 Jy; P_ 1.4 =0.053 ). Its red IRAC/MIPS counterpart GS10a ( [ 3.6 m ] [ 4.5 m ] = +0.14) has a reported spectroscopic redshift of z=0.0338 in the GOODS/ESO VIMOS DR1 catalog popesso09 , but the same group revised its redshift to z=2.035 using new data balestra10 . This revised spectroscopic redshift is in excellent agreement with our photometric redshift, z_ MR =2.03^ +0.41 _ 0.75 . Another red IRAC source GS10b ( [ 3.6 m ] [ 4.5 m ] = +0.04; P_ color =0.077 ), located only 3.2 \ away from the AzTEC position, is not detected in the radio or by MIPS. The 870 \ LABOCA source LESS J033207.6 275123 ( S_ 870 =7.6 1.3 mJy) is located only 4.3 \ away from GS10a, and it is also identified as a robust LABOCA counterpart by biggs11 .

gmw_00005301.name: AzTEC/GS11
gmw_00005301.overview: AzTEC/GS11 is a high-redshift infrared galaxy [arXiv:1109.6286]. It is associated with a tentative radio and X-ray counterpart that is blended with an optically bright foreground source [arXiv:1109.6286].

sources.summary: AzTEC/GS11 has a tentative radio/X-ray counterpart blended with a foreground object; its photometric redshift conflicts with the BLAST foreground association, and LABOCA peaks at GS11a.

quotes.quote: AzTEC/GS11. There is a single tentative VLA radio counterpart for this source located 6.5 \ from the AzTEC centroid ( S_ 1.4 =46.0 6.4 Jy; P_ 1.4 =0.081 ), and it is also a \ X-ray source. Its IRAC/MIPS counterpart GS11a has a slightly blue color ( [ 3.6 m ] [ 4.5 m ] = 0.02), but the VLT ISAAK K- band image retzlaff10 shows that this IRAC source is a blend of an optically bright ( i=21.7 ) z=0.246 foreground source and an optically faint source second source, which is the radio source. The 250 \ BLAST source 109, located 30 \ southeast of the AzTEC centroid, is undetected at longer wavelength bands and is identified with a z=0.124 foreground disk galaxy dunlop10 . Our photometric redshift for GS11a, z_ MR =2.50^ +0.52 _ 0.88 , is completely inconsistent with this identification, and the proposed BLAST counterpart is unlikely to be related to the AzTEC source. No nearby source is found in the 870 \ LABOCA catalog, but the LABOCA map shows a S/N 3 peak ( 3.5 1.2 mJy) at the position of GS11a.

gmw_00005302.name: AzTEC/GS13
gmw_00005302.overview: AzTEC/GS13 is an infrared galaxy located in a region with a high concentration of IRAC/MIPS sources [arXiv:1109.6286]. Its most likely counterpart is GS13a, a red IRAC/MIPS source and faint radio source situated 2.1 arcseconds from the AzTEC centroid [arXiv:1109.6286].

sources.summary: AzTEC/GS13 lies in a crowded redshift field; the authors favor nearby red IRAC/MIPS radio source GS13a over a lower-likelihood X-ray source and a likely foreground radio source.

quotes.quote: AzTEC/GS13. There is a high concentration of IRAC/MIPS sources with spectroscopic redshifts between 1.0 and 1.6 in the region surrounding AzTEC/GS13. The most likely counterpart for AzTEC/GS13 is a red IRAC/MIPS source GS13a ( [ 3.6 m ] [ 4.5 m ] = +0.24), located only 2.1 \ away from the AzTEC centroid. This source is also a faint radio source with no known spectroscopic redshift. A second IRAC/MIPS sources GS13b is a \ X-ray source at z=1.039 mignoli05 , but its statistical likelihood of being the AzTEC counterpart is lower (see Table tab:ID ). A third candidate counterpart, GS13c, is a faint radio source, but it has a blue IRAC color ( [ 3.6 m ] [ 4.5 m ] = -0.25) and is therefore likely a foreground source.

gmw_00005303.name: AzTEC/GS14
gmw_00005303.overview: AzTEC/GS14 is a high-redshift infrared galaxy [arXiv:1109.6286]. It lacks radio emission but is associated with a faint, red infrared counterpart and a coincident submillimeter peak [arXiv:1109.6286].

sources.summary: AzTEC/GS14 lacks radio emission but has a faint red IRAC candidate near a LABOCA peak, with z=3.640 spectroscopy consistent with its photometric redshift.

quotes.quote: AzTEC/GS14. There are no radio sources within the 9.0 \ search radius. The only tentative counterpart is a faint, red IRAC source GS14a ( [ 3.6 m ] [ 4.5 m ] = +0.04; P_ color =0.083 ) located only 3.1 \ from AzTEC/GS14. Although no nearby source is found in the 870 \ LABOCA catalog, the LABOCA map shows a S/N 3 peak ( 3.3 1.2 mJy) nearly coincident with this position. The previously unpublished redshift of z=3.640 (Keck LRIS spectrum, H. Spinrad, priv. comm.) for GS14a is consistent with its photoz z_ opt =3.50 0.30 and the non-detection in the radio and the MIPS 24 \ bands, similar to the z=4.762 AzTEC/LABOCA source GS12a.

gmw_00005304.name: AzTEC/GS16
gmw_00005304.overview: AzTEC/GS16 is a high-redshift infrared galaxy [arXiv:1109.6286]. It is most plausibly associated with the red source GS16a at a spectroscopic redshift of z=1.719, which is detected in both radio and X-ray wavelengths [arXiv:1109.6286].

sources.summary: AzTEC/GS16 is most plausibly tied to red IRAC/MIPS radio/X-ray source GS16a at z=1.719, while the brighter blue GS16b is likely foreground.

quotes.quote: AzTEC/GS16. There are two faint radio sources within the counterpart search radius of 10.5 . The red IRAC/MIPS source GS16a ( [ 3.6 m ] [ 4.5 m ] = +0.53) is 6.1 \ away from the AzTEC position, and it is a tentative radio counterpart with P_ 1.4 =0.072 . GS16a is also an X-ray source detected by \ and has a spectroscopic redshift of z=1.719 (Silverman et al., in prep.). The second radio source GS16b is located slightly further away, 7.9 . Although it is a brighter MIPS 24 \ source, it has a blue IRAC color ( [ 3.6 m ] [ 4.5 m ] = 0.29) and a spectroscopic redshift of z=1.017 mignoli05 , suggesting it is a foreground source.

gmw_00005305.name: AzTEC/GS18
gmw_00005305.overview: AzTEC/GS18 is an isolated, high-redshift star-forming disk galaxy [arXiv:1108.5679, arXiv:2304.04683] with a photometric redshift estimated around z = 2.7 to 2.97 [arXiv:1108.5679, arXiv:2304.04683]. It features a large stellar mass [arXiv:1108.5679] and contains two off-center, dusty substructures that host a minor fraction of its total star formation [arXiv:2304.04683].

sources.summary: The paper treats the HUDF sub-millimetre galaxy as an unlensed, extremely red z=2.97 case study with a large stellar mass and disc-like rest-frame optical morphology.

quotes.quote: Despite the large beams, the three independent positions delivered by these millimetre--far-infrared facilities, combined with ultra-deep VLA imaging at 1.4 GHz, have enabled the identification of a unique optical/infrared galaxy counterpart associated with the far-infrared dust emission, as shown in Figure 1. This galaxy is optically very faint ( B 30 AB mag), but also extremely red, and its location in the sub-region of the HUDF imaged by WFC3/IR provides a unique opportunity to study an ``ordinary'', unlensed sub-mm galaxy with extra-ordinary optical/infrared/mid-infrared imaging. As shown in Figure 2, the unparalleled 12-band optical-infrared photometry delivers an extremely robust and accurate photometric redshift of z = 2.97 0.09 . The stellar mass of this HUDF galaxy as derived from the two-component spectral-energy distribution (SED) fit shown in Figure 2 is M_* 2.5 10^ 11 M_ .

Because of the exquisite depth and high angular resolution of the WFC3/IR imaging, it is possible to determine rather accurately the rest-frame ( B band) optical morphology of the (sub)-millimetre galaxy in the HUDF. As illustrated in Figure 3 (bottom row of panels) the result of fitting a two-dimensional axi-symmetric model with variable S e rsic index, is that the galaxy is a disc ( n = 1.09 ) with a reasonably large half-light radius r_ 0.5 4 kpc. These parameter values are similar to the (albeit more uncertain) values derived using ground-based near-infrared imaging of 15 sub-millimetre galaxies by Targett et al. (2011), who reported median values of n = 1.08 and r_ 0.5 = 3.1 kpc.

sources.summary: The paper identifies UDF2 as a z=2.7 star-forming disk whose JWST and ALMA data reveal an isolated stellar disk plus off-center dusty substructures containing significant stellar mass and star formation.

quotes.quote: The JWST images of UDF2 are shown in Figure fig:JWST_images . The most striking feature is the presence of a single stellar mass component, seen in all long-wavelength filters (F430M-F480M; rest-frame 1.2-1.3 ), but not obvious at shorter wavelength bands. The long-wavelength images show an isolated disk with no major morphological disturbance. This represents the true stellar mass distribution, an observation that was not previously measurable in HST /F160W image because of the dust attenuation. The centroid and position angle of the stellar mass component is in close alignment with that of the cold dust disk and the rotation plane of the molecular gas disk Figure 7 of Kaasinen20 .

In summary, we have identified, within a typical SFG at z = 2.7 , two off-center substructures containing 10-20 of total stellar mass and 5 of total SF. We highlight the fact that the central star-forming region is where 90 of SF is located and also contains 70 of the total stellar mass, which implies that the substructures have 2-4 lower specific SFR (sSFR) compared to that of the center.

sources.summary: BLAST 732 is a possible high-redshift source near a LABOCA detection; primary candidate 732-1 is a very red HUDF galaxy with robust z_est 2.97.

quotes.quote: BLAST 732: z_ est = 2.97 (or z_ est = 2.63 ) This source does not have a counterpart at 350 m or 500 m , but the 250 m flux-density is too faint for this to offer a useful redshift constraint. However, the lack of any radio detection suggests the source, if real, lies at high redshift, and indeed the 250 m position lies only 10 arcsec from the fourth brightest 870 m LABOCA source in GOODS-South (Weiss et al. 2009). We therefore fitted galaxy models to the optical-infrared photometry of all objects in the ISAAC K band within 15 arcsec of the 250 m position. This search produced six potential counterparts, three of which have estimated redshifts z > 1.5 . Of these 3, only one has a 24 m counterpart, so we adopt this as the primary identification.

sources.summary: AzTEC/GS18 has several candidates: nearby very red GS18a is formally robust, z=2.688 X-ray galaxy GS18b is plausible, and GS18c is likely foreground.

quotes.quote: AzTEC/GS18. There are two faint radio sources within the 9.3 \ search radius of AzTEC/GS18. The closest IRAC/MIPS source GS18a is formally a robust counterpart owing to its proximity to the AzTEC position and its very red IRAC color ( [ 3.6 m ] [ 4.5 m ] = +0.47, P_ color =0.113 ). Additionally, GS18b is a z=2.688 galaxy which is an X-ray source detected by \ akiyama05 and has a red IRAC color ( [ 3.6 m ] [ 4.5 m ] = +0.31), making it a plausible candidate for the counterpart to AzTEC/GS18. A third radio-faint IRAC/MIPS source GS18c is a tentative candidate based on its MIPS 24 \ emission, but it has very blue colour ( [ 3.6 m ] [ 4.5 m ] = 0.39) and is likely a foreground object. The 870 \ LABOCA source LESS J033243.6 274644 ( S_ 870 =6.4 1.3 mJy) is located 8.4 \ south of the AzTEC position, putting GS18a between the AzTEC and LABOCA centroids.

sources.summary: ASPECS-LP.07 is a faint z=2.696 CO(3-2) source whose photometric redshift and MUSE spectrum support the high-redshift CO identification.

quotes.quote: ASPECS-LP.07: CO(3-2) at z=2.696 . Photometric redshift indicates z=2-3 Skelton2014, Rafelski2015 , perfectly in agreement with the detection of CO(3-2) at z=2.69 . The source is faint ( m_ F775W > 27 mag) and a reextraction of the MUSE spectrum yields essentially no continuum signal (see fig:MUSE-spectra-2 ). This supports a redshift solution between z=2-3 , where no bright emission lines lie in the MUSE spectral range (see sec:muse ). Beside there being little continuum in the spectrum, there are no spectral features indicative of a lower redshift or higher redshift solution (cf. ASPECS-LP.03).

gmw_00005306.name: AzTEC/GS22
gmw_00005306.overview: AzTEC/GS22 is a massive, gas- and dust-rich galaxy at a redshift of z = 1.794 [arXiv:2108.05596, arXiv:1109.6286]. It features an ongoing starburst that is nearly 1 Gyr old [arXiv:2108.05596], alongside a complex, clumpy optical morphology that may indicate minor companions [arXiv:2103.05011].

sources.summary: The paper describes AzTEC.GS22 as a z=1.794 DSFG with compact ALMA dust emission, radio flux from star formation, and an almost 1 Gyr-old starburst in a massive gas-rich galaxy.

quotes.quote: AzTEC.GS22 (J033212.55-274306.1) AzTEC.GS22 is a DSFG galaxy firstly detected at 1.1 mm with AzTEC/ASTE (S _ 1.1 mm =2.1 0.6 mJy). Yun2012:2012MNRAS.420..957Y found a red IRAC/MIPS counterpart for the AzTEC source, with a spectroscopic redshift of z _ spec = 1.794 0.005 Tab. tab:galaxy_properties Wuyts2009:2009ApJ...706..885W, Targett2013:2013MNRAS.432.2012T , that we adopt in this work. We found a >5 continuum detection for AzTEC.GS22 in the ALMA B9 ( =400-500 m) and we measured a flux S _ 0.45 mm =5.8 0.8 mJy (Tab. tab:continuum ), that was essential to constrain the dusty peak of galaxy SED in Pantoni2021:2021MNRAS.504..928P , since the lack of the Herschel 500 m photometric point. The ALMA source is not resolved in the continuum B9 thus we provide just an upper limit on its size, i.e. _ ALMA <1.7 kpc, tracing the bulk of dusty star formation.
Moreover in Pantoni2021:2021MNRAS.504..928P we found that the VLA flux at 1.4 GHz (S _ 1.4 GHz = 34.6 6.5 Jy) can be traced back solely to galaxy star formation.

The burst of ongoing star formation in this object is almost 1 Gyr old (age 900-1000 Myr) and it is forming stars at a rate 90-100 M _ yr ^ 1 . Massive (M _ 6 10^ 10 M _ ), gas and dust rich (M _ gas 5 10^ 10 M _ ; M _ dust 1.4 10^9 M _ ), AzTEC.GS22 is characterized by a depletion timescale of _ depl 550 Myr.

sources.summary: AzTEC/GS22 is most likely associated with red radio/MIPS galaxy GS22a at z=1.794, though GS22b is a closer red alternative near the LABOCA peak.

quotes.quote: AzTEC/GS22. The faint radio source GS22a, located 7.8 \ away from the AzTEC centroid, is the most likely counterpart ( S_ 1.4 =34.6 6.5 Jy). This galaxy is also a red IRAC/MIPS source ( [ 3.6 m ] [ 4.5 m ] = +0.30) and has a spectroscopic redshift of z=1.794 wuyts09 . The 250 \ BLAST source 552 position centroid is 10 \ east of the AzTEC position, and dunlop10 also identified the radio source GS22a (located 7 \ away from the BLAST position) as the likely counterpart. A second red IRAC/MIPS candidate counterpart, GS22b, is closer to the AzTEC centroid and is an interesting alternative. No nearby source is found in the 870 \ LABOCA catalog, but the LABOCA map shows a S/N 3.1 emission peak near this red IRAC/MIPS source GS22b.

sources.summary: U5152 lacks a PACS detection but requires a starburst component for the mid-IR data; SED fitting also finds significant AGN emission at 3 sigma.

quotes.quote: For the 3 sources undetected by \ (U5050, U5152, and U5153), we consider the conservative 5 upper limits (2.2, 2.0 and 3.0 mJy at 70, 100 and 160 , respectively).

For the three sources with no PACS detection (U5050, U5152, and U5153), the starburst component is required by the SED-fitting procedure to reproduce the mid-IR spectral data, although its shape is not well constrained.

The SED-fitting procedure found that for all but one source (U428) the presence of an AGN is consistent with the photometry, although for only nine sources ( 35 of the sample: U4639, U4950, U4958, U5150, U5152, U5153, U5652, U5805, and U5877) its presence is significant at the 3 confidence level (i.e., solutions with no torus emission have ^ 2 ^ 2 _ min +28.5).

sources.summary: AzTEC.GS22 is described with a deep-field X-ray non-detection, an ALMA angular size measurement, and complex clumpy optical morphology.

quotes.quote: We note that the two X-ray non-detections (UDF5 and AzTEC.GS22) lie in very deep regions of the Chandra map (equivalent exposure times are 6.22 Ms and 5.80 Ms, respectively), thus the hypothesis of totally obscured X-ray sources is the most probable.

Just for two sources (ALESS067.1, AzTEC.GS22) we found the angular size (i.e., S e rsic effective radius R _ e ) of their ALMA counterparts in the DANCING ALMA catalog by Fujimoto2017:2017ApJ...850...83F , that we exploited to derive the circularized radii.

Most of the sources show an optical isolated morphology, while 4 galaxies (UDF11, ALESS067.1, AzTEC.GS21, AzTEC.GS22; Pantoni et al. in preparation) have more complex (i.e., clumpy) morphologies, possibly indicating the presence of minor companions - that can enhance the formation of stars and prolong the star formation in the dominant galaxy by refuelling it with gas - or just being a signature of the ongoing dusty star formation.

gmw_00005307.name: AzTEC/GS25
gmw_00005307.overview: AzTEC/GS25 is a dusty star-forming galaxy at a redshift of z=2.292 [arXiv:2108.05596, arXiv:1109.6286]. It is a young, isolated, and gas-rich object whose intense starburst is expected to trigger the rapid growth of its central active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2108.05596].

sources.summary: The paper describes AzTEC.GS25 as a z=2.292 DSFG with compact, smooth millimeter and optical counterparts; SED analysis finds a young gas-rich object whose starburst may feed AGN growth.

quotes.quote: AzTEC.GS25 (J033246.83-275120.9) AzTEC.GS25 is a DSFG galaxy firstly detected at 1.1 mm with AzTEC/ASTE (S _ 1.1 mm =1.9 0.6 mJy).

Both the millimeter and optical counterparts are resolved and compact. The ALMA radius is _ ALMA 1.2 kpc, while the optical radius is slightly larger, i.e. _ H 1.8 kpc (tabs. tab:galaxy_properties and tab:continuum ). The smooth morphology of ALMA continuum and optical emission of the galaxy suggest that this object must be isolated. These evidences support the scenario in which the bulk of star formation can be traced back to local, in-situ condensation processes.

From the SED analysis, we found AzTEC.GS25 to be a young object (age 300 Myr), forming stars at a rate SFR 400 M _ yr ^ 1 , with a sSFR 3.8 Gyr ^ 1 (M _ 8 10^ 10 M _ ). Gas and dust rich (M _ gas 4 10^ 10 M _ ; M _ dust 1.4 10^9 M _ ), with a typical depletion timescale _ depl 100 Myr, its very intense burst of star formation must trigger a rapid growth of the central AGN.

sources.summary: AzTEC/GS25 is associated with red radio/IRAC/MIPS galaxy GS25a at z=2.292, which is also X-ray detected and robustly matched to the LABOCA source.

quotes.quote: AzTEC/GS25. The red IRAC/MIPS source associated with radio emission GS25a is located only 6.8 \ away from the AzTEC centroid position. This galaxy, also detected in the X-ray by , has a spectroscopic redshift of z=2.292 popesso09 . The 870 \ LABOCA source LESS J033246.7 275120 ( S_ 870 =5.9 1.3 mJy) is well-centered on GS25a, and biggs11 also identify the same galaxy as the robust LABOCA counterpart.

gmw_00005308.name: AzTEC/GS27
gmw_00005308.overview: AzTEC/GS27 is a high-redshift galaxy with a plausible counterpart designated as GS27a [arXiv:1109.6286]. This counterpart is a red, weak-radio galaxy located at a redshift of z=2.577 near a LABOCA emission peak [arXiv:1109.6286].

sources.summary: AzTEC/GS27 has a plausible but not formally secure counterpart, GS27a, a red IRAC/MIPS weak-radio galaxy at z=2.577 near a LABOCA emission peak.

quotes.quote: AzTEC/GS27. The red IRAC/MIPS source GS27a ( [ 3.6 m ] [ 4.5 m ] = +0.38) is located 13.0 \ away from the AzTEC centroid, just within the counterpart search radius of 13.0 , and is associated with weak radio emission ( S_ 1.4 =23.6 6.5 Jy) from a z=2.577 popesso09 galaxy. However, formally this is not a secure identification ( P_ 1.4 > 0.20 ) owing to its large separation from the AzTEC centroid. Still, this is the most plausible counterpart within the proximity of AzTEC/GS27. No nearby source is found in the 870 \ LABOCA catalog, but the LABOCA map shows a S/N 3.1 emission peak near this red IRAC/MIPS source GS27a.

gmw_00005309.name: AzTEC/GS30
gmw_00005309.overview: AzTEC/GS30 is an infrared galaxy that is most plausibly associated with the red MIPS and radio source GS30a [arXiv:1109.6286]. Although its spectroscopic redshift is unknown, the galaxy is also associated with extended emission in the LABOCA map [arXiv:1109.6286].

sources.summary: AzTEC/GS30 is most plausibly associated with red MIPS/radio source GS30a, though its redshift is unknown and other red candidates plus extended LABOCA emission indicate ambiguity.

quotes.quote: AzTEC/GS30. The brightest MIPS 24 \ source within the counterpart search radius of 13.5 \ ( S_ 24 =459 6 Jy; P_ 24 =0.44 ) is also a faint radio source ( S_ 1.4 =37.2 6.2 Jy; P_ 1.4 =0.082 ) with a red IRAC color ( [ 3.6 m ] [ 4.5 m ] = +0.19). Therefore, GS30a is a tentative but the most plausible counterpart for AzTEC/GS30. The spectroscopic redshift of this galaxy is yet unknown. A second radio source (GS30b) and a radio-faint IRAC source (GS30c) are also tentative counterparts to AzTEC/GS30, both with red IRAC colors. No nearby source is found in the 870 \ LABOCA catalog, but the LABOCA map shows an extended source with a S/N=2.9 centered on the AzTEC centroid position.

gmw_00005310.name: NGC 160
gmw_00005310.overview: NGC 160 is a lenticular galaxy that features a clearly identified outer ring [arXiv:1309.5908]. This ring exhibits a strong correlation between ultraviolet and H-alpha emissions, although the H-alpha emission is patchier and completely absent in the galaxy's center [arXiv:1309.5908].

sources.summary: NGC 160 is described as a lenticular galaxy with a clearly detected outer ring, strong UV/H-alpha correspondence, patchier H-alpha emission, and no central H-alpha counterpart.

quotes.quote: NGC 160 NGC 160 has been classified as (R)SA0 ^ +pec in the RC3. In the H and UV images (panels a and of Fig. fig1 ), the outer ring is clearly identified. This is one of the best cases of correlation between H and UV. The H emission is more patchy than the UV in this ring. UV emission is detected in the centre, but H emission from there is completely absent.

gmw_00005311.name: NGC 5389
gmw_00005311.overview: NGC 5389 is classified as an S0/a lenticular galaxy [arXiv:1309.5908]. It features a ring structure visible in both H-alpha and ultraviolet wavelengths, which exhibits asymmetric brightness likely caused by inclination-dependent dust effects [arXiv:1309.5908].

sources.summary: NGC 5389 is described as an S0/a galaxy with a ring seen in H-alpha and UV, whose asymmetric brightness may arise from inclination-dependent dust effects.

quotes.quote: NGC 5389 NGC 5389 is classified as SAB0/a?() in the RC3. The ring can be identified in both H and UV images (panels d and e of Fig. fig2 ). However, in the UV image, the right part (East) of the ring is not as bright as the left one. This effect, also seen in the H image but not as clearly as in the UV, can be due to the inclination of the galaxy, which can cause one part of the galaxy to be affected more by dust than the other.

gmw_00005312.name: NGC 6962
gmw_00005312.overview: NGC 6962 is classified as an SAB(ab) spiral galaxy [arXiv:1309.5908]. It features two faint outer arms that are visible in both H-alpha and ultraviolet wavelengths, with the H-alpha emission appearing patchier than the more regular ultraviolet emission [arXiv:1309.5908].

sources.summary: NGC 6962 is discussed as an SAB(ab) galaxy whose two faint outer arms are visible in both H-alpha and UV, with patchier H-alpha emission.

quotes.quote: NGC 6962 NGC 6962 is a SAB()ab galaxy. This galaxy presents two main, faint outer arms, easily recognised in both H and UV images (panels a and of Fig. fig3 ). As in the other cases, the H emission is patchy, whereas the UV emission is more regular.

gmw_00005313.name: NGC 7371
gmw_00005313.overview: NGC 7371 is a spiral galaxy classified as SAB(s)ab [arXiv:1309.5908]. While its inner disc is devoid of recent star formation [arXiv:1309.5908], high-resolution imaging confirms the presence of spiral arms that appear as a false ring in lower-resolution ultraviolet images [arXiv:1309.5908].

sources.summary: NGC 7371 is described as having little inner-disc emission, a revised spiral classification, and H-alpha/R-band images that reveal spiral arms blurred into a ring-like UV appearance.

quotes.quote: NGC 7371 NGC 7371, whose classification is (R)SA0/a?() in the RC3, shows hardly any emission from the inner part of the disc, which is devoid of recent star formation. Buta2010 revised the RC3 classification, changing it to S A B(s)ab. The high spatial resolution of the H (panel d in Fig. fig3 ) and the R band (panel e in Fig. fig3 ) images allows us to confirm the presence of the spiral arms, whereas in the UV image (panel f in Fig. fig3 ) the poor GALEX spatial resolution leads to the false perception that the galaxy has a ring instead of spiral arms.

gmw_00005314.name: PGC 065981
gmw_00005314.overview: PGC 065981 is a peculiar spiral galaxy classified as SAB(s)ab? [arXiv:1309.5908]. Its spiral arms are identifiable in both H-alpha and ultraviolet images, though the inter-arm zone is difficult to define in the ultraviolet [arXiv:1309.5908].

sources.summary: PGC 065981 is treated as a peculiar SAB(s)ab galaxy whose spiral arms are identifiable in H-alpha and UV, but whose UV inter-arm zones are hard to define.

quotes.quote: PGC 065981 This galaxy, which has been classified as SAB(s)ab?, is a peculiar case. In both H and UV images (panels j and l of Fig. fig3 ), the spiral arms can be identified, more easily in the H than in the UV image due to the different resolutions of the images or due to the different stellar populations (see Section generalmorph ). Besides, in the UV, the inter-arm zone cannot be easily defined.

gmw_00005315.name: DLS212072337
gmw_00005315.overview: DLS212072337 is a confirmed strong gravitational lens system [arXiv:2211.00047]. The system features a foreground deflector galaxy at a redshift of z = 0.43 and a lensed background arc at a redshift of z = 1.81 [arXiv:2211.00047].

sources.summary: The paper describes DLS03 as a visually recovered arc candidate missed by the blind search because of bright lens-galaxy light, near clusters and with the largest Einstein radius.

quotes.quote: We recovered one arc candidate (DLS03) in F2 shown in Fig. fig:byeeye . The system was missed in our blind search because pixel thresholding was not able to successfully separate the arc from the foreground lens galaxy at any threshold. This was due to the brightness of the foreground lens galaxy ( R=18.19 ) and the extent of its halo in the DLS imaging.
The visually detected candidate DLS03 also lies close to these clusters ( 7.4 arcmin from the centre) and has the largest estimated Einstein radius ( 6.7 arcsec) in our sample.
We have started follow-up of the visually detected system but leave a detailed discussion and lensing analysis for a future paper.

sources.summary: The paper reports Keck/NIRES spectroscopy confirming DLS212072337 as a Grade-A lens system, with arc redshift z=1.81 and deflector redshift z=0.43.

quotes.quote: We have obtained spectroscopy with Keck Observatory to confirm the lensing nature of two Grade-A systems presented herein: DLS212072337 and DLS432021848 (Figure fig:keck-spectra ). Observations of the arcs were conducted with NIRES KeckNIRES on the Keck II telescope. Full details of the observations and data reduction are described in AGELpaper , along with spectroscopic redshifts for DLS212072337 (reported as AGEL091935+303156). We find a secure redshift of z_ arc =1.81 for DLS212072337 from detection of 6564 and 4960,5008 emission lines. The deflector galaxy is at a redshift of z_ def =0.43 , based on stellar absorption features from optical SDSS/BOSS spectra.

gmw_00005316.name: 4C 12.05
gmw_00005316.overview: The object 4C 12.05 is resolved as a double source consisting of a star blended with a fainter, south-preceding blue object [arXiv:astro-ph/9705034].
