Spectroscopic analysis identifies this fainter component as a radio-loud quasar at a redshift of z=1.395 [arXiv:astro-ph/9705034].

sources.summary: 4C 12.05 is resolved as a blended star plus fainter blue object, with the south-preceding component identified spectroscopically as a z=1.395 QSO.

quotes.quote: 4C 12.05 (Gower et al., 1967) = PKS 0035+121 (Shimmins et al., 1975) has been tentatively identified by Wills Wills (1976) and Jauncey et al. (1978) with a 16.5-17.0 mag object, the position of which is in good agreement with the accurate radio position measured by Condon et al. (1977); but they have shown that the optical spectrum, although inconclusive, was probably that of a star. However, Wills Wills have remarked that there appears to be a small, fainter, south-preceeding blue object, visible on the Palomar Sky Survey prints, blended with the image of the star. A V image, obtained on August 10, 1996 with EFOSC at the 3.6m ESO telescope on La Silla shows that, indeed, the object is double, with a separation of 2.8 arcsec. The spectrograph slit was aligned on the two objects (PA= 229 ^ o ); a 10 min exposure spectrum shows the north-following object to be a star, while the south-preceeding object is a QSO at z=1.395 (Fig. 1). The magnitude of the QSO, as measured on the spectrum is about 0.55 mag weaker than the star in B, and 0.87 mag in V.

gmw_00005317.name: Mark 998
gmw_00005317.overview: Mark 998 is a galaxy with suspected but unconfirmed nuclear activity [arXiv:astro-ph/9705034]. Based on its low-dispersion spectrum, the object is most probably classified as an H II region [arXiv:astro-ph/9705034].

sources.summary: Mark 998 is discussed as a galaxy with suspected but unconfirmed activity; its low-dispersion spectrum leads the authors to classify it probably as an HII region.

quotes.quote: Mark 998 . According to Markarian et al. (1984), this galaxy could have an active nucleus; Denisyuk Lipovetski (1984) and Lipovetski et al. (1989) were not able to confirm this. Our low dispersion spectrum (Fig. 2) shows 6584/ H = 0.23 . It follows that this object is most probably a HII region.

gmw_00005318.name: Mark 391
gmw_00005318.overview: Mark 391 is a lenticular S0a galaxy that was previously classified as a Seyfert galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/9705034]. However, its absorption lines and narrow emission-line ratios demonstrate that it is actually an HII region [arXiv:astro-ph/9705034].

sources.summary: Mark 391 is identified with NGC 2691 and reclassified away from Seyfert status; its absorption plus narrow emission-line ratios indicate an HII region.

quotes.quote: Mark 391 = NGC 2691 is a S0a galaxy (Huchra, 1977). For Arakelian et al. (1972), it weakly shows the characteristics of the Seyfert nuclei, with a broad H emission line ( 50 ). On this basis, V eron-Cetty V eron (1985) classified it as a Seyfert 1. Shuder Osterbrock (1981), however, concluded from their own spectroscopic observations that it is not a Seyfert. Our spectra (Fig. 4) show Balmer lines in absorption together with narrow (FWHM < 215 kms ^ 1 ) emission lines with 5007/ H = 1.21 and 6584/ H = 0.55 , proving that it is a HII region.

gmw_00005319.name: KUG 0929+324
gmw_00005319.overview: KUG 0929+324 is an emission-line galaxy characterized by moderate excitation [arXiv:astro-ph/9705034]. It is classified as an H II region that exhibits narrow emission lines [arXiv:astro-ph/9705034].

sources.summary: KUG 0929+324 is remeasured as an HII-region emission-line galaxy, with the authors finding narrow lines and a much higher radial velocity than earlier work.

quotes.quote: KUG 0929+324 is a moderate excitation ( 5007/ H = 2.68 ) emission line galaxy with an heliocentric radial velocity V=1500 70 kms ^ 1 according to Augarde et al. (1994). Our spectrum (Fig. 7) shows that it is a HII region with narrow emission lines (FWHM < 150 kms ^ 1 ) and 6584/ H = 0.10 . We found the radial velocity to be V=4740 kms ^ 1 (Augarde, 1995, private communication, gave V=4478 kms ^ 1 ).

gmw_00005320.name: CG 49
gmw_00005320.overview: CG 49 is classified as a Seyfert 2 galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/9705034]. This classification is confirmed after correcting for atmospheric-band absorption, which reveals a normal Seyfert nitrogen-to-hydrogen line ratio and emission line widths of 300 kilometers per second [arXiv:astro-ph/9705034].

sources.summary: CG 49 is classified as a Seyfert 2 after atmospheric-band correction raises the NII/H-alpha ratio to a normal Seyfert value.

quotes.quote: CG 49 . Salzer et al. (1995) published line intensity ratios for this object as follows: 5007/ H = 11.68 and 6584/ H = 0.30 . The 6584 line is too strong for a HII region and too weak for a Seyfert 2 galaxy. Our spectrum shows 6584 with the same low intensity, however it so happens that the redshifted wavelength of the 6584 line (6874 ) falls precisely at the position of the atmospheric B band. When corrected for atmospheric absorption by dividing the observed spectrum by the spectrum of a standard star (Fig. 7), we obtain 6584/ H = 0.79 , a normal value for a Seyfert 2 galaxy. The measured FWHM of the emission lines is 300 kms ^ 1 , in agreement with the adopted classification.

gmw_00005321.name: US 2896
gmw_00005321.overview: US 2896 is an emission line galaxy classified as a Seyfert 1.5 galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/9705034]. This classification is confirmed by the presence of a broad H-alpha emission component and an observed oxygen emission line [arXiv:astro-ph/9705034].

sources.summary: US 2896 is confirmed as a Seyfert 1.5 galaxy through broad H-alpha emission, with an observed OI line relative to the narrow H-alpha component.

quotes.quote: US 2896 (Huang Usher, 1984) = CS 109 (Sandu leak Pesch, 1984) is an emission line galaxy (Mitchell et al., 1984), and a Seyfert 1.5 galaxy according to Everett Wagner (1995). This is confirmed by our spectrum (Fig. 7) which shows a broad H component (2100 kms ^ 1 FWHM). A OI 6300 emission line is observed with 6300 / H _ narrow = 0.03.

gmw_00005322.name: Mark 646
gmw_00005322.overview: Mark 646 is classified as a Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/9705034]. This classification is confirmed by the presence of a broad H-alpha emission component and an observed oxygen emission line [arXiv:astro-ph/9705034].

sources.summary: Mark 646 is confirmed as a Seyfert 1 galaxy from a broad H-alpha component, with aliases and an OI emission line reported in the paper.

quotes.quote: Mark 646 , PG 1203+35 (Green et al., 1986), CG 885 (Pesch Sandu leak, 1988) or KUG 1203+354 (Takase Miyauchi-Isobe, 1991a) is a Seyfert galaxy according to Green et al. (1986). Our spectrum (Fig. 7) shows a broad H component (2350 kms ^ 1 FWHM); Mark 646 is therefore a Seyfert 1 galaxy. A 6300 emission line is observed with 6300 / H _ narrow = 0.05.

gmw_00005323.name: PKS 1420-27
gmw_00005323.overview: PKS 1420-27 is a radio-loud quasar candidate first identified in 1966 [arXiv:astro-ph/9705034]. Spectroscopic observations have confirmed that it is indeed a quasar at a redshift of z=0.985 [arXiv:astro-ph/9705034].

sources.summary: PKS 1420-27 is confirmed spectroscopically as a z=0.985 QSO, after earlier radio and optical work identified it with an 18th-magnitude quasar candidate.

quotes.quote: PKS 1420-27 . This radiosource was identified by Bolton Ekers (1966) with an 18 mag QSO. The identification was later confirmed by accurate optical and radio position measurements (Hunstead 1971, 1972). Our spectrum (Fig. 1) shows that it is indeed a QSO at z=0.985 . The emission line fluxes are 83 and 53 10^ 16 erg s^ 1 cm^ 2 for CIII 1909 and MgII 2798 respectively.

gmw_00005324.name: Mark 816
gmw_00005324.overview: Mark 816 is a 16.5 magnitude galaxy that was previously suggested to be a possible Seyfert galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/9705034]. However, its narrow emission lines and low line ratios indicate that the object is most probably an H II region [arXiv:astro-ph/9705034].

sources.summary: Mark 816 is discussed as a possible Seyfert galaxy, but the authors find narrow lines and low line ratios, making it most probably an HII region.

quotes.quote: Mark 816 = KUG 1431+529 (Takase Miyauchi-Isobe, 1985) is a 16.5 mag, possibly Seyfert, galaxy (Afanasev et al., 1979); however, 6584/ H < 0.3 (Afanasev et al., 1980). Our spectrum (Fig. 6) shows narrow emission lines ( < 240 kms ^ 1 FWHM) with 5007/ H = 0.63 . This object is most probably a HII region.

gmw_00005325.name: Mark 833
gmw_00005325.overview: Mark 833 is an emission-line galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/9705034]. Spectra of the galaxy reveal narrow emission lines with line ratios characteristic of an H II region [arXiv:astro-ph/9705034].

sources.summary: Mark 833 is identified as an emission-line galaxy whose confused literature identity is clarified by new spectra showing HII-region line ratios.

quotes.quote: Mark 833 = CG 590 (Sanduleak Pesch, 1987) is an emission line galaxy (Markarian et al., 1985) which has been called a "narrow-line active galactic nucleus" by Veilleux Osterbrock (1987), based on emission-line intensity ratios published by Shuder Osterbrock (1981); it however happened that the object studied in this last paper is Mark 883 which is, in a few occasions mistakenly called Mark 833 (H. Falcke, private communication). The nature of the emission-line nebulosity in Mark 833 was therefore unknown. Our spectrum (Fig. 8) shows narrow (FWHM < 225 kms ^ 1 ) emission lines with 6584/ H = 0.30 . This object is, therefore, a HII region.

gmw_00005326.name: Mark 483
gmw_00005326.overview: Mark 483 is an emission-line galaxy characterized by a strong ultraviolet excess [arXiv:astro-ph/9705034]. Its narrow emission lines and line ratios confirm its classification as an H II region [arXiv:astro-ph/9705034].

sources.summary: Mark 483 is confirmed as an HII-region emission-line galaxy with UV excess, narrow lines, and line ratios consistent with earlier HII-region suggestions.

quotes.quote: Mark 483 = CG 741 (Sanduleak Pesch, 1987) is an emission line galaxy (Markarian et al., 1988; Izotov et al., 1993) with a strong UV excess (U-B =-0.45 , Peterson et al., 1981). The emission-line ratios published by Markarian et al. ( 6584/ H < 0.33 ) and Izotov et al. ( 5007/ H 2.0 ) suggested that it is a HII region. This is confirmed by our spectrum (Fig. 8) which shows narrow emission lines (FWHM < 225 kms ^ 1 ) with 6584/ H =0.12 .

gmw_00005327.name: PKS 1903-80
gmw_00005327.overview: PKS 1903-80 is a flat-spectrum radio source [arXiv:astro-ph/9705034] identified as a quasar at a redshift of z=1.756 [arXiv:astro-ph/9705034]. Its identification is supported by precise radio position measurements and detected ultraviolet emission lines [arXiv:astro-ph/9705034].

sources.summary: PKS 1903-80 is confirmed as a z=1.756 QSO, with radio identification supported by accurate radioposition measurements and measured ultraviolet emission-line fluxes.

quotes.quote: PKS 1903-80 . This flat spectrum radiosource (Quiniento Cersosimo, 1993) was identified with a 19.0 mag QSO by Anguita et al. (1979). The identification was confirmed by an accurate radioposition measurement (Russel et al., 1992). It is indeed a QSO at z=1.756 (Fig. 1). The emission line fluxes are 57 and 25 10^ 16 erg s^ 1 cm^ 2 for CIV 1550 and CIII 1909 res pectively.

gmw_00005328.name: E 2344+184
gmw_00005328.overview: E 2344+184 is a spiral emission-line galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/9705034]. Due to its strong oxygen emission, it is classified most probably as a Seyfert 2 galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/9705034].

sources.summary: E 2344+184 is described as a spiral emission-line galaxy whose strong oxygen emission leads the authors to classify it probably as a Seyfert 2 galaxy.

quotes.quote: E 2344+184 is a spiral galaxy (Hutchings Neff, 1992). According to Margon et al. (1985), it is an emission line galaxy with a strong 6584 emission line. Our low signal-to-noise blue spectrum (Fig. 6) shows a strong 5007 line ( 5007/ H > 6 ), so it is most probably a Seyfert 2 galaxy.

gmw_00005329.name: J0020--0531
gmw_00005329.overview: J0020--0531 is an active galactic nucleus at a redshift of z=0.334 [arXiv:1607.06481]. The galaxy features strong, high-surface-brightness [O III] emission that resembles the open arms of a spiral galaxy and appears tidally warped by two nearby elliptical galaxies [arXiv:1607.06481].

sources.summary: J0020--0531 has high-surface-brightness [O III] gas arranged like open spiral arms, apparently warped by nearby ellipticals and plausibly shaped by outflow, infall, tides, or rotation.

quotes.quote: J0020--0531 (z=0.334) The strong [ O III ] emission mimics the widely opened arms of a SBc galaxy. The gas has high surface brightness in the centre, and appears tidally warped by two nearby elliptical galaxies at the same redshift. It is not clear whether the gas in the two 'arms' is outflowing due to AGN activity, infalling, or pulled out by tidal forces. It could be forced into the spiral shape by differential rotation.

gmw_00005330.name: J0024+3258
gmw_00005330.overview: J0024+3258 is a disrupted galaxy system located at a redshift of z=0.293 [arXiv:1607.06481]. The system features intense central [O III] emission and a twisting, 31 kpc collimated bipolar outflow that is likely ionized by a double cone [arXiv:1607.06481].

sources.summary: J0024+3258 is described as a disrupted system with bright central [O III] emission and a twisting, 31 kpc collimated bipolar outflow likely ionized by a double cone.

quotes.quote: J0024+3258 (z=0.293) This system has a complex, disrupted morphology. The [ O III ] emission is high in the centre. A collimated bipolar outflow extends over a total of 31 kpc in North-South direction. It gets wider with distance from the nucleus, and shows a twist beginning about halfway from the centre. It is probably ionized by a double ionization cone.

gmw_00005331.name: J0111+2253
gmw_00005331.overview: J0111+2253 is a compact host galaxy dominated by a 22 kpc long luminous unipolar outflow to the southeast [arXiv:1607.06481]. A nearby elliptical galaxy is located 35 kpc to the east, suggesting a past close encounter between the two systems [arXiv:1607.06481].

sources.summary: J0111+2253 is a compact host dominated by luminous outflows; a nearby same-redshift elliptical and shared diffuse halo suggest a past close encounter.

quotes.quote: J0111+2253 (z=0.319) This galaxy is dominated by a 22 kpc long luminous unipolar outflow to the Southeast. A fainter outflow of similar extent is visible to the South. The host galaxy is compact. 35 kpc to the East of J0111+2253 is an elliptical galaxy at z=0.321 . Their relative radial velocities are 450 220 .

gmw_00005332.name: J0159+2703
gmw_00005332.overview: J0159+2703 is an atypical green bean galaxy hosted by a bright barred spiral galaxy [arXiv:1607.06481]. Field spectroscopy indicates that the galaxy is relatively isolated, with its oxygen emission line region detected out to 25 kpc from the nucleus [arXiv:1607.06481].

sources.summary: J0159+2703 is an atypical Green Bean hosted by a bright barred spiral; [O III] is detected to 25 kpc and field spectroscopy indicates relative isolation.

quotes.quote: J0159+2703 (z=0.278) This is a rather untypical GB because the emission-line region is hosted in a 46 kpc size, bright spiral galaxy with a pronounced bar. In addition, or because of this, the [ O III ] EW is lower than in other GBs. A long-slit observation for our field spectroscopy survey intersects the outer areas of this galaxy and we detect [ O III ] out to 25 kpc from the nucleus. The barred spiral is not visible in the SDSS images in which this object was discovered. Field spectroscopy shows that J0159 + 2703 is relatively isolated.

gmw_00005333.name: J1347+5453
gmw_00005333.overview: J1347+5453 is a red, edge-on spiral galaxy with a diameter of 21 kpc [arXiv:1607.06481]. It hosts an active galactic nucleus that launches a bipolar outflow nearly perpendicular to the galactic disk [arXiv:1607.06481].

sources.summary: J1347+5453 is a red edge-on spiral where an AGN jet and double ionization cone light a bipolar outflow launched nearly perpendicular to the disk.

quotes.quote: J1347+5453 (z=0.332) This is a text-book example of an AGN jet and a double ionization cone lighting up a bipolar outflow. As such it is reminiscent of J0024+3258. The host galaxy is a red edge-on spiral with 21 kpc diameter, unresolved along its minor axis. The outflow is launched from the nucleus and nearly perpendicular to the disk. The southeastern outflow has a smaller opening angle, and after 5 kpc it bends by 90 deg and runs parallel to the disk.

gmw_00005334.name: J1351+0816
gmw_00005334.overview: J1351+0816 is a galaxy at a redshift of 0.306 that features a smooth, spherically round nebula with a diameter of 28 kpc [arXiv:1607.06481]. A compact, blue star-forming galaxy is superimposed 9.4 kpc from the nucleus, which may reside at a slightly lower redshift or contain gas from the host [arXiv:1607.06481].

sources.summary: J1351+0816 shows a smooth, round 28 kpc nebula; a superimposed compact blue emission-line source may be nearby in redshift or gas from the host.

quotes.quote: J1351+0816 (z=0.306) Imaging data reveal a featureless, spherically round nebula with symmetric brightness profile peaking at the centre. The sky noise is met at a diameter of 28 kpc. A compact blue star-forming galaxy is superimposed at a distance of 9.4 kpc of the nucleus. We took a long slit spectrum of it and detect a weak continuum and nebular emission lines. The latter place it either at a slightly lower redshift than the host galaxy ( z=-0.0007) , or they are due to superimposed gas from the host.

gmw_00005335.name: J1441+2517
gmw_00005335.overview: J1441+2517 is a low-redshift Green Bean galaxy with a likely compact host [arXiv:1607.06481]. It features an asymmetric emission-line nebula with a bright core elongated north-south and a weaker, more diffuse southern region [arXiv:1607.06481].

sources.summary: J1441+2517 is the lowest-redshift Green Bean in the sample, with a likely compact host and an asymmetric [O III] nebula extending north and south.

quotes.quote: J1441+2517 (z=0.192) This GB has the lowest redshift in our sample. The [ O III ] nebula consists of a 7 kpc long bright core elongated North-South. A host galaxy is likely compact. A fainter area of irregular surface brightness stretches over 17 kpc to the North, with an opening angle of approximately 90 degrees. The southern part of the emission-line nebula is weaker and more diffuse than the northern part.

gmw_00005336.name: J1455+0446
gmw_00005336.overview: J1455+0446 is a disturbed galaxy merger system that extends over 40 kiloparsecs [arXiv:1607.06481]. The system features an off-edge nucleus with bright emission wings, a northern star cluster candidate, and color gradients indicating tidally mixed gas and stars [arXiv:1607.06481].

sources.summary: J1455+0446 is a 40 kpc disturbed merger system with bright nuclear [O III] wings, an off-edge nucleus, and color gradients indicating tidally mixed gas and stars.

quotes.quote: J1455+0446 (z=0.334) This is the most a bizarre system in our sample, extending over 40 kpc. The [ O III ] emission is brightest in the nucleus and has two wings that extend in East-West direction. The nucleus appears at the edge of the galaxy. A condensation (probably a star cluster) is seen North of the nucleus. Significant colour gradients suggest a wild mix of tidally disturbed gas and stars, consequence of a violent merger.

gmw_00005337.name: J1504+3439
gmw_00005337.overview: J1504+3439 is an elliptical galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.294 [arXiv:1607.06481]. It features an ionized gas nebula with fragmented clouds, a 12-kiloparsec bubble-like structure to the east, and a distribution resembling low-redshift ionization echoes [arXiv:1607.06481].

sources.summary: J1504+3439 is an elliptical galaxy with fragmented [O III] clouds, a 12 kpc bubble-like feature, and ionized gas resembling low-redshift ionization echoes.

quotes.quote: J1504+3439 (z=0.294) The [ O III ] nebula in this galaxy is brightest at the nucleus, and fragments into several clouds of medium surface brightness. We find a bubble-like structure extending 12 kpc to the East of the nucleus. Fainter parts reach radii of 18 kpc in different directions. The ionized gas is superimposed over an elliptical galaxy measuring approximately 37 24 kpc. This is the only GB where the gas distribution resembles that of the Voorwerpjes (ionization echoes at z 0.1 ) described by kcb12,kmb15 , with the notable difference that Voorwerpjes are not observed in ellipticals.

gmw_00005338.name: J1505+1944
gmw_00005338.overview: J1505+1944 is a high-redshift Green Bean galaxy with an ionized gas nebula extending 28 kpc [arXiv:1607.06481]. A small elliptical galaxy at the same redshift is embedded in the nebula's western wing and appears to be tidally interacting with the gas [arXiv:1607.06481].

sources.summary: J1505+1944 is the highest-redshift Green Bean in the sample, with a 28 kpc nebula, a same-redshift embedded elliptical, tidal interaction, and offset [O III]/X-ray peaks.

quotes.quote: J1505+1944 (z=0.341) This is the highest redshift GB in our sample. Its nebula extends East-West over 28 kpc with high central surface brightness. The eastern wing is brighter than the western wing, in which a small elliptical galaxy is embedded and confirmed at the same redshift. It appears to be tidally interacting with the ionized gas. J1505+1944 is the only GB where the [ O III ] and X-ray peaks are significantly offset from each other (see Section xrayoffsets ).

gmw_00005339.name: J2050+0550
gmw_00005339.overview: J2050+0550 is an active galactic nucleus at a redshift of 0.301 [arXiv:1607.06481]. It features a smooth, high-surface-brightness extended emission-line region, a nearby 7 kiloparsec cloud to the northwest, and ionized gas extending out to a radius of 20 kiloparsecs [arXiv:1607.06481].

sources.summary: J2050+0550 has a smooth high-surface-brightness EELR, a nearby 7 kpc cloud, and [O III] detected to 20 kpc, implying larger ionized extent than imaging alone.

quotes.quote: J2050+0550 (z=0.301) J2050+0550 has a smooth EELR with high central surface brightness. A 7 kpc cloud is seen 4.5 kpc to the Northwest, less pronounced yet similar to J2240 0927. One of our field long-slit spectra was taken southwest of the nucleus. We still detect [ O III ] emission at a radius of 20 kpc. This shows that the [ O III ] sizes listed in Table targetlist , based on band surface brightness, may significantly underestimate the true extent of the ionized nebulae.

gmw_00005340.name: J2135--0314
gmw_00005340.overview: J2135--0314 is a compact galaxy at a redshift of z=0.246 [arXiv:1607.06481]. The system is dominated by a high central nebular surface brightness with curved and straight fringes, and it is located in an empty field without any visible interacting companions [arXiv:1607.06481].

sources.summary: J2135--0314 is a compact, high-surface-brightness nebular system with curved and straight fringes; the paper finds it in an empty field without plausible companions.

quotes.quote: J2135--0314 (z=0.246) The host galaxy of J2135 0314 is compact, overpowered by the high nebular central surface brightness. Three fringes lend a tattered look to the nebula. The northeastern fringe curves to the South over 9 kpc, the southeastern fringe is straight an can be traced over 13 kpc before disappearing in the sky noise. The galaxy is located in a remarkably empty area. Plausibly interacting companions are not seen.

gmw_00005341.name: J2202+2309
gmw_00005341.overview: J2202+2309 is a luminous elliptical galaxy with a physical extent of at least 40 by 25 kiloparsecs [arXiv:1607.06481]. It exhibits visible tidal disturbances in its halo and shares a common faint halo with another elliptical galaxy of comparable size and luminosity [arXiv:1607.06481].

sources.summary: J2202+2309 differs from other Green Beans as a luminous elliptical with visible tidal halo disturbances and a common faint halo shared with comparable ellipticals.

quotes.quote: J2202 + 2309 differs from the other GBs in the sense that its host galaxy is a luminous ( M_i 22.5 mag) elliptical. The [ O III ] emission does not dominate the broad-band colours as much. Tidal disturbances are visible in the halo of J2202 + 2309. The physical extent of the elliptical host is at least 40 25 kpc. A contrast-enhanced band image shows that this galaxy shares a common faint halo with J220215.5+230859, another elliptical of comparable size and luminosity, and possibly a third elliptical as well.

gmw_00005342.name: J2308+3303
gmw_00005342.overview: J2308+3303 is a face-on spiral galaxy at a redshift of z=0.284 with a bright, barely resolved nucleus and a featureless disk measuring 22 by 20 kpc [arXiv:1607.06481]. The system has one spectroscopically confirmed neighbor, a distorted spiral galaxy located 29 kpc to the north [arXiv:1607.06481].

sources.summary: J2308+3303 appears like a face-on spiral with a bright nucleus, possible compact or diffuse nebular flux, a 22 by 20 kpc disk, and a nearby distorted spiral.

quotes.quote: J2308+3303 (z=0.284) This system appears rather normal in our broad-band data, like a face-on spiral with insufficient resolution to discern the spiral arms. The nucleus is bright and barely resolved. Either the nebular flux is concentrated in the nucleus (and therefore within a physical diameter of 6 kpc), or it is more extended but with low EW and of relatively uniform surface brightness. The surrounding featureless disk has a diameter of 22 20 kpc. There is one spectroscopically confirmed neighbour, a distorted spiral, 29 kpc to the North.

gmw_00005343.name: SDSS J0823+2418
gmw_00005343.overview: SDSS J0823+2418 is a candidate dual or lensed quasar system located at cosmic noon with a redshift of approximately 1.8 [arXiv:2306.04041]. The system consists of two sources separated by 0.64 arcseconds that exhibit highly similar spectral energy distributions, which strongly suggests a gravitational lensing scenario [arXiv:2306.04041].

sources.summary: The paper tests SDSS J0823+2418 as a dual or lensed quasar; multiwavelength flux ratios and similar SEDs favor strong lensing, while radio and X-ray differences leave some ambiguity.

quotes.quote: Starting from a sample of dual/lensed quasar candidates discovered using astrometric jitter in Gaia data, we present a pilot case study of one of the most promising yet puzzling candidate dual quasars at cosmic noon ( z 1.8 ). Using multi-wavelength imaging and spectroscopy from X-ray to radio, we test whether the \ system is two individual quasars in a bound pair at separation 0.64 , or instead a single quasar being gravitationally lensed by a foreground galaxy. We find consistent flux ratios ( 1.25 1.45) between the two sources in optical, NIR, UV, and radio, and thus similar spectral energy distributions, suggesting a strong lensing scenario.

We plot the SEDs of SDSS J0823+2418 based on our multi-wavelength photometry in Figure fig:sed . We overplot several template SEDs of typical quasars from the literature for comparison Richards06b, Shang11 . The SEDs of the N and S source are qualitatively similar across the rest-frame optical/UV regime, and both show a steeper slope than the template SEDs towards the Keck K_ p filter.

gmw_00005344.name: NGC 1400
gmw_00005344.overview: NGC 1400 is an early-type lenticular or elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1209.4351, arXiv:1310.2607] and the second-brightest member of the NGC 1407 group [arXiv:0801.2003, arXiv:1310.2607]. It exhibits stellar rotation [arXiv:1002.0845], hosts a bimodal globular cluster system [arXiv:1209.4351], and displays compact X-ray emission [arXiv:astro-ph/0610934] with a diffuse X-ray trail [arXiv:1206.5751].

sources.summary: The paper studies NGC 1400 as the second-brightest early-type galaxy in the NGC 1407 group, measuring extended rotation and photometric structure while rejecting an interaction with NGC 1407.

quotes.quote: This is the first paper of a series focused on investigating the star formation and evolutionary history of the two early-type galaxies NGC 1407 and NGC 1400. They are the two brightest galaxies of the NGC 1407 (or Eridanus-A) group, one of the 60 groups studied as part of the Group Evolution Multi-wavelength Study (GEMS). Here we present new high signal-to-noise long-slit spectroscopic data obtained at the ESO 3.6m telescope and high-resolution multi-band imaging data from the HST/ACS and wide-field imaging from Subaru Suprime-Cam. We spatially resolved integrated spectra out to 0.6 (NGC 1407) and 1.3 (NGC 1400) effective radii.

NGC 1400 results The very central regions of NGC 1400 has been studied by many authors ( tonry81 ; davies87 ; faber89 ; beuing02 ). In this study we spatially resolved 82 regions along the major axis of NGC 1400, reaching a maximum radius of 1.3 r_ e from the galaxy centre. The radial profiles are presented in Figure kin2 and the kinematic measurements are reported in Table kinematics (all the data are available electronically). The galaxy displays a net rotation about the minor axis.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 1400 as a nearby elliptical whose high velocity, diffuse X-ray trail, and possible encounter may have disturbed the NGC 1407 group core.

quotes.quote: Previous \ observations have shown NGC 1407 to be more X--ray luminous than NGC 1400, and that there is a clump of emission between the two e.g., 2003ApJS..145...39M . Figure fig:xmm shows the \ image, adaptively smoothed using the sas asmooth task with a signal-to-noise ratio of 10. The image confirms the features observed in the \ data. The 240 MHz emission (contours) extends well beyond the brightest part of the X--ray emission associated with NGC 1407. The clump of emission between NGC 1407 and NGC 1400 consists of both point sources and diffuse emission.

Although brighter to the east of NGC 1400, the diffuse emission extends across the galaxy and to the west as far as the edge of the field of view. The origin of this trail is unclear. NGC 1400 is known to have a velocity offset from the group mean by 1100 km s ^ 1 1994A&A...283..722Q , which is 3 times the velocity dispersion of the group ( _v 370 km s ^ 1 ) and 2 times the sound speed v_ sonic (for a 1.2 keV gas, v_ sonic 520 km s ^ 1 ).

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 1400 as an old Eridanus-group galaxy near NGC 1407, with bimodal globular-cluster colours, steep red-GC density, and measured GC velocities.

quotes.quote: NGC 1400 has been classified both as a face-on S 0 Jarrett and as an E0 (e.g., daCosta ). It is the second brightest galaxy in the Eridanus group after NGC 1407 . We have assumed that NGC 1407 and NGC 1400 lie at the same distance of 26.8 Mpc. NGC 1400 is characterised by a uniformly old stellar age up to 1.3 \ Spolaor and by an unusually low systemic velocity (V _ sys = 558 ) for its distance.

Forbes06A studied the GC system of this galaxy using Keck/LRIS in imaging mode, detecting significant bimodality. The photometric GC selection was performed within 8 arcmin from the galaxy centre to minimise the contamination from NGC 1407 GC system, resulting in a clear bimodal distribution, with the colour separation occurring at (g-i)=0.98 . The GC surface density was corrected for the local NGC 1407 contribution. This galaxy shows the steepest red surface density profile among our galaxies, similar to the slope of the galaxy surface brightness.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes NGC 1400's globular cluster system, reporting its group context, shared distance with NGC 1407, inner GC core radius of 5.8 kpc, and flatter outer GC profile.

quotes.quote: Here we study the GC systems associated with NGC 1407 and NGC 1400. NGC 1407 (E0) is the brightest group galaxy (BGG) in an X-ray bright group (Osmond Ponman 2004; Brough et al. 2005, in prep.), which lies within the larger Eridanus cloud. NGC 1400 (S0-) is some 10 arcmins away from NGC 1407 in the same group. Although the two galaxies have quite different heliocentric velocities (V = 1783 km/s for NGC 1407 and V = 546 km/s for NGC 1400), both are thought to lie at the same distance b:tonry .

We fit isothermal-like profiles and derive GC system core radii of 9.4 kpc for NGC 1407 and 5.8 kpc for NGC 1400. For NGC 1407 we are able to separate the surface density distribution into blue and red subpopulations, giving 17.8 and 7.6 kpc respectively. Outside this central region, the radial profile of the GC surface density is similar to that of the galaxy light for NGC 1407 but it is flatter for NGC 1400.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 1400 as the group's second-brightest galaxy, emphasizes its extreme blueshift relative to the group, and reports ROSAT X-ray emission from it.

quotes.quote: The most notable is the second brightest galaxy, NGC 1400, with a velocity of 1072 km s ^ 1 with respect to the group mean. Astonishingly, the second brightest galaxy in the group, NGC 1400, is blueshifted by 1072 km s ^ 1 with respect to the group mean of 1630 km s ^ 1 , a line-of-sight velocity excursion of 2.8 . The most plausible resolution is that most of the mass is in an extended group halo and that NGC 1400 is akin to a test particle of negligible mass, presently near the potential minimum and directed close to our line of sight. 2001) assure that NGC 1400 is indeed at the same distance as NGC 1407.

The ROSAT data also show significant X-ray emission associated with NGC 1400 and with the probable member dwarf galaxy LEDA 074845. The resulting 0.1-10 keV X-ray fluxes are 1.5 0.3 10^ 13 ergs s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 for NGC 1400 and 1.1 0.4 10^ 13 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 for LEDA 074845.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 1400 as the second-brightest NGC 1407 group member, compact in X-rays, with an unusually high peculiar velocity and possible ram-pressure-related structure.

quotes.quote: Besides NGC 1407, the X-ray emissions associated with the group member NGC 1400 are also clearly detected. Being the second brightest group member in both optics and X-rays, NGC 1400 has a projected distance of about 11.7^ (90 kpc) southwest to NGC 1407 and has a much more compact appearance in X-rays. At the north-east of NGC 1400 there is a relatively weak, diffuse X-ray substructure aligned in the NGC 1400-NGC 1407 direction (marked with a cross on the image), which is not identified on either optical or radio images. It may be related to a dwarf galaxy in the 1407 group (Trentham et al. 2006) or may be a background source. Considering the unusually high peculiar velocity of NGC 1400, it is still possible that the structure is formed due to the gas stripping by ram pressure.

Since NGC 1400 is likely to be at the distance of the NGC 1407 group, as was suggested by Perrett et al. (1996) who compared the shapes of GC luminosity functions of NGC 1400 and NGC 1407, the observed unusually large peculiar velocity may imply that NGC 1400 is intruding into and merging with the group.

sources.summary: The paper models NGC 1400 as an elliptical with a power-law stellar profile and a flatter globular-cluster distribution, deriving 73 present globular clusters in the observed radial range.

quotes.quote: NGC 1400 The surface density profile of this galaxy is given by Forbes et al. (2006) (hereafter F06) who fitted it by mean of a power law _s() ^ 1.88 . This fitting law is reliable outside the galactic core, i.e. for >r_b=0.0055 arcmin (Spolaor et al. 2008). The luminosity profile of the galaxy for r_b is almost flat, and linked to the external power law. We fitted the GCS distribution by a modified core model with _0 = 14.1 arcmin ^ 2 , r_c=0.7 arcmin and =0.88 . Integrating _ GC () in the radial range where GCs are observed, i.e. from r_ min =0 arcmin to R=2.8 arcmin, we obtain N=73 as the present number of GC in NGC 1400.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 1400 as a lenticular group member whose stellar kinematics and rotation make polytropic halo models with cosmic baryon fraction worse than stars-only fits.

quotes.quote: NGC 1400 This lenticular galaxy is one of the two bright members of a nearby group along with NGC 1407, go93 . We extract the stellar kinematics from proctor2009 . We take their assumed distance, but use the B magnitude and S ersic model from spolaor2008 . Assuming a cosmic baryon fraction, none of the polytropic halo models fits as well as ``stars only.'' Perhaps this is attributable to the discrepancies between _ los profiles from different observations noted by proctor2009 . We also notice that NGC 1400 has a significant amount of rotation v/ 0.5 at R_ e , bert94 , which could possibly explain its +0.3 dex residual with respect to the Fundamental Plane ps96 .

sources.summary: The paper observes NGC 1400 but detects no planetary nebulae, attributing the non-detection to a distance of at least 8.6 Mpc and membership in the NGC 1407 group.

quotes.quote: In order to have reference data we observed the well-studied galaxy NGC 5128 (field F42 of Hui et al. \ 1993b) as well as NGC 1400, an E-S0 type of unknown distance.

In the field centred on NGC 1400 we detected no PN in approximately the same integration time as that spent on NGC 5128 ( 3 hr). Although the difficulty in co-adding the integrations is a complication, considerable effort was expended in searching for the correct shifts (only one direction is undetermined) and therefore this is not felt to be the cause of the non-detection. The simplest explanation is that the brightest PN in NGC 1400 are fainter than our detection limit of m 25.2 , putting NGC 1400 at a distance of 8.6 Mpc or greater. This is consistent with the result that NGC 1400 is a member of the NGC 1407 group .

sources.summary: NGC 1400 is described as the second-brightest member of the NGC 1407 group, with an embedded disk, rotating bulge, and GC kinematics consistent with the stars.

quotes.quote: This galaxy (E1/S0) is the second brightest galaxy of the NGC 1407 group. The combined velocity map indicates a moderately sized embedded disk on the scales of the effective radius within an aligned and moderately rotating bulge. Additional evidence for this interpretation comes from the clear anti-correlation between V/ and h_3 that is present only in this region. Its GC system kinematics are consistent with the stellar kinematics Pota:2013en .

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 1400 as an S0 Eridanus member with an anomalously low heliocentric velocity, similar distance to the group, and associated X-ray emission.

quotes.quote: All identified members in the group are in the Helio-centric velocity range of 1000 - 2200 , except NGC 1400 (S0), which has a velocity of 558 . However, NGC 1400 is predicted to be at a similar distance as that of the other members of the group based on the surface brightness fluctuation measurements.
The optically bright early type galaxies NGC 1400, NGC 1407, NGC 1395, and NGC 1332 are known X-ray sources in this group.
Apart from the X-ray emission associated with NGC 1400, NGC 1407 and a few other unresolved sources in the field, diffuse emission centered at NGC 1407 and NGC 1395 can be seen in Fig. fig:xray .

sources.summary: The paper gives NGC 1400 the same group-distance treatment as NGC 1407, finds unusual colour/CaT metallicity agreement patterns, and says its GC sample is too small for bimodality testing.

quotes.quote: We assume that NGC 1400 and NGC 1407 are part of the same group and use the mean of their distance moduli.
NGC 1400 is different again with agreement at low metallicities while having more metal rich colour-based values than CaT values at high metallicities (Figures fig:zhist b , fig:metalmetal b ).
NGC 821, NGC 1400 and NGC 7457 do not have enough measured GCs to determine whether their distributions are bimodal or not.

gmw_00005345.name: UDFz-42566566
gmw_00005345.overview: UDFz-42566566 is a bright star-forming galaxy with a redshift of approximately 6.82 [arXiv:1407.3787, arXiv:0909.5183]. It contains sufficient gas and dust to attenuate its Lyman-alpha emission [arXiv:1407.3787] and is resolved into two distinct components that may represent a merger or star-forming clumps in a disk [arXiv:0909.5183].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes HUDF-J033242.56-274656.6/G2 1408 as a bright z~7 galaxy, finding no confirmed Ly emission, a redshift near 6.82, dust attenuation, and a moderately evolved star-forming state.

quotes.quote: HUDF-J033242.56-274656.6 galaxy ( G2 1408 in cast10 and hereafter) has been observed through four different VLT/FORS programs collected in the period 2009-2012, 084.A-0951(A) (P.I. A. Fontana), 086.A-0968(A) -- 088.A-1013(A) (P.I. A. Bunker), 088.A-1008(A) (P.I., R. Bouwens), and 283.A-5063 (P.I., M. Carollo) with exposure times of 18 (Fontana, F18), 27 (Bunker, B27), 7 (Bouwens, B7) and 8 (Carollo, C8) hours on target, respectively, for a total of 60 hours integration time. Unfortunately, in the program 283.A-5063 the G2 1408 source was placed at a position where the CCD has a defect and no dithering has been performed, therefore we exclude the 283.A-5063 program from the following analysis. The total usable exposure time is 52 hours.

Once the Ly constraints are included, the redshift of G2 1408 is constrained within 6.7 and 6.95 at 1 and 6.6 and 7.1 at 2 . The top panel of Figure degene shows the resulting region where the redshift would lie, that is z=6.82_ 0.1 ^ +0.1 (at one sigma). We note that the 2 range is consistent with the continuum-only simulations described above and the non detection in the sky-free region of the spectrum at 9000 < <9280 , that both suggest a lower limit of the redshift, z_ MIN =6.6 .

Even though we cannot rule out a major contribution of the IGM in damping the line, the most plausible interpretation is that G2 1408 is a star-forming galaxy at z 6.82 moderately evolved and containing sufficient gas and dust to attenuate the Ly emission, before it reaches the intergalactic medium. If compared with fainter ( 0.05L^ <L<0.2L^ ), young, less massive and less evolved counterparts that show Ly emission and much steeper UV slopes < -2.5 (e.g., balestra13,vanz14 ), G2 1408 appears more massive and evolved ( 2 ).

sources.summary: The paper describes UDFz-42566566 as the brightest z~7 galaxy in the sample, resolved into two similar components that may be merging or clumps in a disk-like system.

quotes.quote: The galaxy UDFz-42566566 is the brightest galaxy in our sample and consists of two clearly distinct components, separated by 2 kpc. These two each contribute about the same amount of light (1:1.2) with very similar colors. They have individual half-light radii of 0.5 kpc and 0.8 kpc, respectively, very similar to the compact galaxies in our sample. One interpretation for the origin of the individual components is therefore that they are in a merging phase. The linear geometry of the whole galaxy, however, may also suggest that the individual clumps are star-forming regions within a disk structure, similar to what has been found at z 2 in observations and simulations.

gmw_00005346.name: UDFz-39557176
gmw_00005346.overview: UDFz-39557176 is a high-redshift galaxy consisting of at least two components [arXiv:0909.5183]. These two knots are likely physically connected and undergoing a merger, with the fainter northwest component potentially hosting an older stellar population [arXiv:0909.5183].

sources.summary: The paper describes UDFz-39557176 as a two-component high-redshift galaxy whose knots are probably connected and merging, with the redder northwest component possibly containing an older stellar population.

quotes.quote: The galaxy UDFz-39557176 also consists of at least two components. The total light of this galaxy is dominated by a slightly elongated central structure, which has a fainter counterpart about 2 kpc away to the NW. The flux ratio of these two is 1:1.4. It is worth noting that this galaxy has been split into two sources in the mclure09 catalog.

However, the two knots are most probably physically connected and are about to merge with each other. The fainter component to the NW shows a significantly redder \ color compared to the central core by 0.2 mag, and also a redder \ color by 0.1 mag. This may indicate that the second component consists of older stellar populations.

gmw_00005347.name: 4FGL J1544.3-0649
gmw_00005347.overview: 4FGL J1544.3-0649 is a transient blazar that remained unnoticed at high energies until it brightened in May 2017 [arXiv:2112.06232]. During this active state, the source exhibited highly variable X-ray flux and spectral slopes [arXiv:2011.10237].

sources.summary: The paper presents 4FGL J1544.3-0649 as a transient blazar that brightened from an anonymous radio source to an extreme X-ray/gamma-ray blazar with variable flux, hard X-rays, and SSC-like broadband emission.

quotes.quote: We present a multifrequency study of the transient \ source , a blazar that exhibited a remarkable behaviour raising from the state of an anonymous mid-intensity radio source, never detected at high energies, to that of one of the brightest extreme blazars in the X-ray and \ sky. Our analysis shows that the averaged ray spectrum is well described by a powerlaw with a photon index of 1.87 0.04 , while the flux above 100 MeV is (8.0 0.9) 10^ 9 photon cm^ 2 s^ 1 , which increases during the active state of the source. The X-ray flux and spectral slope are both highly variable, with the highest 2-10 keV flux reaching (1.28 0.05) 10^ 10 erg cm^ 2 s^ 1 . On several observations the X-ray spectrum hardened to the point implying as SED peak moving to energies larger than 10 keV. As in many extreme blazars the broadband spectral energy distribution can be described by a homogeneous one-zone synchrotron-self-Compton leptonic model.

sources.summary: The paper describes 4FGL J1544.3 0649 as a transient blazar brightened in 2017, with high expected neutrino flux among bright-blazar candidates.

quotes.quote: Transient Blazars and Neutrino Astronomy The source 4FGL J1544.3 0649 is a remarkable blazar that remained unnoticed at high energies until May 2017 when it showed a transient-like behaviour brightening to such a level to be detected by Fermi LAT and the MAXI X-ray sky monitor.

Considering that the expected neutrino flux from 4FGL J1544.3 0649 is the fifth largest in the list of 66 bright blazars considered in Ref. , blazars that are currently uncatalogued and below detectability in the X-ray and bands could even be a major population of neutrino sources.
Intermittent proton acceleration occurring in persistent IHBLs, or in transient blazars like 4FGL J1544.3- 0649, would further ease the constraint on the background.

gmw_00005348.name: 1RXS J101015.9-311909
gmw_00005348.overview: The galaxy 1RXS J101015.9-311909 is a high-frequency-peaked BL Lacertae object located at a redshift of z=0.14 [arXiv:1305.4597, arXiv:1204.1964]. It hosts an active galactic nucleus with a compact radio core and a faint jet [arXiv:1204.1964, arXiv:2310.10206] that emits very-high-energy gamma rays [arXiv:1204.1964].

sources.summary: The paper applies a one-zone SSC algorithm to this high-frequency-peaked BL Lac, constraining its SED, Doppler factor, emitting-region size, and out-of-equipartition energy budget.

quotes.quote: We present a first application of our algorithm to the typical high-frequency-peaked BL Lac object , provide constraints on the SSC parameters, and discuss the result in terms of our understanding of the blazar emitting region. Radiation mechanisms: non-thermal; Relativistic processes; Methods: numerical; Galaxies : BL Lacertae objects; Galaxies : individual : 1RXS J101015.9-311909

In this Section we present a first application of our numerical algorithm to the typical HBL . The TeV emission from this source, located at a redshift of 0.143 Piranomonte07 , has been detected with \ tentenpaper . The multi-wavelength study presented in the \ discovery paper shows that there are two major systematic uncertainties in the SED of this object. The first one comes from a possible absorption effect in X-rays, affecting the evaluation of the position of the synchrotron peak; the second one comes from a problem in the determination of the \ spectrum at low energies due to a possible contamination by diffuse ray emission from the Galactic foreground. In the following we consider that the synchrotron peak is located at an energy between the available UV and X-ray data case B in tentenpaper , and the \ spectrum is evaluated using a 1 GeV low-energy threshold.

The first important result is that we can define a minimum Doppler factor that solves the system: _ min 31 . In Fig. 1RXSJ1010contourplot (top-left plot) we show the contour plots of all our solutions in the B plane, and we compare them to the analytical constraints computed following Tavecchio98 . It becomes clear that with our numerical approach we significantly narrow down the accessible parameter space.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 1RXS J101015.9-311909 as a z=0.14 BL Lac AGN, reports its first VHE detection, and models its SED with a one-zone SSC scenario.

quotes.quote: \ is a galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.14 hosting an active nucleus (called AGN) belonging to the class of bright BL Lac objects.

VHE emission at a position coincident with \ is detected with \ for the first time. In a total good-quality livetime of about 49 hours, we measure 263 excess counts, corresponding to a significance of 7.1 standard deviations. The photon spectrum above 0.2 TeV can be described by a power-law with a photon index of . The integral flux above 0.2 TeV is about 0.8 of the flux of the Crab nebula and shows no significant variability over the time reported.

The spectral energy distribution (SED) can be reproduced using a simple one-zone \ Self Compton (SSC) model with emission from a region with a Doppler factor of 30 and a magnetic field between 0.025 and 0.16 G .

sources.summary: H.E.S.S. detected 1RXS J101015.9--311909 as an HBL; Swift hinted at intrinsic soft X-ray absorption, and SSC fits imply a weakly magnetized blazar emission zone.

quotes.quote: 1RXS J101015.9--311909 ( z=0.140 ): The 2006/10 observations of this HBL have been presented in . The source was detected at the significance level of 7.1 after 49 h exposure, with _ VHE = 3.08 0.42_ stat 0.20_ sys , and F( _ >0.2 TeV ) = 2.35 10^ 12 cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 . No evidence for a variability within the TeV range was found in the collected dataset. The accompanying observations with Swift X-ray Telescope revealed some hints for the intrinsic absorption of the soft X-ray continuum of the source by the neutral gas with the hydrogen column number density N_ H 10^ 21 cm ^ 2 .

The tentative status of the detection of the absorption features in the X-ray spectrum of 1RXS J101015.9--311909 complicates the modeling of the averaged non-simultaneous SED of the source. Different versions of the applied SSC model fits indicated however in accord that, in the framework of the one-zone approximation, the blazar emission zone has to be only weakly magnetized, with the radiating electrons and magnetic field comoving energy densities ratio of the order of u'_e/u'_B 10-100 .

sources.summary: The paper reports a fast jet component in 1008 310, observed across four epochs, appearing as a shoulder off the core, and receiving a Fair quality code.

quotes.quote: 1008 310 Component 1 is observed at four epochs, with a proper motion of 0.459 0.143 mas yr ^ 1 . It is a `shoulder' of emission off of the core rather than a well-defined feature. It has a quality code of `Fair'.

sources.summary: The paper reports that 1008 310 has a compact X-band core with a faint jet, a slightly more extended 2.3 GHz jet, and a measured TeV flux.

quotes.quote: 1008 310: X band observations of piner14 and piner18 show a compact core structure with a faint jet with opening angle of 47.8 . Our 2.3 GHz image shows a slightly more extended jet towards the east--northeast. Integral flux above 0.2 TeV is 0.008 CU hess1008 .

gmw_00005349.name: SDSS J1029+2623
gmw_00005349.overview: SDSS J1029+2623 is a background quasar at a redshift of approximately 2.199 [arXiv:2301.11240, arXiv:2401.03024]. The quasar is strongly lensed by a foreground galaxy cluster into three distinct images [arXiv:2301.11240, arXiv:2401.03024], with the lensed emission line galaxy displaying a clearly visible rotation curve [arXiv:astro-ph/0703312].

sources.summary: The paper treats J1029 as the lens system with a clearly visible lensed-galaxy rotation curve, using IFU data for three-dimensional lens modeling despite limited spatial resolution.

quotes.quote: With one exception, the internal velocity gradients in the lensed emission line galaxies observed by this program are unresolved spectroscopically. The exception is J1029, for which the rotation curve of the lensed galaxy is clearly visible. Figure rotcurve shows a section of the extracted IFU spectrum array for this system. The appearance of resolved velocity structure in the lensed source affords a unique opportunity for three-dimensional strong lens modeling: two spatial dimensions plus one velocity dimension.

In practice, the spatial resolution of our J1029 data is not sufficient to enable a high-quality analysis. Nevertheless, the wavelength-dependent (i.e., velocity-dependent) lensed image structure, seen in iso-wavelength slices through the continuum-subtracted data-cube in the first and third rows of Figure ifu1029 , reveals intriguing behavior that invites a plausible explanation in terms of three-dimensional lens modeling. We describe our modeling technique here, which predicts the data-cube slices shown in the second and fourth rows of Figure ifu1029 . Though the data and model disagree in detail, the qualitative features of the data are reproduced by the model.

sources.summary: The paper uses SDSS J1029+2623 as a three-image cluster-lensed quasar with refined source redshift, HST/MUSE constraints, lens modeling, and a measured A-B delay.

quotes.quote: \ is a cluster at \ = 0.588 that is strongly lensing a quasar at \ = 2.1992 into three images Inada2006, Oguri2008 . The quasar images are in a naked cusp configuration with a maximum image separation of 22 5 (Table table:quasar_table ). reported spectroscopic redshifts of several galaxies in the field, based on Multi Unit Spectroscopic Explorer (MUSE) spectroscopy from the Very Large Telescope. They refined the redshift measurement of the quasar to \ = 2.1992 (formerly reported as \ = 2.197, ).

These data were originally proposed to identify multiply-imaged galaxies to construct a mass model Sharon2005 , search for the fifth quasar image Inada2005 , derive _ , perform a weak lensing analysis, and search for supernovae in massive high-redshift clusters Sharon2010 . These data also enabled studies of the spectral energy distribution of the quasar host galaxy Ross2009 , the ultraviolet upturn in red sequence galaxies Ali2018 , and active galactic nuclei in massive clusters Klesman2012 . We modeled \ using one cluster-scale halo, one brightest cluster galaxy (BCG)-scale halo, and a galaxy-scale halo for each of the cluster member galaxies, 168 in total, five (including the BCG) of which have their slope parameters optimized instead of adopting the scaling relations from . We optimized the parameters for these five galaxies because of either their proximity to the quasar images or their necessity for reproducing the lensed image configuration. We modeled the cluster using both source-plane minimization and image-plane minimization, and evaluated the quality of the models obtained by each approach. While formally the image-plane minimization resulted in a better image-plane scatter, these models produced additional quasar images that are not observed. Therefore, we proceeded with the source-plane minimization for \ for the remainder of the analysis. We note that the best-fit lens model produced large scatter between the observed and model-predicted positions in the image plane for quasar image C. In our results, we checked what happens when image C is removed from the \ measurement. The model consists of \ free parameters and \ constraints. The \ data and the lens model for \ are shown in Figure fig:lens_models . The redshifts of the arcs in our lens model are the same as those used by . The strong lensing mass model parameters are reported in Table table:1004_model . The measured time delay between images A and B ( t _ AB = -38.4 2.0 days) was first published in . In this notation, a positive value of the time delay means image A leads the other image. In addition to reporting a refined value of t _ AB = -40.6 1.8 days, measured the time delay between images A and C ( t _ AC = -821.6 2.1 days) and set a lower limit of t _ AD > 1250 days. After the completion of a 14.5 year monitoring campaign at the 1.2m Fred Lawrence Whipple Observatory (FLWO), presented new light curves for the four brightest images in , resulting in updated time delay values of t _ AB = -43.01 0.27, t _ AC = -825.23 0.46 days, and t _ AD = 1633.23 0.97 days (Table table:time_delays ). \ is a cluster at \ = 0.588 that is strongly lensing a quasar at \ = 2.1992 into three images Inada2006, Oguri2008 . The quasar images are in a naked cusp configuration with a maximum image separation of 22 5 (Table table:quasar_table ). reported spectroscopic redshifts of several galaxies in the field, based on Multi Unit Spectroscopic Explorer (MUSE) spectroscopy from the Very Large Telescope. They refined the redshift measurement of the quasar to \ = 2.1992 (formerly reported as \ = 2.197, ). The other spectroscopically confirmed objects from MUSE include a doubly-imaged galaxy at =2.1812, a septuply-imaged galaxy at =3.0275, a quadruply-imaged galaxy at =3.0278, a doubly-imaged galaxy at =1.0232, and a quadruply-imaged galaxy at =5.0622 Acebron2022 (Table table:constraints ). We used archival HST multi-color imaging from GO-12195 (PI: Oguri): WFC3/F160W (2 orbits), ACS/F814W (3 orbits), and ACS/F475W (2 orbits). These data were originally proposed to identify multiply-imaged galaxies to construct a mass model that could be used to better understand the anomalous flux ratios between two of the quasar images and the dynamical state of the cluster Oguri2013 . These \ data also enabled a weak lensing analysis and a morphology study of the quasar host galaxy Oguri2013 .
published the time delay measurement between images A and B ( t _ AB = 744 10 days) based on photometric monitoring campaign at the FLWO 1.2m.

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSS J1029+2623 as a double-image cluster-lensed quasar system and compares its separation, redshift, and inferred cluster mass with simulations.

quotes.quote: The second case, SDSS J1029+2623, was a double-image system with a separation of 22.5 arcsec. The lensing cluster is likely at redshift 0.55 ( Inada06 ), and the background quasar at redshift =2.197 .

J1029+2623 is from SDSS-II, which has now discovered about 10,000 QSOs, which is roughly 1/4 of the QSOs discovered in the Data Release 3, which has an area of about 3700 deg ^2 , so these two lenses were discovered in an effective area of 5740+3700/4 6500 deg ^2 in the SDSS spectroscopic sample. In the 0 model, the two crosses in the top left panel in Fig. fig:multi shows the positions we predict, (14.6, 0.55) and (22.5, 0.93) for these two systems. The number of lenses with separation larger than 14.6 quadrupole-images (J1004+4112) and 22.5 double images (J1029+2623) are 0.55 6500/5000=0.7 and 0.93 6500/5000=1.2 .

The mass of SDSS J1029+2623 is more uncertain. The image separation implies a cluster velocity dispersion 900km/s (see Inada06 ), and this can be converted to M 5.6 10^ 14 h^ 1 M_ using cluster scaling relations, but with a large error bar.

sources.summary: The paper uses SDSS J1029 + 2623 as a multiply imaged lensed quasar sightline system, observed with MUSE and UVES to probe foreground absorption over 1.9-22.5 arcsec image separations.

quotes.quote: The fields SDSS J1029 + 2623 (J1029 hereafter) and SDSS J2222 + 2745 (J2222 hereafter) were observed with MUSE for 4.8 h and 4.4 h on target, respectively [ PID: 0102.A-0642(A), 0103.A-0554(A); P.I.: C. Grillo ] .

The left panel of Fig. fig:j1029 shows the MUSE image of the field J1029. This field contains three multiple images (A, B, C) of a quasar at \ = 2.1992 that is lensed by a galaxy cluster at \ = 0.588. The angular separation between the quasar images ranges from 1.9 to 22.5 arcsec. The MUSE spectra of the three quasar multiple images are shown in the right panel of Fig. fig:j1029 . In addition to the MUSE spectra, archival [ PID: 092.B-0512(A) ] UVES spectra are available for the multiple images A and B of the quasar J1029.

sources.summary: The paper uses Subaru spectroscopy of lensed images of SDSS J1029+2623 to study quasar outflow absorption, finding persistent sightline differences and time-variable C IV absorption.

quotes.quote: Using Subaru/HDS, we performed high-resolution ( R 36,000) spectroscopic observations of images A and B of the gravitationally lensed quasar SDSS J1029+2623 (at \ 2.197) whose image separation angle, 22 ^ .5, is the largest among those discovered so far. We confirm that the difference in absorption profiles in the images A and B discovered by mis13 remains unchanged since 2010, implying the difference is not due to time variability of the absorption profiles over the delay between the images, t 744 days, but rather due to differences along the sightlines. We also discovered time variation of C 4 absorption strength in both images A and B, due to change of ionization condition.

In this letter, we present results from new spectroscopic observations of SDSS J1029+2623 conducted 4 years later with the goal of conclusively determining the origin of the difference in the absorption features. We also examine the global and internal structure of the outflow.

gmw_00005350.name: SDSS J1326 + 4806
aliases_external: SDSS J1326+4806 BCG / lens candidate system center
gmw_00005350.overview: SDSS J1326 + 4806 is a massive early-type galaxy that serves as a brightest cluster galaxy [arXiv:1909.02010]. It acts as a gravitational lens, with two blue, point-like sources on either side forming a doubly lensed active galactic nucleus system [arXiv:1909.02010].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J1326 + 4806 as a high-probability strongly lensed AGN candidate, with a massive early-type BCG and two blue point-like sources arranged as a doubly lensed system.

quotes.quote: In addition, we identify a high-probability strongly-lensed AGN candidate --- SDSS J1326 + 4806.

The left panel in Figure fig:J1326 shows a colour cutout centered on the BCG of SDSS J1326 + 4806 made from gri imaging data from the Panoramic Survey Telescope and Rapid Response System (PanSTARRS) survey Chambers16 . The BCG, at R.A.=201.50006 ^ , Decl.=48.11208 ^ , is an SDSS spectroscopically-confirmed massive early-type galaxy at z=0.396 . Two blue, point-like sources, labeled as A and B, are located on either side of the BCG, consistent with the image configuration of a doubly lensed system.
Based on the analyses above, SDSS J1326 + 4806 has a very high probability of being a strongly-lensed AGN.

gmw_00005351.name: J104454+354055
gmw_00005351.overview: J104454+354055 is a twin radio galaxy consisting of a gravitationally bound dumb-bell pair of massive elliptical galaxies [arXiv:2205.04483]. The system hosts four extended, edge-darkened Fanaroff-Riley I radio jets that display a mirror-symmetric wiggly pattern [arXiv:2205.04483].

sources.summary: The paper presents J104454+354055 as the third known twin radio galaxy: a bound dumb-bell pair with two massive ellipticals, four extended FR I jets, and cleaner jet dynamics than earlier TRGs.

quotes.quote: The u GMRT observations reported here have revealed of a pair of bi-polar extended radio jets emanating from a dumb-bell system containing two gravitationally bound massive elliptical galaxies within a common stellar envelope. This newly discovered `Twin-Radio-Galaxy' (TRG), J104454+354055, is the third TRG found so far.

All four jets of the present TRG are extended on 100-kiloparsec scale and exhibit an edge-darkened radio morphology (FR I), as well as a mirror-symmetric wiggly pattern which is consistent with both host galaxies performing orbital motion about a common gravitational centre (the recent LoTSS/DR2 map shows a total size of 0.5 Mpc). Unlike the other two TRGs, there is no indication that the jets of the present TRG merge with each other early on, or become distorted due to a cross-wind. This goes to make this TRG a much neater exponent of cosmic tango, more amenable to modelling the jet dynamics in TRGs and for studying the impact of sideway collisions of relativistic plasma jets on their survivability e.g., Achterberg1988A&A...191..167A, Musoke2020MNRAS.494.5207M .

gmw_00005352.name: PKS 2149-158
gmw_00005352.overview: PKS 2149-158 is a Fanaroff-Riley class I dumbbell system composed of two elliptical radio galaxies sharing a common envelope [arXiv:0709.2652]. Located near the cluster Abell 2382, the system features interacting twin jets with a C-shaped bending and a radio extent of approximately 400 to 410 kiloparsecs [arXiv:0709.2652, arXiv:2205.04483].

sources.summary: The paper treats PKS 2149-158 as an FR I dumb-bell system of two elliptical radio galaxies with interacting twin jets, large oscillating morphology, and a 410 kpc radio extent.

quotes.quote: PKS 2149-158 (A and B) PKS 2149-158 (FR class I) is a double system composed of two nearly equally bright elliptical galaxies in a common envelope ("dumb-bell'' galaxy). The radio source was first mapped by Parma et al. (1991) at 1.4 GHz. Both galaxies of the dumb-bell system are radio-emitters, forming a double twin jet system like 3C 75 (Owen et al. 1985). The two radio cores, labelled A and B in Fig. , are separated in projection by 13 5 which corresponds to 15.8 kpc.

The radio morphology of PKS 2149-158, which shows regular large amplitude oscillations, is rather unusual and can be interpreted in terms of two distinct radio sources whose jets are strongly interacting. The true (three-dimensional) source structure is undoubtedly even more complex, because of the radio jets may very well bend along the line-of-sight. The maximum angular size of the radio source at 20 cm is about 5.8 ( 410 kpc). The magnetic field configuration, as traced by the 6 cm images, is initially transverse in the jets and becomes circumferential in the lobes.

sources.summary: The paper treats PKS 2149-158 as the second known twin radio galaxy, a 400 kpc source near Abell 2382 with separated hosts and bent bipolar jets.

quotes.quote: The second TRG, which was discovered 3 decades ago and has received much less attention, is the source PKS 2149-158 of size 400 kpc, located near the outskirts of the cluster Abell 2382 ( z = 0.062) Parma1991AJ....102.1960P, Guidetti2008A&A...483..699G .

Also, the apparent separation between their twin host galaxies is around 10 kpc (between 8 and 18 kpc) and the line-of-sight velocity difference between the paired host galaxies are quite similar: 430 \ km \ s^ 1 (3C 75) Lauer..2014ApJ...797...82L , 546 \ km \ s^ 1 (PKS 2149-158) Owen..1995AJ....109...14O, Smith..2004AJ....128.1558S , and 300 \ km \ s^ 1 (present TRG) (see, Table tab:spec ).

A similar structural complexity appears to plague the twin bi-polar jets of the second TRG PKS 2149-158, which exhibit a conspicuous C-shaped bending Guidetti2008A&A...483..699G , again indicating a strong dynamical pressure acting on the jets.

gmw_00005353.name: P3
gmw_00005353.overview: P3 is a bright early-type galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.159 [arXiv:astro-ph/0202080]. Although it appears optically quiet and normal [arXiv:astro-ph/0202080, arXiv:astro-ph/0107396], its broadband properties and X-ray emission indicate the presence of a moderately to heavily obscured active galactic nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0202080, arXiv:astro-ph/0107396].

sources.summary: The paper identifies P3 as a bright early-type, optically quiet X-ray source at z=0.159 whose broadband properties indicate a heavily obscured active nucleus.

quotes.quote: Here we report the results of what can be considered the first example of this class of objects: CXOUJ031238.9--765134, originally discovered by Chandra , and optically identified by Fiore et al. (2000) with an apparently normal early--type galaxy at z =0.159, usually known as `` FIORE P3 ''. The analysis of the broad-band energy distribution suggests the presence of a heavily obscured active nucleus.

In this paper we present a multiwavelength study of what can be considered the first example of these objects: the X--ray source CXOUJ 031238.9--765134, optically identified as a bright ( R =18.0), ``normal'' early-type galaxy at z =0.159 by Fiore et al. (2000, hereinafter F00) in their pilot study of serendipitous X--ray sources in two shallow Chandra fields. The field surrounding the radio loud quasar PKS 0312--76 has been observed by both Chandra and XMM-- Newton during their Calibration and Performance Phases. The Chandra source CXOUJ 031238.9--765134 (hereinafter P3, being the third source catalogued by F00 in the PKS field) was also detected by XMM with a number of photons sufficient for a rough spectral analysis. Here we present the X--ray spectral analysis combined with deep radio observations and near--infrared and optical spectroscopy.

sources.summary: The paper describes P3 as an unusual X-ray luminous apparently normal galaxy whose XMM spectrum is best explained as a moderately absorbed, obscured AGN rather than an exotic emission mechanism.

quotes.quote: For source P3, fixing the absorption to the galactic column provides a power law slope of 1.0 (comparable with the estimate of Fiore et al ) but this is an unacceptable fit (Reduced ^ 2 1.8). The free absorbing column added at the source redshift is formally, the highest value in this sample and the resulting power law returned is typical for an AGN, suggesting that the source is moderately absorbed, rather than demanding a more exotic emission mechanism. However neither the power law slope, nor the excess absorption are well-constrained, rendering it difficult to speculate on the emission mechanisms in detail.
An unusual X-ray luminous, but otherwise apparently normal galaxy (P3) seems to be most likely explained as an obscured AGN.

gmw_00005354.name: CDFS-6664
gmw_00005354.overview: CDFS-6664 is a low-mass Lyman-break galaxy at a redshift of z=3.797 [arXiv:2111.10507]. It features a young stellar population with low dust attenuation and a high star formation rate [arXiv:2111.10507].

sources.summary: The paper reports CDFS-6664 as a z=3.797 Lyman-break galaxy with significant offset LyC emission, low interloper likelihood, low mass, young stars, and high star formation.

quotes.quote: We report the detection of Lyman Continuum (LyC) emission from the galaxy, CDFS-6664, at z=3.797 in a sample of Lyman break galaxies with detected [ OIII ] emission lines. The LyC emission is detected with a significance 5 in the F336W band of the Deep UV Legacy Survey, corresponding to the 650-770 rest-frame. The light centroid of the LyC emission is offset from the galaxy center by about 0.2 ( 1.4 pkpc). The deep images at longer wavelengths show that the emission is unlikely provided by low-redshift interlopers.

The results of SED fitting show that CDFS-6664 is a low mass galaxy with ( M_ * /M_ ) =9.15 0.15 and star formation rate of 52.1 4.9 M_ yr^ 1 . The stellar population of this galaxy is quite young, with an average age of 0.05 0.01 Gyr. The dust attenuation for this galaxy is quite low, with E(B-V)_s=0.10 0.05 .

gmw_00005355.name: MRC 0600-399
gmw_00005355.overview: MRC 0600-399 is a wide-angle-tail radio galaxy located in the Abell 3376 cluster at a redshift of z=0.04559 [arXiv:2106.13049, arXiv:2401.05595]. The galaxy features sharply bent, two-sided jets that form a double-scythe structure, with spectral flattening that suggests the re-acceleration of relativistic electrons [arXiv:2106.13049, arXiv:2401.05595].

sources.summary: The paper presents MRC 0600-399 as a z=0.04559 radio galaxy in A3376 with sharply bent two-sided jets, a double-scythe structure, and spectral-index behavior suggesting re-acceleration.

quotes.quote: MRC 0600-399 was observed at 1.28 GHz with MeerKAT [ 18,19 ] to investigate its morphology (Fig. 1). In the center of A3376, there are two prominent radio galaxies - MRC 0600-399 ( z=0.04559 ) and galaxy B ( z=0.0480 )( Fig. 1. (b)) MRC 0600-399 also shows two-sided bent jets, but the collimated structures continue to the east direction over 100 kpc and 50 kpc for the northern and southern jets, respectively, beyond the bend points.

We confirmed that there is no other radio galaxy overlapping with MRC 0600-399 and causing the ``double-scythe'' structure, thus, we confirm its association with the jets of MRC 0600-399. Spectral index map and 1-dimensional profiles of the spectral index and flux density ( F_ ^ ) along the bent jets are shown in Fig.2 (a). The spectral index values decrease gradually in N1 and S1 starting from MRC 0600-399. The spectral index decrement across 49 kpc (7 open circles) is 0.76 for N1, and that across 25 kpc (4 open circles) is 0.44 for S1. The trend then drastically changes and these values become fairly constant in N2 and S2, suggesting re-acceleration of relativistic electrons.

sources.summary: The paper cites MRC 0600-399 as a wide-angle-tail radio galaxy whose northern jet shows spectral flattening where bent plasma may undergo magnetic-reconnection particle acceleration.

quotes.quote: As supporting evidences, they cite the detection of spectral flattening near the bouncing point of (i) the back-flowing synchrotron plasma of the northern primary lobe of the XRG PKS 2014-55 (see Fig. \ 5 in Cotton et al. \ 2020), and (ii) in the northern jet of the Wide-Angle-Tail (WAT) radio galaxy MRC 0600 399 (termed ``double-scythe"), as seen in its MeerKAT map at 1.28 GHz (Fig. \ 1 of Chibueze et al. \ 2021). In both these radio galaxies, localised regions of enhanced radio emission, accompanied by spectral flattening, are actually observed near the region where a powerful collimated flow of synchrotron plasma (of a jet or backflow) undergoes a sharp bending or rebound.

Alternatively, as noted in section 4.1, when plasma flows are diverted via interaction with clumps of matter, or with significant external magnetic fields, the increased tension of the field lines can lead to magnetic reconnection between the jet and the compressed magnetic layer, as inferred from the MeerKAT observations of the WAT MRC 0600-399 in the cluster Abell 3376 (Chibueze et al. \ 2021). Their magnetohydrodynamical simulations show that such a process can lead to efficient relativistic particle acceleration and their transport downstream (see also, Giri et al. \ 2022).

gmw_00005356.name: Source 3
gmw_00005356.overview: Source 3 is a high-redshift emission line galaxy with a measured grism redshift of 3.153 [arXiv:1501.05312]. It is a photometric redshift neighbor to a nearby nebula, but its ionizing output is considered insufficient to power the nebula's emission [arXiv:1501.05312].

sources.summary: The paper identifies Source 3 as a likely galaxy member near the nebula, with weak [OII] emission, a grism redshift consistent with the nebula, and insufficient ionizing output.

quotes.quote: We find that one of the \ photometric redshift neighbors (Source 3) shows a weak [ OII ] detection (Figure fig:source3 ). This corresponds to a measured grism redshift of z_ grism =3.153_ 0.004 ^ +0.019 (Table tab:galmember ), consistent with the photometric redshift ( z_ phot =3.206^ +0.065 _ 0.058 ) and with the nebula redshift measured from the \ emission line ( z_ Ly =3.157 ). Neither the extended nebula itself nor any of the other galaxy members, which are all much fainter in F160W continuum magnitude than Source 3, show a line detection in either the G102 or G141 grism spectroscopy.

The Energetics: Revisited nil06 estimated that even if Source 3, the brightest neighbor galaxy, was at the nebula redshift, it was unlikely to be able to power the \ emission unless highly collimated in the direction of the nebula.

gmw_00005357.name: Source 6
gmw_00005357.overview: Source 6 is a diffuse mid-infrared source first identified in Spitzer IRAC and MIPS data [arXiv:1501.05312]. Although previously suggested to be an obscured starburst, its mid-infrared colors place it within active galactic nuclei selection windows, indicating it is likely an obscured active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1501.05312].

sources.summary: The paper reanalyzes Source 6 as a diffuse mid-infrared source whose colors put it in AGN selection windows, making it a likely obscured AGN rather than a starburst galaxy.

quotes.quote: Source 6 - The IRAC/MIPS Source We take a second look at Source 6, the mid-infrared source first identified in the Spitzer IRAC and MIPS data. We plot the full SED including the original GOODS-S imaging and Spitzer data, as well as the new HST/WFC3-IR imaging and measurements from Herschel/PACS and SPIRE (Figure fig:source6 ). The source shows diffuse emission in the WFC3-IR imaging, but it is below the detection limit in the combined WFC3 detection image used in skelton14 ; therefore, we measure the photometry in these bands using a 2.4 \ radius aperture at the position of Source 6, as determined from the IRAC 3.6 and 4.5 m imaging.

While nil06 suggest Source 6 is an obscured starburst at an approximate redshift of z 5.5 based on its MIR colors, the MIR color measurements taken from the GOODS-Herschel catalog place Source 6 elsewhere in the ivison04 color space, much higher in S_ 24.0 /S_ 8.0 and therefore much closer to the Mrk231 (i.e., AGN) locus.

These measurements place Source 6 solidly within the AGN color selection window of lac04,lac07 , and within the more recent IRAC power-law AGN selection box of don12 . From the mid-infrared colors, Source 6 is therefore very likely an obscured AGN and not a starburst galaxy.

gmw_00005358.overview: 0823 500 is classified as a galaxy with an unknown redshift [arXiv:2007.10474]. Observations at 8.4 GHz reveal a double-sided structure featuring a central jet core, a counterjet emission gap, and a largely misaligned jet orientation [arXiv:2007.10474].

sources.summary: The paper describes 0823 500 as a TANAMI source classified as a galaxy, with a double-sided 8.4 GHz structure, central jet core, counterjet gap, and misaligned jet orientation.

quotes.quote: 0823 500 This source with unknown redshift is included in the PKS catalog 1990PKS...C......0W , but not in the V eron-V eron AGN catalog 2010A&A...518A..10V , most likely due to its low galactic latitude ( b=-7.06^ ). It is unclassified in the PKS catalog as well as in an optical identification of radio sources from the AT20G survey AT20G J082526 501039 2011MNRAS.417.2651M . It is classified as a galaxy on the http://ned.ipac.caltech.edu/ NASA/IPAC Extragalactic Database (NED) . This source is a relatively recent addition to the TANAMI sample, and was first observed in 2013.

The TANAMI 8.4 GHz image (see Fig. fig:wallpaper ) shows a double-sided structure, with a core, jet and counterjet extending along the NE-SW direction. It is not yet possible to identify the core component by means of a spectral index map, therefore we consider the brightest, compact central component to be the jet core. There is a gap of emission on the counter-jet side that extends for 4 mas from the core.
