Figure fig:optical_spectrum displays their optical spectra, obtained with the Subaru/Faint Object Camera and Spectrograph (FOCAS; 2002PASJ...54..819K ) or the Gran Telescopio Canarias (GTC)/Optical System for Imaging and low-Intermediate-Resolution Integrated Spectroscopy (OSIRIS; 2000SPIE.4008..623C ) 2016ApJ...828...26M, 2018ApJS..237....5M, 2019ApJ...883..183M . We note that two of the four candidates, J1205 0000 and J1146 0154, have broad absorption line (BAL) features.

J1146 0154: this is an i band dropout source and is not detected in the near-IR bands (the flux upper limits are given in Figure fig:SED_results ), so we fitted the SED models to the HSC y band flux and the decomposed W1, W2 band fluxes. It is reasonably fitted by both the quasar plus extinction model with E(B-V) = 0.162^ +0.020 _ 0.022 and the quasar plus galaxy model. Thus, it could be a red quasar. However, we noticed that the WISE flux decomposition is uncertain for this object.

gmw_00005560.name: J023858.09 031845.4
gmw_00005560.overview: J023858.09 031845.4 is a high-redshift red quasar [arXiv:2007.08685]. Spectral energy distribution fitting favors a quasar plus dust extinction model over a luminous host-galaxy contribution, and the source appears point-like on optical imaging [arXiv:2007.08685].

sources.summary: J0238 0318 is classified as a red quasar after SED fitting favors dust extinction over a luminous host-galaxy contribution, despite similar formal fits.

quotes.quote: J0238 0318: this is an i band dropout source and is not observed in the near-IR bands. We fitted the SED models to the HSC y band flux and the AllWISE W1, W2 band fluxes. The quasar plus extinction model with E(B-V) = 0.127^ +0.018 _ 0.021 and the quasar plus galaxy model with a luminous host galaxy ( M_ 1450 = -23.0 mag) give similarly good fits.

However, the ^2 is smaller in the former case, and the host galaxy in the latter case becomes very luminous relative to the characteristic magnitude of the galaxy luminosity function at that redshift ( M^*_ 1450 20.9 mag; 2018PASJ...70S..10O ). The source is not extended on the HSC image, and has a typical spectrum of a high- z quasar in the rest-UV. We thus regard that the quasar plus extinction model is more reasonable, and that this object is a red quasar.

gmw_00005561.name: galaxy 2
gmw_00005561.overview: Galaxy 2 features a radio tail that extends up to 40 kpc from its disk [arXiv:2305.19941]. However, this tail appears to connect with another galaxy to the north-west, suggesting the emission may be blended due to insufficient image resolution [arXiv:2305.19941].

sources.summary: Galaxy 2 has a radio tail complicated by apparent connection to another galaxy; the authors treat its model results as inconclusive and likely blended.

quotes.quote: For galaxy 2 we adopt special considerations because the radio tail connects with another galaxy located toward the north-west (Figure sample_img ). The current image resolution does not allow us to discriminate if the connection is real or just a blend of multiple sources. Regardless, we decide to sample its tail up to 40 kpc from the disk.

For galaxies 2 and 5 results are inconclusive because either the flux density or the spectral index profiles are not decreasing monotonically. In both cases, we conclude that this is due to the fact that the radio tails are not real and/or induced by the RPS. For galaxy 2 , we suggest that the putative radio tail is instead the result from the emission from two galaxies that is blended due to the insufficient resolution of our images.

gmw_00005562.name: galaxy 7
gmw_00005562.overview: Galaxy 7 is a spiral galaxy that exhibits consistent model velocities across three different tracers [arXiv:2305.19941]. It also displays a potential tail feature beyond 30 kpc, which may be caused by fresh cosmic-ray electrons or changing radiative losses [arXiv:2305.19941].

sources.summary: Galaxy 7 has model velocities consistent across tracers and a possible tail feature beyond 30 kpc, perhaps from fresh cosmic-ray electrons or changing losses.

quotes.quote: For galaxies 1 , 3 , and 7 the velocities derived from the three tracers are consistent within 2 ; For galaxies 4 and 6 the results are less clear.

In our sample, only galaxy 7 shows a potential signature of this process for D>30 kpc, beyond which the observations slightly diverge from the model. This feature could be signature of either presence of `fresh' CRe produced in-situ by extraplanar star formation, or that the CRe losses may be dominated by adiabatic losses instead of synchrotron/IC (e.g., due to increasingly longer radiative times as consequence of a decrease of the magnetic field with the distance).

gmw_00005563.name: VGS31b
gmw_00005563.overview: VGS31b is a highly inclined, starburst Markarian ring galaxy located along a void filament [arXiv:1307.2905]. It exhibits a highly disturbed structure, featuring a central early-type disk, an inner bar, a ring-like structure, and a one-sided tail curved toward the northeast [arXiv:1307.2905].

sources.summary: The paper studies VGS31b as a disturbed, highly inclined ring galaxy in a void filament, finding low ring metallicity and arguing that cold gas accretion best explains its structure.

quotes.quote: VGS31b is a highly-inclined ring galaxy found along a filament in a void Kreckel12 . Detailed photometry, by using u, g, , i, z SDSS images, has shown that the overall morphology of VGS31b is very tricky, due to i) the presence of a highly inclined ( 72^ ) ring-like structure, which reaches the galaxy center tracing a ``spiral-like'' pattern, ii) a one sided tail towards North-East and iii) a bar in the central regions Beygu13 . Such structure is reasonably the result of a ``second event'' in the evolution history of this galaxy, which could be a gravitational interaction with a companion galaxy or with the environment.

VGS31b is the most disturbed object of this small group; it is at a distance of about 84 Mpc, based on H_ 0 = 75 \ km \ s^ 1 \ Mpc^ 1 and has a heliocentric radial velocity of V = 6218 \ km \ s^ 1 , which implies that 1 arcsec = 0.4 kpc. VGS31b is a starburst Markarian galaxy (MRK1477) with a one sided tail, curved toward north-east, kinematically connected to the central early-type disk galaxy (HG), a ring-like structure around the HG and a fast rotating inner structure with streaming motions characteristic of a bar. Both the tail and the ring are not detected in H or UV, but are clearly visible in optical and HI.
Given all the evidence shown above, we can conclude that the cold accretion of gas by the filament could well account for the main properties of VGS31b.

gmw_00005564.name: NGC 1353
gmw_00005564.overview: NGC 1353 is a disk galaxy that features a central pseudobulge [arXiv:astro-ph/0507525]. This central structure is a nuclear disk with a similar flattening and orientation to the galaxy's outer disk [arXiv:astro-ph/0507525].

sources.summary: The review uses NGC 1353 as a pseudobulge example, describing its central disk, disk-like flattening and orientation, low Sersic index, and nuclear disk contribution to central brightness.

quotes.quote: One example among many, NGC 1353, is shown in Figure 3. The images show, as Carollo et al. (1997, 1998) concluded, that the central structure in NGC 1353 is a disk with similar flattening and orientation as the outer disk. To make this quantitative, KK04 measured the surface brightness, ellipticity, and position angle profiles (plots in Figure 3). The apparent flattening at 2^ 4^ is the same as that of the main disk at large radii.

So the part of the galaxy shown in the top-right panel really is a disk. The brightness profile shows that this nuclear disk is responsible for much of the central rise in surface brightness above the inward extrapolation of an exponential fitted to the outer disk. Presented only with the brightness profile or with the bottom two panels of images, we would identify the central rise in surface brightness as a bulge. Given Figure 3, we identify it as a pseudobulge.

gmw_00005565.name: host galaxy of AT 2020mot
gmw_00005565.overview: The host galaxy of AT 2020mot, also identified as WISEA J003113.52 +850031.8 [arXiv:2305.03767], is tentatively classified as an E+A galaxy [arXiv:2208.14465]. This post-starburst system [arXiv:2305.03767] exhibits a low star-formation rate [arXiv:2208.14465] and hosted a tidal disruption event [arXiv:2305.03767].

sources.summary: The paper characterizes the AT 2020mot host as a low-star-formation E+A galaxy, measuring host flux, effective radius, Sersic profile, absolute magnitude, and an upper-limit black-hole mass.

quotes.quote: The spectrum of Fig. S5 shows no strong emission lines, consistent with the tentative classification of the galaxy as an E+A galaxy . In particular, we do not detect H _ , but as discussed in subsection Radio Observations , this is consistent with the estimated low star-formation rate.

We fitted a galaxy template to the summed R+i band image. The best-fit effective radius of the galaxy was 0.67", corresponding to 0.87 kpc at z=0.07. Therefore, it is safe to assume that all of the galaxy flux is contained within aperture of 4.5" that was used for the analysis of the polarimetric data.

The best-fit Sersic value was 1.6, but with large errorbars due to short exposure times. Therefore, we could not determine if the host is a disk or an elliptical galaxy. However, the rather small residuals from the elliptic profile fit indicate that an edge-on spiral is unlikely.

sources.summary: The paper identifies WISEA J003113.52 +850031.8 as AT 2020mot’s host, analyzes its infrared photometry, and links host properties to a post-starburst TDE environment.

quotes.quote: AT 2020mot was discovered by the Zwicky Transient Facility (ZTF) on MJD 59014.39 (June 14 2020) as a nuclear transient 2020TNSTR1791....1F , later classified by the Global Supernova Project as a TDE 2020TNSCR2478....1H given its central location to the host galaxy at (RA, Dec) = (00:31:13.57, +85:00:31.9) and broad He II emission feature. Hammerstein2023 include AT 2020mot in their sample of 30 TDEs, classifying it as a ``TDE-H+He" from observed broad H and He II. Liodakis2022 also report polarized emission from AT 2020mot that is the highest measured for a TDE without a jet, with radio upper-limits of 27 Jy at 15 GHz.

WISE photometry The host galaxy of AT 2020mot, WISEA J003113.52 +850031.8, was first observed by WISE in its NEOWISE survey in 2010, and its observations resumed in 2013 when the survey was reactivated as NEOWISE-R Mainzer2011 . We collected W1 ( 3.4 m) and W2 ( 4.6 m) data from the Infrared Sci ence Archive https://irsa.ipac.caltech.edu , resulting in 20 epochs of MIR photo metry each separated by six months, five of which were taken after the TDE flare. WISE data was parsed for detections with good-quality frames ( qi fact > 1 ), no contamination and confusion ( cc flag = 0 ) and with magnitude errors <0.15 mag; these requirements filtered out 10 of observations across all epochs.

gmw_00005566.name: HIPASS J1337-39
gmw_00005566.overview: HIPASS J1337-39 is a gas-rich dwarf irregular galaxy [arXiv:1408.2609]. The galaxy has active ongoing star formation [arXiv:1408.2609] and is morphologically dominated by H II emission knots [arXiv:1408.2609].

sources.summary: The paper treats HIPASS J1337-39 as a gas-rich, star-forming dwarf irregular, marginally detected in H band and showing an unusually high near-infrared H I mass-to-light ratio.

quotes.quote: HIPASS J1337-39 (LEDA 592761) HIPASS J1337-39 is a gas rich dIrr and has active ongoing star formation Grossi2007 . We are only able to get a marginal detection of the galaxy with our detection limit of 22.8 mag arcsec ^ 2 . Employing the same analysis as in the previous section using the total apparent magnitude of K _s 16.61 from , we derive a K _s band mass-to-light ratio of 17.4 ( M _ HI, /L_ ,Ks ), which is significantly higher than the corresponding mass-to-light ratio for the B band, 2.1 ( M _ HI, /L_ ,B ) Grossi2007 . Since the NIR flux contribution of young massive stars and associated gas continuum can typically contribute only 20-40 , the large difference in the B band and K _s band mass-to-light ratio must be at least partially attributable to the active ongoing star formation. According to figure 4 in , the galaxy has a rather high surface brightness in the B band and is morphological dominated by H ii emission knots.

gmw_00005567.name: HIPASS J1348-37
gmw_00005567.overview: HIPASS J1348-37 is a morphologically diffuse, low-surface-brightness dwarf irregular galaxy [arXiv:1408.2609]. It has an extremely low surface brightness and a major axis spanning more than 30 arcseconds in length [arXiv:1408.2609].

sources.summary: The paper barely detects HIPASS J1348-37, describing it as a diffuse low-surface-brightness dwarf irregular and arguing prior photometry likely measured a background galaxy.

quotes.quote: HIPASS J1348-37 Despite our image limiting surface brightness of 23.1 mag arcsec ^ 2 , we were just barely able to detect this galaxy that was found in the blind H i survey of the southern sky with the Parkes Telescope Banks1999 . Furthermore, the coordinates used in our observation were slightly incorrect (note they are correct in Table sample ), and so the location of the galaxy is off center (see Figure results3 ). presented a HST image of the galaxy (in their Figure 3) and derived a TRGB-based distance of 5.75 0.66 Mpc. The HST image shows a morphologically diffuse, low surface brightness dIrr with a major axis greater than 30 arcsec in length. This extremely low surface brightness dwarf galaxy appears as a relatively large object in the field of view compared to a point-source-like high surface brightness background galaxy located approximately 30 arcsec to the northwest.

gmw_00005568.name: HIPASS J1351-47
gmw_00005568.overview: HIPASS J1351-47 is a low surface brightness dwarf irregular galaxy [arXiv:1408.2609]. It has a low estimated stellar mass of approximately 10 to the power of 6.0 to 6.5 solar masses and a K-band H I mass-to-light ratio of 4.0 [arXiv:1408.2609].

sources.summary: The paper reports HIPASS J1351-47 was not unambiguously detected, derives a K-band H I mass-to-light ratio, and places its stellar mass below the survey detection threshold.

quotes.quote: HIPASS J1351-47 (LEDA 3097113) The estimated limiting surface brightness of 23.5 mag arcsec ^ 2 was insufficient to unambiguously detect this galaxy, mainly due to crowding from a significant number of galactic foreground stars (See Fig. results3 ). Using the total apparent K band magnitude K _s = 15.8 from and the HIPASS H i mass of 7.4 ( _ 10 ( M _ ) ) Koribalski2004 , we derive a K _ s band H i mass-to-light-ratio of M _ HI, /L_ ,Ks = 4.0. Although the H i mass-to-light ratio is not as extreme as for KK195 or HIPASS J1337-39 these galaxies do share a low stellar mass estimate, _ 10 ( M _ * / M _ ) = 6.0-6.5 , as determined through the K _s surface brightness photometery of . These mass estimates are over an order of magnitude lower than what our surface brightness limits could detect.

gmw_00005569.name: 2PBC J2010.3 2522
gmw_00005569.overview: The active galaxy 2PBC J2010.3 2522 is classified as a type 1 quasar [arXiv:1702.01629] and a flat-spectrum radio quasar [arXiv:2106.00100] at a redshift of approximately 0.8 [arXiv:1702.01629, arXiv:2106.00100]. It is associated with a gamma-ray emitter [arXiv:2106.00100] and has exhibited multiple flaring episodes [arXiv:2106.00100].

sources.summary: The paper associates PMN J2010 2524 with a transient 1FLT source, notes repeated temporal-bin detections, and contrasts its high Compton dominance with Mrk 1501.

quotes.quote: The source 1FLT J2010 2523 is associated with a Bayesian probability of 0.99 with PMN J2010 2524, a well-known FSRQ ( z= 0.825 , massaro20155th ). It is also reported in the Swift-BAT 105-Month Hard X-ray Survey as SWIFT J2010.6 2521 and as a ray emitter by 2019ApJ...881..154P . We detected the source in several temporal bins. The first detection was in TBIN 6 (2009 February 3 to 2009 March 5) followed by a detection in TBINs 68 and 68.5 (2014 April 5 to 2014 May 20) and then from TBIN 72 to TBIN 73.5 (2014 August 5 to 2014 October 20).

The flaring activity of the source is quite evident in the light curve reported in Appendix sec:light . In contrast to Mrk 1501, the SED of PMN J2010 2524 has a CD_ greater then 1, which is fairly common among FSRQs.

sources.summary: The paper classifies 2PBC J2010.3 2522 as a type 1 QSO at redshift about 0.8 and notes its possible association with a Fermi transient and radio source.

quotes.quote: For the object 2PBC J2010.3 2522 that we classified as type 1 QSO at z 0.8, we found in the literature that this object could be associated with a Fermi transient J2007 2518 that is also spatially coincident with a radio source (Kocevski et al. 2014). Strader et al. (2014) acquired an optical spectrum of this same source and obtained results equivalent to ours. We thus confirm the QSO nature of this object and its possible association to the Fermi transient J2007 2518.

gmw_00005570.name: 2PBC J1555.0 6225
gmw_00005570.overview: The galaxy 2PBC J1555.0 6225 is classified as a starburst galaxy based on its optical spectrum [arXiv:1702.01629]. However, its WISE colors and X-ray emission suggest the presence of an elusive, hidden active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1702.01629].

sources.summary: The paper classifies 2PBC J1555.0 6225 as a starburst galaxy but argues WISE colors suggest an elusive AGN whose optical spectrum shows mostly starburst emission.

quotes.quote: Among our AGN sample, 20 objects are intermediate type Sy 1.2-1.5 and 5 are Sy 1.9, whereas of the 18 type 2 AGNs, 14 are Seyfert 2 galaxies, one is a starburst galaxy (2PBC J1555.0 6225, Fig. 8), one is a LINER (2PBC J2238.9+4050, Fig. 9) and one is a X-ray bright optically normal galaxy (XBONG, Comastri et al. 2002) which displays absorption features only (2PBC J1332.1 7751, Fig. 10). We found that the object classified as Starburst galaxy in our sample, 2PBC J1555.0 6225, has WISE magnitudes and colors that indicate an AGN nature according to the criteria of Secrest et al. (2015): (W1 - W2) = 0.56, (W2 - W3) = 2.34 > 2. This could be an elusive AGN as in Smith et al. (2014) and we are probably only seeing the starburst contribution in its optical spectrum, with the X-ray emission detected from the hidden nuclear activity.

gmw_00005571.name: 2PBC J1332.1 7751
gmw_00005571.overview: 2PBC J1332.1 7751 is classified as an X-ray bright optically normal galaxy [arXiv:1702.01629]. It displays absorption-only optical features and is likely an active galactic nucleus hidden by dust obscuration [arXiv:1702.01629].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 2PBC J1332.1 7751 as an XBONG with absorption-only optical features, X-ray obscuration near Compton-thick levels, and a likely dust-hidden AGN appearance.

quotes.quote: Among our AGN sample, 20 objects are intermediate type Sy 1.2-1.5 and 5 are Sy 1.9, whereas of the 18 type 2 AGNs, 14 are Seyfert 2 galaxies, one is a starburst galaxy (2PBC J1555.0 6225, Fig. 8), one is a LINER (2PBC J2238.9+4050, Fig. 9) and one is a X-ray bright optically normal galaxy (XBONG, Comastri et al. 2002) which displays absorption features only (2PBC J1332.1 7751, Fig. 10). For the object 2PBC J1332.1 7751, classsified as XBONG, Davies et al. (2015) reported a column density in X-ray of Log( N_ H ) = 23.8 cm^ 2 , that is almost in the limit to be a Compton Thick object (with Log( N_ H ) 24 cm^ 2 ) and they classified it as Seyfert galaxy, but no optical classification is considered for this object in that work. Thus, 2PBC J1332.1 7751 is a peculiar object that is absorbed in X-ray and optically obscured. Our classification as XBONG is then supported by the information from Davies et al. (2015): the AGN optical appearence is likely hidden due to dust obscuration revealed in X-ray spectrum.

gmw_00005572.name: SDSS J1026 0042
gmw_00005572.overview: SDSS J1026 0042 is a low-luminosity active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2004.01501]. High-resolution radio observations reveal a compact, resolved milliarcsecond-scale core that exhibits a positional offset from its optical counterpart [arXiv:2004.01501].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J1026 0042 as the only firmly EVN-detected LLAGN in its sample, with a compact mas-scale AGN core, radio-optical offset, and nonthermal radio power.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: right ascension _ EVN = 10^ h 26^ m 40.4381^ s and declination _ EVN = -00^ 42^ 06.496^

quotes.quote: From the five targeted LLAGN sources, only SDSS J1026 0042 was firmly detected with the EVN. One source component was found at both observing frequencies, 1.7 and 5 GHz. The VLBI positions (the coordinates corresponding to the origin of the maps in Figure  fig:J1026 ) agree with each other within the uncertainty of about 1 mas. At both frequencies, the component is seen as a compact source.

The single detected component in SDSS J1026 0042 is resolved at both frequencies as the fitted circular Gaussian diameters, 0.79 0.06 mas and 0.065 0.007 mas (FWHM) at 1.7 and 5 GHz, respectively, are larger than the minimum resolvable sizes. There is a radio--optical positional difference of 15 mas in right ascension and 10  mas in declination (Figure fig:J1026 ). Given that the EVN positions are accurate to within 1 mas, and for the optical position the uncertainties are 5  mas and 8  mas, the difference is significant.

We also derived the K corrected monochromatic radio power values for the compact VLBI-detected components of SDSS J1026 0042: P_ 1.7GHz = (7.08 0.48) 10 ^ 23  W Hz ^ 1 and P_ 5GHz = (1.10 0.13) 10 ^ 23  W Hz ^ 1 .

gmw_00005573.name: SDSS J153715.74+582933.9
gmw_00005573.overview: SDSS J153715.74+582933.9 is a broad-absorption line (BAL) quasar [arXiv:1806.08673]. Its Si IV and C IV BALs contain multiple narrow absorption line systems that exhibit coordinated weakening, which is attributed to ionization changes driven by continuum variations [arXiv:1806.08673].

sources.summary: The paper presents SDSS J153715.74+582933.9 as a BAL quasar whose Si IV and C IV absorption variability is attributed to ionization changes and saturation effects.

quotes.quote: First, we explore a typical case showing Phenomenon I, quasar SDSS J153715.74+582933.9 (hereafter J1537+5829). We identify four narrow absorption line (NAL) systems within its Si IV BAL and two additional NAL systems within its C IV BAL, and confirm their coordinated weakening. Combining with the obvious strengthening of the ionizing continuum, we attribute the BAL variability in J1537+5829 to the ionization changes caused by the continuum variations.

As a BAL quasar, J1537+5829 has been studied in some systematic BAL studies Trump2006,Gibson2009a,Scaringi2009,Allen2011,Bruni2014,Filiz2014,He2015, He2017 . The MJD 52347 spectrum of J1537+5829 has a balnicity index (BI; see the definition in Weymann1991 ) of 455.2 and 429.2 km s^ 1 for the Si IV and C IV BALs (these estimations are taken from Allen2011 ), respectively. As Type N BALs, both the Si IV and C IV BALs in J1537+5829 actually contain multiple NAL systems, but they show different trough profiles: Si IV BAL shows multiple-absorption troughs, while the C IV BAL has a P-Cygni/trough-like shape.

gmw_00005574.name: B2 1420+32
gmw_00005574.overview: B2 1420+32 is a blazar located at a redshift of approximately 0.682 [arXiv:2012.11380, arXiv:2102.03516]. It is a changing-look active galactic nucleus that has transitioned multiple times between flat-spectrum radio quasar and BL Lacertae spectral states [arXiv:2402.17099, arXiv:2103.08707].

sources.summary: The paper identifies QSO B1420+326 as a redshift 0.682 FSRQ and models its enhanced state, including MAGIC VHE detection, polarization rotation, and a new superluminal radio knot.

quotes.quote: QSO B1420+326 , also known as OQ 334, is a blazar located at redshift of 0.682 hw10 . Based on its radio spectrum it has been classified as a Flat Spectrum Radio Quasar FSRQ; he07 .

During the enhanced state the flux of both SED components drastically increased and the peaks were shifted to higher energies. Follow up observations with the MAGIC telescopes led to the detection of very-high-energy gamma-ray emission from this source, making it one of only a handful of FSRQs known in this energy range. Modeling allows us to constrain the evolution of the magnetic field and electron energy distribution in the emission region.

The gamma-ray flare was accompanied by a rotation of the optical polarization vector during a low polarization state. Also, a new, superluminal radio knot contemporaneously appeared in the radio image of the jet. The optical spectroscopy shows a prominent FeII bump with flux evolving together with the continuum emission and a MgII line with varying equivalent width.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes OQ 334 as an FSRQ blazar with renewed gamma-ray flaring in 2018 and 2020, rapid variability, separated gamma-ray/radio emission regions, and changing jet power.

quotes.quote: The blazar OQ 334 displayed a ray flare in 2018, after being in the long quiescent ray state since 2008. Subsequent to the flare, the source was in a higher ray flux state and again flared in 2020. We present here the first spectral and timing analysis of the source at its various flaring states. During the higher ray state, we found four major peaks identified as P1, P2, P3 and P4. From timing analysis we found rise and decay time of the order of hours with the fastest variability time of 9.01 0.78 hr.

The blazar OQ 334 is classified as a flat spectrum quasar (FSRQ) in 4FGL ( 2020ApJS..247...33A ) and it is at a redshift z = 0.6819 ( Hewett_2010 ). It has been in the low ray brightness state since 2008. After a decade it flared in the ray band in 2018 ( Atel_12277 ).

sources.summary: The paper studies OQ 334 as a changing-look blazar, dividing Fermi LAT data into FSRQ, transition, and BL Lac epochs and modeling its gamma-ray and multiwavelength SEDs.

quotes.quote: We explore the light curve and spectral energy distribution (SED) of the CL blazar OQ 334 as obtained by Fermi LAT. By examining the variability of the equivalent width (EW), we categorise the Fermi LAT light curves of OQ 334 during the epoch of MJD 54628-58677 into seven distinct epochs, including the flat spectrum radio quasar (FSRQ) state, the transition state, and the BL Lac state. We obtained both a Fermi LAT SED and a multi-wavelength SED for each of these distinct epochs. The source exhibits a transformation from a quiescent state to a highly active state, as evidenced by the variability of the EW.

OQ 334, also known by other names such as QSO B1420+326 2022icrc.confE.775D , B2 1420+32 2021ApJ...913..146M , and FSRQ QSO B1420+326 2021A&A...647A.163M , is classified as a FSRQ and falls under the category of blazars in the 4FGL catalogue 2020ApJS..247...33A . It has a redshift value of 0.6819 2010MNRAS.405.2302H . Since the launch of Fermi Gamma-Ray Space Telescope Large Area Telescope ( Fermi LAT) in 2008, OQ 334 has been observed in ray at relatively low brightness 2021MNRAS.502.5245P . However, in 2018, a ray flare was detected 2018ATel12277....1C . Two years later, another flare was observed by Fermi LAT 2020ATel13382....1C,2022ATel15793....1K . found significant evidence of the changing nature of OQ 334 (also known as B2 1420+32).

sources.summary: The paper monitors B2 1420+32 during 2018-2020 outbursts, finding extreme optical and gamma-ray variability and repeated transitions between FSRQ and BL Lac spectral states.

quotes.quote: We present multi-wavelength photometric and spectroscopic monitoring observations of the blazar, B2 1420+32, focusing on its outbursts in 2018-2020. Multi-epoch spectra show that the blazar exhibited large scale spectral variability in both its continuum and line emission, accompanied by dramatic gamma-ray and optical variability by factors of up to 40 and 15, respectively, on week to month timescales. Over the last decade, the gamma-ray and optical fluxes increased by factors of 1500 and 100, respectively.

Following a series of flares starting in 2018, it transitioned between BL Lac and FSRQ states multiple times, with the emergence of a strong Fe pseudo continuum. Two spectra also contain components that can be modeled as single-temperature black bodies of 12,000 and 5,200 K. Such a collection of ``changing look'' features has never been observed previously in a blazar.

gmw_00005575.name: candidate H
gmw_00005575.overview: Candidate H is an extended, underluminous blue galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.584 [arXiv:astro-ph/0609444]. It is identified as the likely host galaxy of GRB 050223 and exhibits strong star formation dominated by an A-star stellar population with large extinction [arXiv:astro-ph/0609444].

sources.summary: The paper identifies candidate H as the likely GRB 050223 host: an extended z=0.584, underluminous blue star-forming galaxy with strong extinction and an A-star dominated population.

quotes.quote: Object H is extended (roughly 1 5 in radius) and morphologically consistent with a galaxy. Its coordinates (J2000) are = 18^ h 05^ m 32 992 , = -62 28 18 81 ( 0 16, which takes into account the 2MASS catalog uncertainty of 0 1), only 1 8 apart from the nominal XRT afterglow position, and 2 7 from that of XMM . There is no other conspicuous object within the error circles down to our 3 limiting magnitudes of R 26 and K_ s 21 . We note that with these limits a typical LGRB HG like those in the sample of Le Floc'h et al. ( ) would be detectable up to a redshift of 2.5. Hence, although at this point we cannot completely discard a high redshift galaxy, H is a good HG candidate for GRB 050223.

Combining the accurate X-ray positioning of LGRB afterglows given by Swift XRT, and very deep VLT optical and NIR images and optical spectra, we identified the likely HG of the dark GRB 050223. It is a 0.4 L^* , blue galaxy at z = 0.584 , with strong SF ( SFR > 7 M_ yr ^ 1 , SSFR > 17.5 M_ yr ^ 1 L^ *-1 ), a stellar population dominated by A0V stars with an age of 40 Myr--1.5 Gyr, and a large extinction ( A_ V > 2 mag). This galaxy has some typical properties of LGRB HGs.

gmw_00005576.name: RE J2248-511
gmw_00005576.overview: RE J2248-511 is a local, extreme-ultraviolet-selected broad-line Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:1311.3075]. The galaxy is highly variable in both the optical and soft X-ray bands, exhibiting a strongly variable soft X-ray excess on year timescales [arXiv:1311.3075].

sources.summary: The paper studies RE J2248-511 as a variable ultrasoft X-ray Seyfert/quasar-like AGN, using multi-epoch optical-X-ray SEDs to interpret its soft excess and absorption variability.

quotes.quote: RE J2248-511 is one of only 14 non-blazar AGN detected in the far ultraviolet by the ROSAT Wide Field Camera implying a large ultrasoft X-ray flux. This soft X-ray excess is strongly variable on year timescales, a common property of Narrow Line Seyfert 1s, yet its optical linewidths classify this source as a broad-lined Seyfert 1. We use four nearly simultaneous optical--X-ray SEDs spanning 7 years to study the spectral shape and long term variability of RE J2248-511. Here we show that the continuum SED for the brightest epoch dataset is consistent with the mean SED of a standard quasar, and matches well to that from an XMM-SDSS sample of AGN with M/M_ 10^8 and L/L_ Edd 0.2 .

RE J2248-511 is a local ( z 0.1 ), EUV-selected Seyfert galaxy. It is classified as a broad-line Seyfert 1 (BLS1) due to its Balmer broad-line widths of FWHM 3600 km s ^ 1 . For an estimated black hole mass of 10 ^ 8 M _ , this source is highly variable in both optical and soft X-rays emp95,aad .

gmw_00005577.name: J0101 + 422
gmw_00005577.overview: J0101 + 422 is a Seyfert galaxy that hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2306.16683]. It exhibits a measured hydrogen-beta reverberation time lag of approximately 117 days [arXiv:2306.16683].

sources.summary: The paper measures a 117-day H reverberation lag for J0101 + 422, finds it consistent with other lag estimators, and rates the measurement A.

quotes.quote: For J0101 + 422, we measured 117 17 18 days of the time lag. This is consistent with its and lags. We assess the lag of this object as rating A.

gmw_00005578.name: PG 0947 + 396
gmw_00005578.overview: PG 0947 + 396 is classified as an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2306.16683]. It is also identified as a Seyfert galaxy, for which a 71-day time lag has been measured [arXiv:2306.16683].

sources.summary: The paper measures a 71-day H lag for PG 0947 + 396, notes bimodality in other lag estimates, and assigns the object a B-quality lag.

quotes.quote: For PG 0947 + 396, we measured 71 16 34 days of the time lag. While this is consistent with the and lags, they both showed bimodality in their lag, where preferred the smaller mode and preferred the larger mode. On the other hand, the ICCF lag captured the average between the two lags. We assess the lag of this object as rating B.

gmw_00005579.name: J1217 + 333
gmw_00005579.overview: J1217 + 333 is classified as an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2306.16683], specifically a Seyfert galaxy [arXiv:2306.16683]. It has been the subject of reverberation mapping studies, though its lag measurements are considered unreliable and unconstrained [arXiv:2306.16683].

sources.summary: The paper finds J1217 + 333 has unreliable lag measurements, with unconstrained and multi-modal results, and excludes it from further analysis.

quotes.quote: For J1217 + 333, we assess that our lag measurements are not reliable. is unconstrained, and the lag is consistent with 0 within its 68 confidence interval. While we measured 199 137 132 days from the ICCF, this is likely to be the average of the window size we used to find the lag.

Supporting this, the ICCF and both show multiple modes in the given window. Moreover, it is far larger than what is measured using samp_hb_inprep . We assess the lag of this object as rating C and exclude it from further analysis.

gmw_00005580.name: VIII Zw 218
gmw_00005580.overview: VIII Zw 218 is classified as an active galactic nucleus and a Seyfert galaxy [arXiv:2306.16683]. It exhibits a clean cross-correlation function peak with a measured H-beta reverberation time lag of 140 days [arXiv:2306.16683].

sources.summary: The paper reports a clean CCF peak for VIII Zw 218, measures a 140-day H reverberation lag, and rates the measurement A.

quotes.quote: VIII Zw 218 showed a single, clean peak in CCFs, and we measured 140 25 25 days of the time lag. This value is consistent with and lags. We assess the lag of this object as rating A.

gmw_00005581.name: KK 203
gmw_00005581.overview: KK 203 is a dwarf spheroidal galaxy that hosts an extended, 400-pc wide, heart-shaped H-alpha emission ring [arXiv:2011.04990]. This emission is not powered by recent star formation, but is instead potentially linked to a 40-million-year-old supernova remnant [arXiv:2011.04990].

sources.summary: The paper derives properties for KK 203 and reports a 400-pc heart-shaped H emission ring whose source is unclear and probably not powered by recent star formation.

quotes.quote: Surprisingly, in the dwarf KK 203 we find an extended H ring. Careful examination of Hubble Space Telescope photometry reveals a very low level of star formation at ages between 30-300 Myr. The H emission is most likely linked to a 40 Myr old supernova remnant, although other possibilities for its origin cannot be entirely ruled out.

For one of our dSphs -- KK 203 -- we found a surprising H emission, which forms a 400-pc wide, heart-shaped ring. The H emission is not powered by recent star-formation. Its origin remains unclear but several possibilities are discussed, including a central supermassive black hole, AGB and post-AGB stars, a 40 M

gmw_00005582.name: KK 198
gmw_00005582.overview: KK 198 is a background, face-on spiral galaxy located at a distance of approximately 50 Mpc [arXiv:2011.04990]. It is a star-forming galaxy that exhibits a redshift of 0.0128 and displays strong Balmer and forbidden emission lines [arXiv:2011.04990].

sources.summary: The paper reclassifies KK 198 as a background star-forming face-on spiral at about 50 Mpc, with strong Balmer and forbidden emission lines and redshift 0.0128.

quotes.quote: Our MUSE data of KK 198 and dw1315-45 revealed that these two galaxies are not dwarf galaxies associated with the Centaurus group but rather are background star forming galaxies. Both KK 198 and dw1315-45 show strong Balmer emission lines (H and H ) as well as [ NII ] , [ SII ] , and [ OIII ] lines. KK 198 has a redshift of 0.0128, dw1315-45 of 0.0100, which puts them at a distance of 50 Mpc. The former is a face-on spiral galaxy, as already noted in the optical FORS2 images MuellerTRGB2019 , the latter must be an ultra-diffuse galaxy with an effective radius of 2300 pc. In Fig fig:spectra_all1 and Fig fig:spectra_all2 we present all the integrated spectra of our targets. The best fit provided by pPXF includes the stellar absorption and emission lines. Two targets (KK 198 and dw1315-45) are clearly background star-forming galaxies with strong emission lines.

gmw_00005583.name: dw1315-45
gmw_00005583.overview: dw1315-45 is a background star-forming ultra-diffuse galaxy located at a distance of approximately 50 Mpc [arXiv:2011.04990]. It features an effective radius of 2300 pc and exhibits strong Balmer, [NII], [SII], and [OIII] emission lines [arXiv:2011.04990].

sources.summary: The paper reclassifies dw1315-45 as a background star-forming ultra-diffuse galaxy at about 50 Mpc, with strong emission lines and an effective radius of 2300 pc.

quotes.quote: Our MUSE data of KK 198 and dw1315-45 revealed that these two galaxies are not dwarf galaxies associated with the Centaurus group but rather are background star forming galaxies. Both KK 198 and dw1315-45 show strong Balmer emission lines (H and H ) as well as [ NII ] , [ SII ] , and [ OIII ] lines. KK 198 has a redshift of 0.0128, dw1315-45 of 0.0100, which puts them at a distance of 50 Mpc. The former is a face-on spiral galaxy, as already noted in the optical FORS2 images MuellerTRGB2019 , the latter must be an ultra-diffuse galaxy with an effective radius of 2300 pc. In Fig fig:spectra_all1 and Fig fig:spectra_all2 we present all the integrated spectra of our targets. The best fit provided by pPXF includes the stellar absorption and emission lines. Two targets (KK 198 and dw1315-45) are clearly background star-forming galaxies with strong emission lines.

gmw_00005584.name: PKS 0040-06
gmw_00005584.overview: PKS 0040-06 is a 17th magnitude optical galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.1243 [arXiv:1810.13142]. It is identified as the northern hotspot of a wide double radio source with a projected linear size exceeding 800 kpc, and its spectrum displays narrow optical emission lines [arXiv:1810.13142].

sources.summary: The paper identifies PKS 0040-06 as the northern hotspot of a wide double radio source, links it to a 17th magnitude optical galaxy, and reports narrow emission lines and large projected size.

quotes.quote: GLEAM J004246-061325 (PKS 0040-06) This GLEAM source corresponds to the northern hotspot of a wide double with a total angular extent of the radio source of at least 6 arcmin.The radio position listed by for this source (J2000: 00 42 45.77 -06 12 41.3) also corresponds to the northern hotspot rather than the whole source. The correct optical identification (with a 17th magnitude galaxy) was first made by . The position listed in columns 9 and 10 of Table tab:data2 is that of the optical galaxy, rather than the FIRST radio centroid. The SDSS spectrum shows narrow optical emission lines superimposed on a stellar continuum.
At the SDSS redshift of z=0.1243, the projected linear size of the source is over 800 kpc.

gmw_00005585.name: PKS 0046-300
gmw_00005585.overview: PKS 0046-300 is a radio galaxy with a tentatively identified host object [arXiv:1810.13142]. This faint host is visible in the WISE W1 band but remains undetected in the W2, W3, and W4 bands [arXiv:1810.13142].

sources.summary: The paper tentatively identifies a faint WISE W1 object offset from the NVSS radio position as the host of PKS 0046-300 and reports its W1 magnitude and non-detections.

quotes.quote: GLEAM J004839-2947189 (PKS 0046-300) A faint uncatalogued source is visible in the WISE W1 image, at an offset of 2.5 arcsec from the NVSS radio position. One of us (THJ) measured a magnitude of W1 = 19.4 0.9 mag. for this object, which we tentatively identify as the host of the radio source. This WISE source was not detected in the W2-W4 bands.

gmw_00005586.name: PKS 0048-12
gmw_00005586.overview: PKS 0048-12 is a radio source likely hosted by a bright WISE source with mid-infrared colors typical of a quasar [arXiv:1810.13142]. The object is detected as an X-ray source, and its radio morphology is consistent with a hotspot of a radio-loud quasar [arXiv:1810.13142].

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 0048-12 as likely hosted by a bright WISE source with QSO-like mid-infrared colors, X-ray detection, and radio morphology consistent with a radio-loud QSO hotspot.

quotes.quote: GLEAM J005026-120115 (PKS 0048-12) A bright WISE source located 7 arcsec from the radio centroid has mid-IR colours typical of QSO, and is also detected as a ROSAT X-ray source. Based on the radio morphology seen in the higher-resolution AT20G image, where the radio contours are extended in the direction of the optical object (see Figure fig:pks0048-12 ), it appears probable that the strongly-scintillating low-frequency source is one hotspot of a radio-loud QSO. We therefore identify this WISE source as the likely host of the radio source PKS 0048-12.

gmw_00005587.name: MRC 0048-107
gmw_00005587.overview: MRC 0048-107 is a radio galaxy associated with a mid-infrared source [arXiv:1810.13142]. This host galaxy is detected in the WISE W1, W2, and W3 bands, but it remains undetected in the W4 band [arXiv:1810.13142].

sources.summary: The paper identifies the closer of two WISE mid-infrared sources near the FIRST position as the radio-source host of MRC 0048-107 and reports WISE photometry.

quotes.quote: GLEAM J005039-102734 (MRC 0048-107) A visual inspection of the WISE W1 image shows two mid-IR sources close to the FIRST radio position. The closest object listed in the WISE catalogue (WISE J005038.95-102739.4 with W1 = 15.78 mag) is 3.0 arcsec away, but a second uncatalogued object is closer to the FIRST radio centroid. Based on measurements of the WISE made by one of us (THJ), we adopt WISE values of W1 = 15.67 0.05 , W2 = 15.21 0.14 and W3 = 10.2 0.95 mag. \ for the radio-source host (which is not detected in the W4 band).

gmw_00005588.name: PKS 0119-04
gmw_00005588.overview: PKS 0119-04 is a radio-loud quasar [arXiv:1810.13142] with an adopted redshift of z = 1.925 [arXiv:1810.13142]. The quasar is associated with an absorbing structure of at least 11 velocity components, which likely represent metal-poor gas infalling from the intergalactic medium [arXiv:1809.05433].

sources.summary: Q0119 046 is studied as a quasar whose associated absorbers form an 11-component complex; the paper favors infalling, metal-poor gas at about 5.7 kpc from the quasar.

quotes.quote: The AALs in Q0119 046 identify a complex absorbing structure with at least 11 distinct velocity components. The velocity shifts of these components, ranging from 0 to roughly +1150 \ (relative to the \ emission line), are indicative of infall towards the quasar with estimated uncertainties of 200 . 2) The electron density implied by the / \ line ratios in component 2, n_e 2500 \ (Section 4.1), indicates that this absorber resides at a distance 5.7 kpc from the quasar (Section 4.4).

However, another possibility is that the AAL absorbers in Q0119 046 represent infalling metal-poor gas from the intergalactic medium (IGM). The measured line velocities favor this infall interpretation. Components 1 and 2 have velocity shifts consistent with zero, while the other systems span a range of positive velocities from 240 to 1150 \ ( tab:Q0119_1 and Section 2.1).

sources.summary: The paper discusses PKS 0119-04 as a radio-loud QSO with an SDSS spectrum, rejects the listed SDSS redshift as inconsistent, and adopts z=1.925.

quotes.quote: GLEAM J012227-042123 (PKS 0119-04) An SDSS spectrum is available for this radio-loud QSO (SDSS J012227.89-042127.1), with the SDSS redshift listed as z = 2.7838. This is inconsistent with the value of z = 1.925 published by and is also inconsistent with the position of several of the emission lines seen in the SDSS spectrum. The listed SDSS redshift appears to be incorrect, and we therefore adopt the redshift of z = 1.925 in this paper.

gmw_00005589.name: NGC 6658
gmw_00005589.overview: NGC 6658 is a galaxy that exhibits a clear large-scale stellar rotation pattern [arXiv:2201.07838]. Its emission line properties remain uncertain, as some analyses detect no optical emission lines or radio emission [arXiv:2201.07838, arXiv:2208.14115], while other spectral synthesis methods resolve weak nuclear emission lines [arXiv:2208.14115].

sources.summary: NGC 6658 shows clear large-scale stellar rotation but no detected optical emission lines and no detected GMRT radio emission in the observations.

quotes.quote: The stellar velocity fields reveal that most of the sources in our sample (10/18 sources, e.g., NGC 940, NGC 924, NGC 1453, NGC 4169, NGC 978, NGC 584, NGC 1587, NGC 4008, NGC 7619 and NGC 6658) show a clear large-scale rotation pattern in their stellar light.
In NGC 6658, optical-emission lines were not detected.
NGC 6658, the source without optical emission lines, also lacks radio emission at any radio frequencies observed with the GMRT telescope.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 6658 as uncertain: one spectral-synthesis approach detects weak nuclear emission lines, while another finds none and integrated spectra lack clear emission-line peaks.

quotes.quote: We note that using the spectral synthesis code FADO GomesPapaderos2017 and the stellar spectral library, we resolved weak [ O iii ] 5007, H , H and [ N ii ] 6584 emission in the nuclear region of NGC 6658. However, using pPXF Cappellari2017 and the Indo-U.S. Coud e Feed stellar library Valdes2004 find no emission lines in the spectrum of this galaxy using the same MUSE dataset. The results for this galaxy are therefore uncertain especially given that no clear emission line peaks are observed in the integrated spectrum.

gmw_00005590.name: host galaxy of GRB 991208
gmw_00005590.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 991208 is located at a redshift of z = 0.7063 [arXiv:astro-ph/0102177]. It is a faint galaxy with an absolute magnitude of M_B = -18.2 and exhibits a substantial star-formation rate [arXiv:astro-ph/0102177].

sources.summary: The paper identifies the GRB 991208 host at z = 0.7063, measures its late-time BVRI photometry, derives M_B = 18.2, and estimates a substantial star-formation rate.

quotes.quote: Evidence for a brigh host galaxy came from the BTA/MPSF 4500-s spectrum of the GRB 991208 optical transient taken on 14 Dec (see Fig. 5). We found four emission lines at = 6350 , 8300 , 8550 , and 8470 , with the most likely identifications of these emission lines being: [ OII ] 3727 , H _ 4861 , [ OIII ] 4959 , 5007 \ at a redshift of z = 0.7063 0.0017 (Dodonov et al. 1999b), a value confirmed by other measurements later on (Djorgovski et al. 1999).

For the galaxy, which is present in the late images (March-April 2000), the astrometric solution also obtained using the same 16 USNO-A stars was _ 2000 =16^h 33^m 53.^s53; _ 2000 =+46^ 27^ ' 21.0^ '' ( 0.2^ '' ), which is consistent with the OA position. See also Fruchter et al. (2000). Our broad-band measurements of B = 25.19 0.17, V = 24.55 0.16, R = 24.27 0.15 on 31.9 Mar and I = 23.3 0.2 on 4.2 Apr, once derredened by the Galactic extinction imply a spectral distribution F_ ^ with = 1.45 0.33 ( ^ 2 per degree of freedom, ^ 2 / dof = 1.20). See Sokolov et al. (2000) for further details. The unobscured flux density at 7510 , the redshifted effective wavelength of the B-band, is 0.65 Jy, corresponding to an absolute magnitude of M _ B = 18.2, well below the knee of the galaxy luminosity function, M _ B ^ * 20.6 (Schechter 1976).

From the H _ flux which is (3.84 0.33) x 10 ^ 16 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 , this corresponds to (18.2 0.6) M _ yr ^ 1 (Pettini et al. 1998). From the [ O II ] 3727 \ flux (Kennicutt 1998), which is (1.79 0.22) x 10 ^ 16 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 we get (4.8 0.2) M _ yr ^ 1 . The mean value, (11.5 7.1) M _ yr ^ 1 , is much larger than the present-day rate in our Galaxy.

gmw_00005591.name: COSMOS-20646
gmw_00005591.overview: COSMOS-20646 is a high-redshift galaxy candidate characterized by red colors and a high inferred stellar mass [arXiv:2111.05351]. However, its proximity to bright neighboring sources introduces photometric uncertainty, leaving both its stellar mass measurement and photometric redshift highly uncertain [arXiv:2111.05351].

sources.summary: The paper treats COSMOS-20646 as a bright z > 8.5 candidate with red colors and high inferred mass, but flags low-resolution deblending and photometric-redshift uncertainty.

quotes.quote: However, we acknowledge that two of our sources appear fairly red, and also have the highest derived stellar masses (UDS-18697 and COSMOS-20646). As discussed further in F21, the proximity to bright neighbors leaves the IRAC photometry for these sources less reliable, thus it is possible that residual light from the neighbors is contributing to the high stellar mass measurement. If we exclude these two galaxies from this median measurement, we find = -1.87^ +0.35 _ 0.43 .

In summary, we find consistent photometric redshift estimates for all galaxies except COSMOS-20646 ( z_ phot =2.63_ 0.16 ^ +0.16 ) and EGS-20381 ( z_ phot =6.79_ 0.13 ^ +0.57 ). This is consistent with the EAZY based results with this photometry from F21 for these two objects.

Both COSMOS-20646 and UDS-18697 have stellar masses of M_ 10^ 11 M_ and therefore lie solidly within the less likely CDM region in Figure fig:overly_massive . Figure appfig:M_SFR_galfit shows that the stellar mass of COSMOS-20646 reduces by roughly a factor of 3 when adopting the GALFIT photometry, making it consistent with the CDM boundary when considering the uncertainty. On the other hand, UDS-18697 seems to get even more massive (and star forming) when adopting the GALFIT photometry. In summary, these differences depending on which photometric method is used highlight a systematic uncertainty when making use of deblended photometry from low-resolution imaging, something which will be alleviated soon with JWST . We adopt throughout this work the TPHOT based IRAC photometry since it does overall a better job of removing the bright neighboring sources (F21). The object COSMOS-20646 has the largest uncertainty regarding which method is more accurate, we therefore caution the reader that this nature of this galaxy is still somewhat uncertain.

gmw_00005592.name: UDS-18697
gmw_00005592.overview: UDS-18697 is a massive galaxy candidate at a redshift greater than 8.5 [arXiv:2111.05351]. It exhibits a declining star formation history with a low measured specific star formation rate, though its infrared photometry is subject to large deblending uncertainties due to nearby bright neighbors [arXiv:2111.05351].

sources.summary: The paper presents UDS-18697 as a massive, red z > 8.5 candidate with low specific star formation, a declining SFH, and substantial IRAC deblending uncertainty.

quotes.quote: However, we acknowledge that two of our sources appear fairly red, and also have the highest derived stellar masses (UDS-18697 and COSMOS-20646). As discussed further in F21, the proximity to bright neighbors leaves the IRAC photometry for these sources less reliable, thus it is possible that residual light from the neighbors is contributing to the high stellar mass measurement. If we exclude these two galaxies from this median measurement, we find = -1.87^ +0.35 _ 0.43 .

An exception is UDS-18697 for which we measure a low sSFR of 0.4_ 0.3 ^ +0.6 Gyr ^ 1 - an interesting galaxy that seems to have gone through an intense episode of star formation early on and now is showing a declining SFH and a high stellar mass (Section subsec:SFH ). An important caveat to this object is that the IRAC deblending uncertainty is large (Appendix appsec:galfit_phot ). Despite this uncertainty and even if this object does not have such a low sSFR, it viscerally shows that your prior does not exclude such ``old'' solutions if the data warrants it.

Both COSMOS-20646 and UDS-18697 have stellar masses of M_ 10^ 11 M_ and therefore lie solidly within the less likely CDM region in Figure fig:overly_massive . Figure appfig:M_SFR_galfit shows that the stellar mass of COSMOS-20646 reduces by roughly a factor of 3 when adopting the GALFIT photometry, making it consistent with the CDM boundary when considering the uncertainty. On the other hand, UDS-18697 seems to get even more massive (and star forming) when adopting the GALFIT photometry.

gmw_00005593.name: Swift J0136.5+3906
gmw_00005593.overview: Swift J0136.5+3906 is a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2007.12609] and a blazar with a featureless, blue optical spectrum [arXiv:2007.12609, arXiv:1701.04305]. The source has been detected at TeV energies [arXiv:2007.12609] and exhibits electric vector position angle rotation [arXiv:1608.08440].

sources.summary: The paper identifies Swift J0136.5+3906 as a BL Lac with catalog blazar associations, TeV detection, and a featureless blue optical spectrum from non-thermal emission.

quotes.quote: Finally, the remaining extragalactic, non-emission line source of our sample (Swift J0136.5+3906) was classified as a BL Lac in Swift70, but as a `beamed AGN' in Swift105, and no optical spectrum was available for it in the literature before our observations. Piranomonte07 opt for the former classification as well. It is, moreover, listed as a ray source in the Third Fermi catalogue Acero15 , as well as a BL Lac in the 5th edition of the Roma-BZCAT catalogue Massaro15 . It was also detected in the TeV band WakelyHoran08 . Its optical spectrum indeed presents a featureless, blue shape typical of blazars of the BL Lac type (see Fig. 20), with neither intrinsic emission nor absorption lines are present in its optical spectrum: this is a key characteristic of the spectra of AGN with collimated jets oriented along the observer's line of sight.

sources.summary: The paper lists BZB J0136+3905 among its TeV BL Lac candidates, notes previous candidate selection and VERITAS observations, and says its missing redshift may explain the TeV non-detection.

quotes.quote: Three of our sources appear as TeV candidates in the Tavecchio et al. (2010) selection: BZB J0109+1816, BZB J0136+3905 and BZB J1058+5628; the last source also in Costamante Ghisellini (2002). We note that VERITAS observations have been performed for 3 of our TBCs selected within the : BZB J0136+3905, BZB J1058+5628 and BZB J1243+3627 aliu12 .

However, as discussed in Aliu et al. (2012), both BZB J0136+3905 and BZB J1243+3627 do not have a redshift estimate, indicating that their non TeV-detection could be due the absorption of high energy photons by the extragalactic background light franceschini08 . Thus, the non-detection at TeV energies of these three sources does not affect our selection that can only be verified with additional TeV observations.

sources.summary: The paper describes RGB 0136+591 as a VHE BL Lac source in its monitoring sample and reports its optical/radio flux level, variability trends, and correlation behavior.

quotes.quote: RGB 0136+591 : One of the faintest radio sources, while in the optical the average flux density is above the median. In the radio band the modulation index calculation failed due to large uncertainties and faintness of the source. The optical light curve has a significant decreasing trend, while the radio light curve does not show any significant trend. This is in agreement with the results from simulated light curves, where in only 2 cases out of 1000 a significant trend occurs in the radio band.

sources.summary: The paper confirms RGB J0136+391 has a featureless high-SNR optical spectrum and derives a lower redshift limit below an earlier imaging-based bound.

quotes.quote: RGB J0136+391 : The first identification of this source as BLL was proposed by laurent1998 showing a featureless optical spectrum. The same result was found in wei1999, piranomonte2007 and shaw2013a . A lower limit on the redshift of z > 0.40 was set on the basis of high quality i-band images obtained at the Nordic Optical Telescope (NOT) ( nilsson2012 ). We found our high SNR ( 200-480) optical spectrum completely featureless, only allowing to set a lower limit to the redshift of z > 0.27.

sources.summary: The paper reports significant EVPA rotation in RGB J0136+391, notes that combined RoboPol and NOT observations were required, and uses it as a rotation-simulation example.

quotes.quote: We find significant rotations in three of the HSP TeV sources (RGB J0136+391, PG 1553+113, and 1ES 1727+502), and nine rotations in six non-TeV sources (GB6 J1037+5711,GB6 J1542+6129, TXS 1557+565, 87GB 164812.2+524023, RXJ 1809.3+2041, S5 2023+760).
We would not have detected the rotations in RGB J0136+391 and 1ES 1727+502 if we did not have both RoboPol and NOT observations of them.
In Fig. simex , we show examples for two of the sources showing rotations RGB J0136+391 (TeV source) and GB6 J1037+5711 ( non-TeV source).

gmw_00005594.name: Grus II
gmw_00005594.overview: Grus II is an ultra-faint dwarf galaxy orbiting the Milky Way [arXiv:1805.01839, arXiv:2005.10767]. It is located behind the Orphan/Chenab stellar stream, which overlaps with the galaxy in sky position and proper motion [arXiv:1812.08172, arXiv:2308.07394], complicating the membership identification of its stars and variable RR Lyrae populations [arXiv:1909.06308, arXiv:2312.07279].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes Grus II as an ultra-faint dwarf galaxy whose member-star abundances suggest high-mass stellar enrichment, possible top-heavy IMF behavior, and delayed r-process enrichment.

quotes.quote: We present a detailed abundance analysis of the three brightest member stars at the top of the giant branch of the ultra faint dwarf galaxy Grus II. All stars exhibit a higher than expected [ Mg/Ca ] ratio compared to metal-poor stars in other ultra faint dwarf galaxies and in the Milky Way halo. Nucleosynthesis in high mass ( 20 M _ ) core-collapse supernovae has been shown to create this signature. The abundances of this small sample (3) stars suggests the chemical enrichment of Grus II could have occurred through substantial high-mass stellar evolution, and is consistent with the framework of a top-heavy initial mass function.
The time delay between the and process element enrichment of the galaxy favours a neutron star merger as the origin of the process elements in Grus II.

In this paper we analyse the UFD galaxy Grus II (Gru II). Gru II was identified as a candidate MW satellite galaxy in the Dark Energy Survey (DES) at a distance of 53 kpc drlicawagner2015 . Spectroscopic follow-up of the system was presented by simon2020 , who was unable to resolve a velocity dispersion or detect a metallicity spread, indicative of dwarf galaxies, for the system.

sources.summary: Grus 2 is classified as a dwarf galaxy but its outer-profile detection may be compromised by the overlapping Orphan/Chenab stream; the paper discusses possible tidal disruption and contamination.

quotes.quote: Grus 2 Gru2 was first discovered in the Dark Energy Survey (DES; drlica-wagner2015 ). It is a moderately distant system ( 55 kpc; martinez-vazquez2019 ), whose luminosity is consistent to other UFDs ( L_ V 3150 L _ ). Though a resolved velocity dispersion has not yet been determined for this system, it is classified as a dwarf galaxy, mainly based on its absolute magnitude ( M_ V 3.9 mag), metallicity ( [ Fe/H ] = 2.51; simon2020 ) and relatively large physical size ( r_ h = 5.9 = 0.1 ^ , or 95 pc). Gru2 is located behind a more metal-rich and diffuse substructure, known as the Orphan/Chenab (OC) stream ( grillmair_dionatos2006 ; belokurov2007 ; shipp2018 ). koposov2019 first reported on these incident structures, finding the proper motions were similar (particularly at stream longitude position of Gru2, or _ 1 = 66.1 ^ ) and suggesting that these structures may be linked. However, there is strong evidence to suggest that Gru2 is not the stream's progenitor, as it differs in heliocentric distances by 10 kpc ( martinez-vazquez2019 ) and it's heliocentric RV is offset by 90 km s ^ 1 (and Gru2's Galactocentric RV v _ GSR, Gru2 131 km s ^ 1 , while v _ GSR, OC 200 km s ^ 1 ; erkal2019 ; koposov2019 ).

According to the central density to MW pericenter density ratio calculated in pace2022 , Gru2 is potentially tidally disrupting. The spectroscopic members traced in simon2020 extend out to 2 r_ h , and so it is possible that some radially distant stars are evidence of this disruption. However, given the OC stream's width and overlap with these stars, it is certainly possible that they are indeed members of the OC stream and not Gru2.

sources.summary: The paper reviews Grus II morphology and spectroscopy, explains why HST data were insufficient for new structural fitting, and reports evidence that Gru II is likely a dwarf galaxy.

quotes.quote: Grus II Gru II was the ultra-faint candidate with the highest sigma detection discovered in the second year of DES data, and had no significant ellipticity or asphericity drlica2015 . As such, there would not be a corresponding a_h ; reported r_h=6 . Using the DES Y3A2 catalog, DES Y3A2 refers to an internal DES release. The public DES DR1 was based on this abbott2018 .

We did not fit structural parameters for Gru II, as past measurements of its size have found a_h 6 drlica2015,moskowitz2020,simon2020 , and our single ACS pointing only extends to 0.25a_h , which is insufficient for a robust morphology measurement. The first spectroscopic observation of Gru II was performed by , who identified 21 member stars with Magellan/IMACS spectroscopy.

Additionally, did not detect a metallicity spread, however based on the low metallicity ( [ Fe/H ] = 2.51 0.11 dex) and large size, they concluded Gru II is likely a dwarf galaxy. Using DOLPHOT photometry of HST data, did not find significant mass segregation among the member stars, leading them to also conclude that Gru II is likely a dwarf galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper estimates Grus II proper motion without public spectroscopy, using horizontal-branch candidates, and reports agreement with previous work in both proper motion and expected members.

quotes.quote: We present mean absolute proper motion measurements for seven ultra-faint dwarf galaxies orbiting the Milky Way, namely Bo o tes III, Carina II, Grus II, Reticulum II, Sagittarius II, Segue 2 and Tucana IV. For four of these dwarfs our proper motion estimate is the first ever provided. The adopted astrometric data come from the second data release of the Gaia mission. We determine the mean proper motion for each galaxy starting from an initial guess of likely members, based either on radial velocity measurements or using stars on the Horizontal Branch identified in the Gaia ( G_ BP G_ RP , G) colour-magnitude diagram in the field of view towards the UFD.

The UFDs Bo o tes III, Grus II, Sagittarius II and Tucana IV do not have publicly available spectroscopic information. As mentioned in Sect. method we therefore use Horizontal Branch (HB) candidates located within 15 from the centre of each UFD (from mc12 ) to obtain an initial guess on the mean astrometric parameters. HB stars have been chosen because they are easy to detect in the CMD, being bluer than the typical sequences described by the majority of stars located in the respective fields of view. This is clearly demonstrated in Fig. sel_hb , where the CMD regions adopted to select HB stars are marked with a cyan box. To demonstrate the effectiveness of the selection procedure described in Sect. method , Fig. sel_hb shows a snapshot of the last iteration step from the point of view of the CMD. The red filled circles are stars that survived the 2.5 clipping astrometric selection and items 4 ii ) and 4 iii ) of the procedure. However, only those located inside the blue delimited box will be used for the final mean proper motion estimate. In general, the selected members are located on the typical evolutionary sequences characteristic of the stellar populations seen in dwarf galaxies. These sequences are quite well-defined despite the meagre numbers ( 40 50 stars). The fact that the poorly populated red giant branches are also quite faint (the brightest HB is at G 19 ) contributes in spreading them out in G_ BP G_ RP colour. The colour uncertainty at these magnitudes is 0.1 , whereas the measured spread amounts to 0.15 . Therefore we cannot exclude the presence of multiple, possibly chemically heterogeneous sub-populations (in fact UFDs typically show spread in their metallicity distribution, see tolstoy09 ). The distribution in the VPD of the final members of the four UFD galaxies is shown in Fig. vpd_hb with red filled circles. Like for the dwarfs analysed in Sec. wspec , they describe a reasonably well concentrated clump of stars, clearly separated from the bulk of the field population (black dots). It is interesting to note that in the case of Bo o tes III, a third concentration of stars clearly stands out at ( _ , _ ) (-5, -1) : it is made up of stars belonging to the Galactic globular cluster NGC 5466, located very close to the galaxy also on the sky. Their quite different distances from the Sun (with the UFD 30 kpc farther away, ferraro99 ) and kinematics, with Bo o tes III having ( _ , _ ) = ( 1.21 0.13 , 0.92 0.17 ) , corresponding to a difference in velocity of about 100 km s ^ 1 , implies that the two systems are not associated to each other. The spreads in the distributions of members in the VPDs of the four UFDs are consistent with the typical proper motion uncertainties of their stars. This is true even for the case of Sagittarius II, whose distribution seems significantly more extended and asymmetric than for the other systems. This is because for its stars the errors on _ are a factor of 1.5 larger than those on _ . The cyan filled circles in Fig. vpd_hb mark the initial HB candidates, most of them being located well within the region of the final members. Finally, Fig. sky_hb shows the distribution of the selected members on the sky. As in Sect. wspec , only stars represented as black dots and within the red circle have been considered for the final mean proper motion estimate. All the four galaxies show a peak in the concentration of their members within the projected distance cut given by the radius of the red circle. Interestingly however, for some of these (namely Bo o tes III and Tucana IV), the peak of the density distribution seems somewhat offset with respect to the nominal centre (given by the centre of the red circle, and taken from grillmair09 and dw15 , respectively). Moreover all the systems, with maybe the exception of Sagittarius II (which has been proposed to be a globular cluster rather than a UFD, see mp18 ), appear asymmetric and possibly depict an elongated shape. It is beyond the aim of this paper to make a quantitative analysis on these aspects, yet our findings already highlight how powerful can be to better assess the density profiles and centres of systems like the UFDs, which suffer from large amounts of contamination by field stars. The location of all the initially selected candidates HB stars are marked in Fig. sky_hb with cyan empty circles. Few of these HB stars have been lost during the procedure, but this was to be expected given that the initial selection is astrometrically blind. The fact that the actual members all lie well within the adopted projected distance cut from the nominal centre is a further check on the robustness of our procedure. Sagittarius II might show hints of the presence of few HB members outside this distance cut. Increasing such a radius does not improve the final proper motion estimate, and given the high degree of contamination in that field we cannot conclude they are false members. In this paper we have estimated the mean absolute proper motion for seven UFD satellites of the Milky Way, all located within 70 kpc from us and with an integrated V-band magnitude brighter than V=15. This has ensured that a sufficiently large number of members are above magnitude limit of G 21 . For four of these seven dwarfs, ours is the first proper motion estimate ever provided. To determine likely member stars from which to compute the mean proper motion of each system we developed a new procedure which proved to be less prone to systematic uncertainties than using only radial velocity members in case of very low number statistics. This procedure starts from an initial guess on the mean proper motion and parallax coming from an initial sample of tentative members, and then refines their membership and the mean astrometric parameters by coupling photometric (CMD selection) and astrometric information together. The initial sample of candidate members is made up of spectroscopic members, if radial velocity measurements are publicly available, or HB stars, otherwise. For all the seven UFDs studied here, our procedure works well. We obtain well-defined evolutionary sequences in the CMDs and a clear concentration of stars in the VPDs. Interestingly, the distribution on the sky of the selected members often highlights elongated or asymmetric structures, suggestive of tidal perturbations or complex dynamical histories, and a couple of galaxies might be offset with respect to their previously determined density centres. Future investigations based on photometric and astrometric datasets, possibly in combination with dynamical models, will be used to assess these findings. When comparing our results to those of and for the sub-sample of UFDs with spectroscopic information, we find good agreement for Carina II and Reticulum-II, where small differences can be ascribed to the different sample of members used (ours not being limited to spectroscopic members only) and to the fact that we took into account the full covariance matrix in the determination of the mean proper motions. On the other hand, we found a 1.5 difference with the results of for Segue 2, a system that this author recognised as peculiar due to the wide spread of its stars in the VPD. In this case, the sub-sample of spectroscopic members turns out to be systematically shifted in the VPD. This demonstrates that when limited to small samples systematic effects related to small numbers statistics, might bias the results. In the case of UFDs without public spectroscopic information, we find good agreement for Bo o tes III with the results of , who used proprietary radial velocities to assess the stellar membership and found a mean proper motion which matches ours well within a 1 uncertainty. This is possibly the best confirmation on the validity of our method. Finally, our estimates on Grus II and Tucana IV agree well with those of , both in terms of mean proper motions and of expected number of members.

sources.summary: The paper classifies Grus II as a likely dwarf galaxy, finds four RR Lyrae nearby, but associates only V1 with Grus II while placing two brighter variables in a foreground stream.

quotes.quote: Grus II (M _V -3.9 mag) was discovered in the DES Year 2 data DrlicaWagner2015 . It is the closest of the systems in our SOAR follow-up sample, at D _ 53 kpc ( _0 18.6 mag, DrlicaWagner2015 ). Based on its absolute magnitude and large size ( _h =93 pc), it is classified as a very likely dwarf galaxy (see Figure 4 in DrlicaWagner2015 ).

We found a total of four RRLs in the neighborhood of Grus II; one RRL within 0.5 _h (at 2.52 arcmin from the center) and three more in the outer regions (at 11.32, 16.17, and 21.98 arcmin from the center).

In summary, taking account of the considerations detailed above, out of the four RRLs detected in the field of Grus II, V3 is a very likely Halo RRL, V1 is consistent with being a Grus II member, and from the latter discussion, V2 and V4 seem to be members of the Chenab/Orphan Stream. In order to obtain their distance moduli, we have assumed a [ /Fe ] =+0.2 dex and a metallicity of [ Fe/H ] =--2.0 dex for V1 (Grus II), [ Fe/H ] =--1.5 dex for V2 and V4 (based on the appereance of the HASP RRL), and [ Fe/H ] =--1.65 dex for V3 (mean metallicity of the Galactic Halo, Suntzeff1991 ).

sources.summary: The paper identifies Grus II as most likely a dwarf galaxy because its inferred size is too large for a star cluster and its HST member stars show no significant mass segregation.

quotes.quote: Grus II was discovered by drlicawagneretal2015 in second year DES data. They estimated a half-light radius of 6 arcmin and a distance of 55 kpc to the system. The half-light radius would correspond to a physical size of about 95 pc, making it unlikely that Grus II is a star cluster.

For the stars in our HST field, we find a half-number radius of 96 arcsec, corresponding to a lower limit of 25 pc on the half-light radius, in agreement with the size estimated by drlicawagneretal2015 . We also do not find significant mass segregation among the member stars ( P_ Mseg =8.9 ). Grus II is therefore most likely a dwarf galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper finds many Grus II candidate members and a systemic proper motion consistent with Massari et al., while noting foreground overlap complicates membership.

quotes.quote: Grus II is relative bright and nearby, therefore it has the most potential members among the satellites with new proper motion measurements. It has 11 RGB and 3 BHB members with high probability. For Grus II, Massari2018A&A...620A.155M find ( _ , _ ) = (0.37 0.07, -1.33 0.08) which is consistent with our result at the 1- level. We find the total membership to be p_i = 32 , which is smaller than expected from the stellar population simulations ( N_ expected =39 7 ) whereas Massari2018A&A...620A.155M find 45 members. We suspect our results are driven by the partial overlap in proper motion space of Grus II and the MW foreground.

sources.summary: The paper discusses Gru 2 as an ultra-faint Milky Way satellite nearly coincident with Orphan Stream debris in sky position and proper motion, though offset by about 10 kpc in distance.

quotes.quote: This happens to be the recently discovered MW satellite Gru 2 DrlicaWagner2015 . Gru 2 and other Galactic satellites Figure fig:sats shows the position of Gru 2 (filled red star) in various phase-space projections, namely in stream-aligned coordinates ( , ) (top panel), distance ( , D) (upper middle), proper motion ( , _ ) (lower middle) and ( , _ ) (bottom). Gru 2 is coincident with the OS RR Lyrae in 3 out of 4 dimensions considered: position on the sky and the two components of the proper motion. It appears offset to slightly larger distances by some 10 kpc compared to the stream's debris at the same . Overall, the connection between Gru 2 and the OS seems rather likely; however, line-of-sight velocity and chemical abundances are required to verify the reality and nature of that association.

sources.summary: The paper revisits Grus II RRL membership, finds three additional candidates, and separates likely Grus II stars from Chenab/Orphan stream contaminants by magnitude.

quotes.quote: Grus II and the Chenab/Orphan Stream martinez19 searched for RRL in Grus II, finding 3 of them in the galaxy. However, further analysis made those authors realize that there may be two substructures overlapping in this part of the sky: Grus II and the Chenab/Orphan stream Koposov2019 that passes several kpc in projection in front of Grus II. Here we recovered the 3 known stars, and found 3 additional ones, which are located outside the area searched by martinez19 . As in martinez19 we also found a bimodal distribution in the magnitudes of the RRL (see lower left panel in Fig. fig:GvsBPRP ), with two stars having a magnitude of G=19.1 , while the other 4 are 0.5 mag brighter. The two fainter stars are hence associated with Grus II, while the other 4 are very likely contamination by the Chenab/Orphan stream.

sources.summary: The review presents Grus II as a bona fide process-enhanced ultra-faint dwarf galaxy, with moderate europium enhancement and a transition from process-free to process-enhanced stars.

quotes.quote: Since the discovery of Ret II, two other ultra-faint dwarf galaxies or galaxy candidates have been clearly shown to contain process enhancements: Tucana III Hansen2017 and Grus II Hansen2020 . Unlike Ret II, these two systems have moderate process enhancements [ Eu/Fe ] +0.5 , which can either be explained by dilution into different gas mass reservoirs Safarzadeh2017,Tarumi2020 or variable process yields Ji2019b .
Gru II is clearly a bona fide dwarf galaxy, and like Ret II it shows a transition from metal-poor process-free stars to (relatively) metal-rich rich process-enhanced stars.

gmw_00005595.name: CGG-z5
gmw_00005595.overview: CGG-z5 is a compact group of six candidate member galaxies located at a redshift of approximately 5.2 [arXiv:2212.09372]. The group consists of one massive galaxy and five low-mass satellites that are predicted to merge into a single massive galaxy by redshift 3 [arXiv:2212.09372].

sources.summary: The paper presents CGG-z5 as a compact z~5.2 group whose six members have JWST photometric redshifts and may merge into a future massive galaxy.

quotes.quote: We report the discovery of a compact group of galaxies, CGG-z5, at z 5.2 in the EGS field covered by the JWST/CEERS survey. CGG-z5 was selected as the highest overdensity of galaxies at z>2 in recent JWST public surveys and it consists of six candidate members lying within a projected area of 1.5'' 3'' (10 20 kpc ^2 ). All group members are HST/F435W and HST/F606W dropouts while securely detected in the JWST/NIRCam bands, yielding a narrow range of robust photometric redshifts 5.0<z<5.3 . The most massive galaxy in the group has a stellar mass log (M_ * /M_ ) 9.8 , while the rest are low-mass satellites (log (M_ * /M_ ) 8.4 -9.2).

To assess the nature and evolutionary path of CGG-z5, we searched for similar compact structures in the Eagle simulations and followed their evolution with time. We find that all the identified structures merge into a single galaxy by z=3 and form a massive galaxy (log (M_ * /M_ )> 11) at z 1 . This implies that CGG-z5 could be a ``proto-massive galaxy'' captured during a short-lived phase of massive galaxy formation.

gmw_00005596.name: NGC7753
gmw_00005596.overview: NGC 7753 is a grand-design barred spiral galaxy [arXiv:1404.7037, arXiv:0904.4908] that hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1404.7037]. It is interacting with a smaller companion galaxy, NGC 7752, via a tidal bridge [arXiv:1404.7037] and features a disk rich in neutral hydrogen [arXiv:0904.4908].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 7753 as Arp 86's main SAB galaxy, with AGN-affected nucleus, flattened metallicity gradient, lower specific star formation than NGC 7752, and interaction-driven tidal features.
